> - **Topic:** Armature Reaction - Cross Magnetising and Demagnetising Effect (Computation of Ampere-Turns not Required) - Compensating Winding - Interpoles - Commutation (Concept only) and Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Armature Reaction ŌĆö The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Armature Reaction (AR)** is defined as the **distortion of the main field flux distribution** in a DC machine caused by the **magnetic effect of the armature conductors carrying current** on the main pole flux produced by the field winding.

In a DC machine, there are **two independent sources of MMF (magnetomotive force)**:

1. **Field Winding MMF** ŌåÆ produces the **main useful flux** ($\Phi_m$) along the **direct axis (d-axis)** from N-pole to S-pole.
2. **Armature Winding MMF** ŌåÆ produced by current-carrying armature conductors; this MMF acts along the **quadrature axis (q-axis)** when brushes are placed on the **Geometric Neutral Axis (GNA)**.

The interaction between these two MMFs causes the **net air-gap flux** to become **non-uniform**, weakening the main field in some regions and strengthening it in others. This phenomenon is called **armature reaction**.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Two Magnets on a Table" Picture

Imagine you place a **strong bar magnet (main field)** on a table pointing NorthŌĆōSouth. Now take a **second, weaker magnet (armature field)** and place it perpendicular to the first one, at the centre. The second magnet does **not** simply add a perpendicular field; instead, it **bends, tilts, and distorts** the original field lines of the strong magnet. The result is a **skewed, lopsided magnetic field** that no longer points purely NŌĆōS. This is exactly what happens inside a DC machine when current flows in the armature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms for KTU 2024:**
> - **GNA** = Geometric Neutral Axis ŌåÆ the axis **midway between two adjacent main poles** (a fixed, mechanical axis, 90┬░ electrical from the main pole axis).
> - **MNA** = Magnetic Neutral Axis ŌåÆ the axis **along which the net flux is actually zero** inside the machine. It is the axis **perpendicular to the resultant air-gap flux density vector**.

---

## 1.2 Cross-Magnetising Effect

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-magnetising effect** is the component of armature MMF that is **perpendicular to the main field axis (d-axis)**. It is the **only** component present when brushes are placed **exactly on the GNA**.

**Mechanism:**

- The armature current in conductors under a **North pole** flows in one direction (say, into the page), and under the **South pole** it flows in the opposite direction.
- By **Right-Hand Grip Rule**, the MMF set up by the left-half conductors (under one pole) and the right-half conductors (under the adjacent pole) combine to form a single large **armature MMF vector along the q-axis** (perpendicular to d-axis).
- This armature MMF **distorts** the symmetrical main field distribution. The flux density:
  - **Decreases** at the **trailing pole tips**.
  - **Increases** at the **leading pole tips**.

**Effect:** The MNA is **shifted from GNA** in the direction of rotation (for a generator) or opposite to rotation (for a motor). The shift is by an angle $\theta$ measured in electrical degrees.

```
         Main Field Only              With Armature Reaction
        (Symmetrical)                (Distorted - Cross Mag.)
              N                              N
        ŌåÉŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌåÆ                       ŌĢ▓     ŌĢ▒
        ŌåÉŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌåÆ                         ŌĢ▓   ŌĢ▒
    ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ GNA ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ                    ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ MNA ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ (shifted)
        ŌåÆŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌåÆ                       ŌĢ▒   ŌĢ▓
        ŌåÆŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌåÆ                       ŌĢ▒     ŌĢ▓
              S                              S
```

---

## 1.3 Demagnetising Effect

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Demagnetising effect** is produced when the **brushes are shifted from the GNA by an angle $\theta$** (mechanically) in order to bring them onto the new MNA. This shift introduces a component of armature MMF that acts **directly opposite to the main field MMF**, thereby **weakening (demagnetising) the net useful flux**.

**Mechanism:**

- When brushes are shifted by angle $\theta$ from GNA, the armature MMF vector (which is always perpendicular to the brush axis) also shifts by $\theta$.
- The shifted armature MMF can be **resolved into two components**:
  1. **Cross-magnetising component** = $AT_a \cdot \cos\theta$ ŌåÆ perpendicular to main field.
  2. **Demagnetising component** = $AT_a \cdot \sin\theta$ ŌåÆ **directly opposite** to main field.

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition |
|----------|--------|------------|
| Total Armature AT per pole | $AT_a$ | MMF produced by armature current per pole |
| Brush shift angle (electrical) | $\theta$ | Angle by which brush axis is moved from GNA |
| Demagnetising AT/pole | $AT_d$ | $AT_a \cdot \sin\theta$ |
| Cross-magnetising AT/pole | $AT_c$ | $AT_a \cdot \cos\theta$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 syllabus directive: **"Computation of Ampere-Turns not Required."** Therefore, numerical evaluation of $AT_d$ and $AT_c$ from conductor data is **excluded** from the assessment. However, the **concepts, graphical interpretation, and qualitative consequences** are **fully examinable**.

---

## 1.4 What is Commutation?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commutation** is the process by which the current in a **single armature coil is reversed** (commutated) as the coil passes under a brush during its travel from one short-circuited state to the next.

Mathematically, if the coil current before commutation is $+I_a$, and after commutation is $-I_a$, the **change in current** is $\Delta I = 2I_a$ and this must occur within the **commutation period $T_c$** (the time during which the coil is short-circuited by the brush).

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Relay Race Baton Handover"

Think of commutation as a **baton handover in a relay race**. Two runners run in opposite directions on parallel tracks. At a fixed handover zone, the incoming runner must pass the baton to the outgoing runner in a **fraction of a second**. If done **smoothly**, the race continues without disturbance. If done **roughly or late**, the baton is dropped (this is analogous to **sparking** at the brushes in a DC machine). The "handover zone" is the **brush contact width** $W_b$, and the "time available" is the **commutation time** $T_c$.

---

## 1.5 Interpoles (Commutating Poles)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpoles** are **small auxiliary poles** placed **between the main poles**, physically located in the **inter-polar space (q-axis region)**, and are connected in **series with the armature winding**.

**Purpose:** To **neutralise the reactance voltage** induced in the short-circuited coil during commutation, thereby producing **sparkless commutation**.

- Interpole winding is wound such that its polarity is the **same as the next main pole ahead** (in the direction of rotation for a **generator**).
- For a **motor**, the interpole polarity is **the same as the next main pole behind** (i.e., opposite to that of a generator).

---

## 1.6 Compensating Winding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compensating Winding** is a **distributed winding** embedded in **slots cut into the pole faces** of the main field poles. It is also connected **in series with the armature winding**.

**Purpose:** To **neutralise the cross-magnetising effect of armature reaction** over the entire pole arc (not just at the commutating zone), thereby preventing **flash-over** between brushes at high loads.

| Feature | Compensating Winding | Interpoles |
|---------|---------------------|------------|
| Location | Slots in **pole face** | In **inter-polar space** |
| Function | Neutralises **cross-magnetising** AR | Neutralises **reactance voltage** |
| Coverage | **Entire pole arc** | **Commutating zone only** |
| Polarity | Same as main pole (generator) / Opposite (motor) | Same as next main pole (gen) / Opposite (motor) |

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Memory Aid:**
> - **Interpoles** ŌåÆ fix **commutation** problems ŌåÆ placed **between** main poles.
> - **Compensating Windings** ŌåÆ fix **armature reaction** problems ŌåÆ embedded **in the pole faces**.
> - **Both** are connected in **series with the armature**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Armature Reaction

### Step 1 ŌĆö Establishing the Two MMF Sources

A DC machine has **two windings**, each carrying its own current and producing its own MMF:

- **Field Winding (F):** Carries the field current $I_f$ and produces $AT_f = N_f \cdot I_f$ ampere-turns per pole. Its MMF is directed along the **direct axis (d-axis)**.
- **Armature Winding (A):** Carries the armature current $I_a$ distributed in the slots. Its MMF $AT_a$ is directed along the **quadrature axis (q-axis)** when brushes sit on GNA.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Why the Armature MMF is Quadrature

Consider a 2-pole DC machine. The current direction in a conductor depends on its position under a pole:

- Under **North Pole** ŌåÆ current flows **into the page (ŌŖŚ)**.
- Under **South Pole** ŌåÆ current flows **out of the page (ŌŖÖ)**.

Applying the **right-hand grip rule** to current-carrying conductors under each pole produces an armature MMF vector pointing **horizontally** (left to right or right to left), which is the **q-axis**, exactly 90┬░ electrical from the d-axis.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Vector Addition of MMFs

The **resultant air-gap MMF** at any point is the vector sum:

$$\vec{F_{res}} = \vec{F_f} + \vec{F_a}$$

This vector sum causes:
- **Magnitude change** ŌåÆ mainly due to demagnetising component (when brushes shifted).
- **Direction change** ŌåÆ mainly due to cross-magnetising component (always present).

### Step 4 ŌĆö Effect on Flux Density Distribution

Because the iron in the pole shoe is in **saturation**, the flux density **does not increase proportionally** with the strengthened region. Instead:
- The **increase** at leading pole tips is **small** (saturation-limited).
- The **decrease** at trailing pole tips is **larger** (linear region).

> Net result: **Total useful flux per pole decreases** with armature current, even when brushes are at GNA (this is called the **demagnetising effect of cross-magnetisation due to saturation**).

### Step 5 ŌĆö Shift of MNA from GNA

The MNA is **always perpendicular to the resultant flux vector**. As the resultant flux tilts, the MNA shifts by an angle $\theta$ (electrical) from the GNA.

- **Generator:** MNA shifts in the **direction of rotation**.
- **Motor:** MNA shifts **opposite to direction of rotation**.

---

## 2.2 Demagnetising & Cross-Magnetising AT ŌĆö Conceptual Breakup

When brushes are shifted by angle $\theta$ (mechanical = electrical in a 2-pole machine, but multiplied by pole-pairs in multi-pole machines), the armature MMF vector also tilts by $\theta$ from the q-axis.

**Resolution into two components:**

$$AT_d = AT_a \cdot \sin\theta \quad \text{[acts opposite to } F_f\text{]}$$

$$AT_c = AT_a \cdot \cos\theta \quad \text{[acts perpendicular to } F_f\text{]}$$

**Total armature AT:**

$$AT_a = \sqrt{AT_d^2 + AT_c^2} = AT_a(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta)^{1/2} \quad \text{(identity check)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Per KTU 2024 syllabus:** Numerical **computation of $AT_d$ and $AT_c$ from conductor/Z/I data is NOT required.** Only **conceptual understanding** of how the two components arise from brush shift is examinable.

---

## 2.3 Compensating Winding ŌĆö Design Logic

### Why We Need It

The interpoles can **only neutralise the reactance voltage in the commutating zone** (a narrow region under the interpole). They **cannot** prevent the **cross-magnetising distortion** of the main field under the **entire pole arc**.

At **heavy overloads** or **sudden load changes**, the cross-magnetising effect becomes so severe that:
- The **voltage between adjacent commutator segments** rises sharply.
- An **arc** may form across the commutator surface between brushes of opposite polarity.
- This is called **flash-over**.

A **compensating winding** solves this by carrying a current equal and opposite to the armature current in each slot, embedded in slots in the pole face itself.

### Construction

- Number of compensating conductors per pole = some fraction of total armature conductors per pole.
- Connected in **series with the armature**.
- Distribution similar to the armature winding it is meant to cancel.

### Polarity Rules

| Machine Type | Compensating Winding Polarity at a Pole |
|--------------|----------------------------------------|
| Generator | **Same** as the main pole it sits on |
| Motor | **Opposite** to the main pole it sits on |

---

## 2.4 Interpoles ŌĆö Design Logic

### Why They Are Necessary

During commutation, a coil is **short-circuited by the brush** for a brief moment. The current must change from $+I_a$ to $-I_a$. This rapid change induces a **reactance voltage**:

$$e_r = -L \frac{di}{dt} = -L \cdot \frac{2I_a}{T_c}$$

This reactance voltage opposes the change of current and tends to maintain the current in its old direction, causing **sparking** at the brush.

### How Interpoles Solve This

The interpole produces a **commutating flux** in the commutating zone such that the **rotational EMF (speed EMF) induced in the short-circuited coil exactly cancels the reactance voltage**.

The interpole flux $\Phi_i$ is set by the interpole winding MMF, which is **proportional to $I_a$** (because the interpole winding is in series with the armature). This **automatic proportionality** is the brilliance of the design ŌĆö it works for **all load currents** without external adjustment.

### Number of Interpoles

In practice: **Interpoles = Main Poles** (one interpole per main pole pair inter-space).

---

## 2.5 Commutation ŌĆö Concept Only

### The Three Phases of Commutation (Linear Assumption)

Let $T_c$ be the total commutation time, $i$ be the instantaneous current in the short-circuited coil, and $I_a$ be the steady-state armature current per parallel path.

| Phase | Time Interval | Coil Current Behaviour |
|-------|---------------|------------------------|
| Phase 1 | $0 \leq t \leq T_c$ | $i$ varies from $+I_a$ to $-I_a$ (linear ideal) |
| End state | $t = T_c$ | Sparkless (ideal) commutation achieved |

### The Three Idealised Cases

1. **Linear Commutation (Ideal):** Current changes uniformly from $+I_a$ to $-I_a$ in time $T_c$. No sparking.
2. **Under-commutation:** Current change is **slower** than linear at the end ŌåÆ contact break occurs before current reaches zero ŌåÆ sparking.
3. **Over-commutation:** Current change is **faster** than linear at the end ŌåÆ current reverses too quickly and then must "snap back" ŌåÆ sparking.

### Mathematical Setup for Commutation Numerical Problems

The **commutation time** $T_c$ is given by:

$$T_c = \frac{W_b}{v_c}$$

where:
- $W_b$ = brush contact width (in metres).
- $v_c$ = **commutator surface velocity** = $\pi D_c N / 60$ (in m/s), with $D_c$ = commutator diameter, $N$ = speed in rpm.

The **reactance voltage** (numerical problem context):

$$e_r = L \cdot \frac{2 I_a}{T_c}$$

For good commutation, the interpole must produce a **rotational EMF** $e_c$ equal in magnitude and opposite in sign to $e_r$:

$$e_c = e_r$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The phrase *"Commutation (Concept only)"* in the syllabus means students should **understand the mechanism and the role of interpoles/compensating windings**, but **numerical derivation of reactance voltage is generally limited to straightforward substitution problems** (not full multi-step derivations).

---

## 2.6 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|--------------------|------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Demagnetising AT/pole (concept) | $AT_d = AT_a \sin\theta$ | AT | Acts opposite to $F_f$ |
| 2 | Cross-magnetising AT/pole (concept) | $AT_c = AT_a \cos\theta$ | AT | Acts perpendicular to $F_f$ |
| 3 | Identity check | $AT_a = \sqrt{AT_d^2 + AT_c^2}$ | AT | Pythagorean verification |
| 4 | Commutation time | $T_c = \dfrac{W_b}{v_c}$ | seconds | $W_b$ = brush width, $v_c$ = commutator velocity |
| 5 | Commutator surface velocity | $v_c = \dfrac{\pi D_c N}{60}$ | m/s | $D_c$ in metres, $N$ in rpm |
| 6 | Reactance voltage magnitude | $e_r = L \cdot \dfrac{2 I_a}{T_c}$ | Volts | Average value, ideal linear commutation |
| 7 | Condition for sparkless commutation | $e_c = e_r$ | ŌĆö | $e_c$ = rotational EMF in commutating coil by interpole flux |
| 8 | Interpole flux condition | $\Phi_i \cdot N_c \cdot \dfrac{P}{60} \cdot n = e_r$ | ŌĆö | Conceptual balance condition |
| 9 | Reduction in flux per pole due to AR (qualitative) | $\Phi_{net} = \Phi_m - \Delta\Phi_{AR}$ | Wb | $\Delta\Phi_{AR}$ increases with $I_a$ |
| 10 | Polarity rule (Generator) | Interpole = next main pole ahead | ŌĆö | In direction of rotation |
| 11 | Polarity rule (Motor) | Interpole = next main pole behind | ŌĆö | In direction of rotation |
| 12 | Total poles | $P = P_{main} = P_{interpole}$ | ŌĆö | Number of interpoles = main poles |

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities in the table use **standard SI units**: AT in ampere-turns, $\Phi$ in Weber, $v$ in m/s, $D$ in metres, $N$ in rpm, $e$ in Volts, $L$ in Henry, $T_c$ in seconds.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Relevance of Topic |
|-------------|-------------------|
| **DC Traction Motors** (trains, trams) | Heavy starting currents cause severe AR; interpoles + compensating windings are mandatory. |
| **Large DC Generators** in power stations | Flash-over prevention is critical; compensating windings are standard. |
| **Universal Motors** (drills, mixers) | AC operation of DC motor design ŌåÆ commutation is the main design challenge. |
| **Servo Motors & Robotics** | Smooth commutation ensures low electrical noise and accurate torque control. |
| **Welding Generators** | Drooping V-I characteristic depends on controlled AR (special "differential" series winding). |

---

## 2.8 Concept Map ŌĆö How Everything Connects

```
                  ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
```

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Directive Compliance:** As per syllabus, **computation of armature ampere-turns** (from conductor data, total $AT_a$ from $Z$, $I$, etc.) is **NOT required**. Therefore, the derivations below focus on the **conceptual resolutions**, **commutation time numericals**, and **reactance voltage problems** that are within the prescribed scope.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resolution of Shifted Armature MMF

**Statement to Prove:** When the brush axis is shifted by an electrical angle $\theta$ from the GNA, the armature MMF $AT_a$ (which is always perpendicular to the brush axis) can be resolved into a **cross-magnetising component** $AT_c$ (perpendicular to main field) and a **demagnetising component** $AT_d$ (anti-parallel to main field).

### Step 1 ŌĆö Set Up the Reference Frame

Let the **main field axis (d-axis)** be the horizontal reference (x-axis). The **brush axis** is shifted by angle $\theta$ from the GNA, which is itself perpendicular to the d-axis. So the brush axis is rotated by angle $\theta$ from the q-axis (vertical y-axis).

The **armature MMF vector** $\vec{AT_a}$ is **always perpendicular to the brush axis**. Therefore, the angle between $\vec{AT_a}$ and the **d-axis** is:

$$\alpha = 90┬░ - \theta$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Resolve the MMF Vector

Resolve $\vec{AT_a}$ into:
- A component **along the d-axis** (magnetising/demagnetising direction)
- A component **along the q-axis** (cross-magnetising direction)

**Demagnetising component** (along d-axis, opposite to $F_f$):

$$AT_d = AT_a \cdot \cos(\alpha) = AT_a \cdot \cos(90┬░ - \theta)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(90┬░ - \theta) = \sin\theta$:

$$\boxed{AT_d = AT_a \cdot \sin\theta}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Cross-Magnetising Component

**Cross-magnetising component** (along q-axis):

$$AT_c = AT_a \cdot \sin(\alpha) = AT_a \cdot \sin(90┬░ - \theta)$$

Using the identity $\sin(90┬░ - \theta) = \cos\theta$:

$$\boxed{AT_c = AT_a \cdot \cos\theta}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Verification by Pythagorean Identity

$$AT_a^2 = AT_d^2 + AT_c^2$$

Substituting:

$$(AT_a)^2 = (AT_a \sin\theta)^2 + (AT_a \cos\theta)^2$$

$$(AT_a)^2 = (AT_a)^2 [\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta] = (AT_a)^2 \cdot 1$$

$$\therefore \quad AT_a^2 = AT_a^2 \quad \checkmark$$

The identity is verified, confirming the resolution is **mathematically consistent**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Computation Not Required" is Respected:** We have **derived the relationships conceptually** without invoking the actual numerical value of $AT_a$ (which would need conductor count $Z$, current $I$, and pole data). This satisfies the KTU 2024 directive perfectly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Commutation Time and Reactance Voltage

**Problem Context (Allowed Numerical):** Given a DC machine's commutator dimensions, speed, and brush width, compute the commutation time and the average reactance voltage. *(This is a typical KTU 2024 numerical problem.)*

### Given Data (Standard Form)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| $D_c$ | Commutator diameter | metres (m) |
| $N$ | Armature speed | rpm |
| $W_b$ | Brush contact width | metres (m) |
| $I_a$ | Armature current | Amperes (A) |
| $L$ | Coil inductance | Henry (H) |

### Step 1 ŌĆö Commutator Surface Velocity

The commutator rotates with the armature. The linear velocity at the brush contact surface is:

$$v_c = \frac{\pi D_c N}{60} \quad \left[\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right]$$

**Derivation of $v_c$:**

- The commutator makes $N/60$ revolutions per second.
- Each revolution sweeps an arc length of $\pi D_c$ (the circumference).
- Therefore, the surface speed is:

$$v_c = (\text{revs/sec}) \times (\text{circumference}) = \frac{N}{60} \times \pi D_c$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Commutation Time

A brush of width $W_b$ takes time $T_c$ to traverse one coil's worth of commutator segments:

$$T_c = \frac{W_b}{v_c} = \frac{W_b}{\pi D_c N / 60} = \frac{60 W_b}{\pi D_c N}$$

$$\boxed{T_c = \frac{60 W_b}{\pi D_c N} \quad \text{seconds}}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Rate of Change of Current

Under the **ideal linear commutation assumption**, the current changes uniformly from $+I_a$ to $-I_a$ during $T_c$:

$$\Delta I = I_{final} - I_{initial} = (-I_a) - (+I_a) = -2I_a$$

$$\left|\frac{di}{dt}\right| = \frac{2 I_a}{T_c}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Reactance Voltage

The self-induced EMF in the short-circuited coil opposes the change of current (Lenz's law):

$$e_r = -L \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = -L \cdot \frac{(-2 I_a)}{T_c} = \frac{2 L I_a}{T_c}$$

**Magnitude of reactance voltage:**

$$\boxed{e_r = \frac{2 L I_a}{T_c} \quad \text{Volts}}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Worked Numerical Substitution (Template)

> **Sample problem pattern (for student practice):**
> A DC machine has $D_c = 0.30~\text{m}$, $N = 1200~\text{rpm}$, $W_b = 12 \times 10^{-3}~\text{m}$ (12 mm), $I_a = 200~\text{A}$, and coil inductance $L = 0.20~\text{mH}$.

**Step 5.1 ŌĆö Commutator surface velocity:**

$$v_c = \frac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1200}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 360}{60} = 6\pi \approx 18.85~\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5.2 ŌĆö Commutation time:**

$$T_c = \frac{W_b}{v_c} = \frac{0.012}{18.85} \approx 6.366 \times 10^{-4}~\text{s} = 636.6~\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 5.3 ŌĆö Reactance voltage:**

$$e_r = \frac{2 \times 0.20 \times 10^{-3} \times 200}{6.366 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.0800}{6.366 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 125.66~\text{V}$$

This $e_r$ must be **exactly cancelled** by the rotational EMF $e_c$ produced by the interpole flux for **sparkless commutation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Condition for Sparkless Commutation via Interpole

**Statement:** The interpole must produce a **rotational EMF** $e_c$ in the short-circuited coil that is **equal in magnitude and opposite in polarity** to the reactance voltage $e_r$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Expression for $e_c$ (Rotational EMF)

The coil being commutated moves through the interpole's magnetic field. The EMF induced in a single conductor of the coil is:

$$e_{cond} = B_i \cdot l \cdot v_c$$

where:
- $B_i$ = flux density under the interpole (in Tesla).
- $l$ = effective conductor length (in metres).
- $v_c$ = commutator surface velocity = armature conductor velocity at that radius (m/s).

A coil has $N_c$ turns (usually 1 turn = 2 conductors), so total rotational EMF in the coil:

$$e_c = 2 N_c \cdot B_i \cdot l \cdot v_c$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Equate to Reactance Voltage

For sparkless commutation:

$$e_c = e_r$$

$$2 N_c \cdot B_i \cdot l \cdot v_c = \frac{2 L I_a}{T_c}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for Required Interpole Flux

$$B_i \cdot l = \frac{L I_a}{N_c \cdot T_c \cdot v_c}$$

The **total interpole flux** $\Phi_i$ (assuming the coil side spans the full interpole width $w_i$):

$$\Phi_i = B_i \cdot l \cdot w_i = \frac{L I_a \cdot w_i}{N_c \cdot T_c \cdot v_c}$$

Since $T_c = W_b / v_c$:

$$\Phi_i = \frac{L I_a \cdot w_i}{N_c \cdot (W_b / v_c) \cdot v_c} = \frac{L I_a \cdot w_i}{N_c \cdot W_b}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_i = \frac{L \cdot I_a \cdot w_i}{N_c \cdot W_b} \quad \text{Weber}}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Physical Interpretation

- $\Phi_i \propto I_a$ ŌåÆ Since the interpole winding is in series with the armature, $AT_i = N_i \cdot I_a$ is also proportional to $I_a$. So the flux $\Phi_i$ automatically scales with the load. This is the **self-regulating brilliance** of the interpole design.
- $\Phi_i \propto 1/N_c$ ŌåÆ More turns per coil means lower flux needed.
- $\Phi_i \propto w_i / W_b$ ŌåÆ Geometric ratio of interpole width to brush width.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question on commutation, students are expected to:
> 1. State the **condition for sparkless commutation** ($e_c = e_r$).
> 2. Show the **expression for $T_c$** in terms of $W_b$ and $v_c$.
> 3. Show the **expression for $e_r$** in terms of $L$, $I_a$, and $T_c$.
> 4. Conclude the **qualitative proportionality** $\Phi_i \propto I_a$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparative Study Table

| Feature | Interpoles (Commutating Poles) | Compensating Windings |
|---------|-------------------------------|-----------------------|
| **Location in machine** | In the **inter-polar space** (between main poles) | In **slots cut in the pole face** |
| **Connection** | In **series with armature** | In **series with armature** |
| **MMF Source** | Concentrated winding (pole shoe) | Distributed winding (along pole arc) |
| **Polarity ŌĆö Generator** | Same as **next main pole ahead** (in rotation direction) | Same as **main pole** it is embedded in |
| **Polarity ŌĆö Motor** | Same as **next main pole behind** | Opposite to **main pole** it is embedded in |
| **Effect Neutralised** | **Reactance voltage** in commutating coil | **Cross-magnetising** armature reaction |
| **Coverage** | **Commutating zone only** (narrow) | **Entire pole arc** |
| **Cost** | Lower (simpler construction) | Higher (slot cutting + extra winding) |
| **Used in** | Most medium/high power DC machines | Large DC machines, traction motors, high-speed generators |
| **Effect on commutation** | **Direct** ŌĆö produces counter-EMF | **Indirect** ŌĆö improves commutation by reducing flux distortion |
| **Effect on flash-over** | No direct effect | **Major preventive effect** |

---

> **L** = Leading pole tip (towards which MNA shifts) ŌåÆ flux density **increases** (but saturates).
> **T** = Trailing pole tip (away from MNA shift) ŌåÆ flux density **decreases** (linear region).
> **Net result:** The **total area under the B-curve decreases** ŌåÆ total flux $\Phi$ per pole **decreases** ŌåÆ induced EMF $E_b = k\Phi\omega$ **drops**.

## 3.6 Summary of Allowed Numerical Problem Types (KTU 2024)

| # | Problem Type | Formula Used | Allowed? |
|---|--------------|--------------|----------|
| 1 | Brush shift angle from given $AT_d / AT_a$ ratio | $\sin\theta = AT_d / AT_a$ | Ō£ģ Yes (only as ratio, not $AT$ computed from $Z$, $I$) |
| 2 | Commutation time from $W_b$, $D_c$, $N$ | $T_c = 60 W_b / (\pi D_c N)$ | Ō£ģ Yes |
| 3 | Commutator surface velocity | $v_c = \pi D_c N / 60$ | Ō£ģ Yes |
| 4 | Reactance voltage from $L$, $I_a$, $T_c$ | $e_r = 2 L I_a / T_c$ | Ō£ģ Yes |
| 5 | Interpole flux requirement | $\Phi_i = L I_a w_i / (N_c W_b)$ | Ō£ģ Yes (conceptual) |
| 6 | $AT_d$ numerical from $Z$, $I$, $\theta$ | $AT_d = Z I \theta_m / (360 \cdot \text{coeff})$ | ŌØī Not required (AT computation excluded) |
| 7 | Identifying polarity rules | (Qualitative) | Ō£ģ Yes |
| 8 | $E_b$ drop due to AR | $\Delta E_b = k \omega \Delta\Phi$ (qualitative) | Ō£ģ Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** If the question asks for **$AT_d$ or $AT_c$ using conductor/slot/pole data**, students should **state the formula conceptually** and **explain the method**, but **not** be expected to **substitute large numerical values**. The 2024 syllabus explicitly excludes this. However, if a question asks for **"brush shift angle from a given $AT_d/AT_a$ ratio"**, that **IS** within scope.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture of Armature Reaction Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[Armature MMF ATa Produced along Q-Axis]
    
    C --> D{Brush Position Check}
    
    D -- "Brushes at GNA" --> E[Cross-Magnetising Effect Only]
    D -- "Brushes shifted by angle theta" --> F[Two Component Resolution]
    
    E --> E1[Distortion of Main Field Distribution]
    E1 --> E2[Flux density rises at Leading Pole Tip]
    E1 --> E3[Flux density falls at Trailing Pole Tip]
    E2 --> E4[Net Flux per pole reduces due to Saturation]
    E3 --> E4
    
    F --> F1[Demagnetising Component ATd equals ATa sin theta]
    F --> F2[Cross-Magnetising Component ATc equals ATa cos theta]
    F1 --> F3[Directly opposes Main Field MMF]
    F2 --> F4[Perpendicular to Main Field MMF]
    F3 --> F5[Net useful flux reduces further]
    F4 --> E1
    
    E4 --> G[Reduced Induced EMF and Torque Distortion]
    F5 --> G
    
    G --> H{Remedial Solutions}
    H --> H1[Interpoles to neutralise Reactance Voltage]
    H --> H2[Compensating Winding to neutralise Cross-Magnetising AR]
    
    H1 --> I[Sparkless Commutation Achieved]
    H2 --> J[Flashover Prevention at High Loads]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 ŌĆö Block Diagram of Commutation Process & Solutions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coil carries current plus Ia] --> B[Coil enters commutating zone]
    B --> C[Brush short circuits coil]
    C --> D[Current must reverse to minus Ia]
    D --> E{Sparkless Commutation}
    
    E -- "Ideal Linear Case" --> F[No Spark - Di equals 0]
    
    E -- "Under Commutation" --> G[Di positive at break - SPARK]
    E -- "Over Commutation" --> H[Di negative at break - SPARK]
    
    G --> I[Reactance Voltage er is positive]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Solution Apply Interpole Flux]
    
    J --> K[Interpole produces rotational emf ec]
    K --> L{ec equals er}
    L -- "Yes" --> M[Net emf in coil equals zero]
    M --> N[Sparkless Commutation Achieved]
    L -- "No - needs adjustment" --> O[Increase or decrease Interpole AT]
    O --> K
    
    style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style N fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#f99,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#f99,stroke:#333
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 ŌĆö Vector Resolution of Shifted Armature MMF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Brush axis shifted by angle theta from GNA] --> B[Armature MMF vector ATa is perpendicular to brush axis]
    B --> C[Angle between ATa and D axis is 90 minus theta]
    C --> D[Resolve ATa into two components]
    
    D --> E[Demagnetising Component ATd = ATa sin theta]
    D --> F[Cross-Magnetising Component ATc = ATa cos theta]
    
    E --> E1[Acts Anti Parallel to Main Field MMF]
    F --> F1[Acts Perpendicular to Main Field MMF]
    
    E1 --> G[Net Main Flux is weakened]
    F1 --> H[Main Field Distribution is distorted]
    
    G --> I[Armature Reaction Effect Quantified]
    H --> I
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 ŌĆö Sequence of Operations in DC Machine Commutation (Time Line)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Commutation Timeline of a Single Coil
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    
    section Coil States
    Coil carries +Ia (before commutation)    :a1, 0, 1
    Brush begins contact - Short circuit starts :a2, 1, 2
    Current reversal phase 1 - Linear change :a3, 2, 4
    Current reaches zero crossing           :a4, 4, 5
    Current reversal phase 2 - Linear change :a5, 5, 7
    Brush contact ends - Commutation complete :a6, 7, 8
    Coil carries -Ia (after commutation)   :a7, 8, 9
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- At $t=0$: Coil current is $+I_a$ (no commutation).
- At $t=1$ to $t=8$: Coil is in the **commutating zone** (short-circuited by brush). Total commutation time $T_c = 7$ units in this example.
- At $t=4$ to $t=5$: Current crosses zero ŌĆö this is the **most critical instant** for sparking.
- At $t=8$: Coil current is $-I_a$ ŌĆö commutation complete.

## 4.6 Diagram 6 ŌĆö Hierarchy of Solutions to Armature Reaction & Commutation Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problems caused by Armature Current] --> B[Problem 1 - Cross Magnetising AR]
    A --> C[Problem 2 - Demagnetising AR]
    A --> D[Problem 3 - Reactance Voltage during Commutation]
    
    B --> B1[Solution - Compensating Winding in pole face]
    B1 --> B2[Reduces flashover risk]
    B1 --> B3[Used in large DC machines]
    
    C --> C1[Solution - Reduce field current at high loads]
    C1 --> C2[Or design with larger field margin]
    
    D --> D1[Solution 1 - Interpoles between main poles]
    D --> D2[Solution 2 - Use high resistance brushes]
    D --> D3[Solution 3 - Brush shifting - manually adjusted]
    
    D1 --> D4[Most common - automatic load compensation]
    D2 --> D5[Reduces reactance voltage effect]
    D3 --> D6[Compromises efficiency - rarely used today]
```

## 4.7 Diagram 7 ŌĆö Air-Gap Flux Density Distribution (Qualitative)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph noLoad["With No Load - Symmetrical Distribution"]
        A1[Flux density peak under pole center] --> A2[Symmetric fall off on both sides]
        A2 --> A3[Total area represents full flux]
    end
    
    subgraph withLoad["With Load - Cross Magnetising AR"]
        B1[Higher peak at leading pole tip] --> B2[Lower value at trailing pole tip]
        B2 --> B3[Total area REDUCED - net flux falls]
    end
    
    subgraph withShift["With Load AND Brush Shift - Demagnetising AR"]
        C1[Both peaks reduced] --> C2[MNA further shifted]
        C2 --> C3[Total area REDUCED MORE - even lower net flux]
    end

    withLoad --> withShift
```

## 4.8 Diagram 8 ŌĆö Current vs Time During Commutation (Three Idealised Cases)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Commutation at t equals 0] --> B{Type of Commutation}
    
    B -- "Linear - Ideal" --> C1[Current drops uniformly from plus Ia to zero at half Tc]
    C1 --> C2[Then uniformly to minus Ia at full Tc]
    C2 --> C3[No Spark at brush break]
    
    B -- "Under Commutation" --> D1[Current changes slower than ideal]
    D1 --> D2[At brush break current is still positive]
    D2 --> D3[Spark occurs - contact break before zero crossing]
    
    B -- "Over Commutation" --> E1[Current reverses too fast initially]
    E1 --> E2[Reaches minus value before brush break]
    E2 --> E3[Current then rises back to minus Ia]
    E3 --> E4[Spark occurs - oscillating behaviour]
```
