# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**
**Differentiate between a DC shunt motor and a DC series motor in terms of construction and speed characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **DC Shunt Motor** | **DC Series Motor** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Field connection** | Shunt field winding is connected in *parallel* with the armature | Series field winding is connected in *series* with the armature |
| **Field winding** | Made of *thin wire*, many turns, *high resistance* | Made of *thick wire*, few turns, *low resistance* |
| **Flux** | $\Phi$ is *constant* (since $I_{sh}$ is constant at constant $V$) | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (before saturation) |
| **No-load speed** | Safe, but moderately high | **Dangerously high**, must never run unloaded |
| **Speed regulation** | Good (5ŌĆō15%) | Poor (varies inversely with load) |
| **Applications** | Lathes, fans, blowers | Traction, cranes, hoists |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**
**Why is a DC series motor never started without a mechanical load? Explain with the speed equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The speed of a DC series motor is given by:
$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k_E \Phi}$$

Before magnetic saturation, $\Phi \propto I_a$, so $\Phi = k_f I_a$. Substituting:
$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k_E \cdot k_f \cdot I_a}$$

At no-load, $I_a$ is very small (only the small field current). Hence, $\Phi \to 0$, and the denominator $k_E k_f I_a \to 0$. Mathematically, $N \to \infty$, which is physically impossible ŌĆö the motor will accelerate to destructive speeds due to the absence of opposing torque. Therefore, a series motor must **always be mechanically coupled to a load** before energization, to ensure a finite armature current and a safe operating speed.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice Format

### **Question A: 14 Marks (Choice Option A)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of $0.3\ \Omega$ and a shunt field resistance of $250\ \Omega$. At no-load, the motor draws 5 A from the supply and runs at 1200 rpm. At full-load, the line current is 60 A. Assuming the flux is constant, calculate:
1. The no-load speed and full-load speed
2. The speed regulation
3. The developed torque at full-load
4. The efficiency at full-load, given rotational losses of 600 W.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the construction and operating principle of a DC compound motor. Compare the cumulative and differential compound configurations in terms of torque-speed characteristics and applications.

---

### **Question A ŌĆö Model Solution (a):**

**Given:** $V = 250$ V, $R_a = 0.3\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250\ \Omega$, $N_0 = 1200$ rpm, $I_{L,NL} = 5$ A, $I_{L,FL} = 60$ A, $P_{rot} = 600$ W.

**Step 1 ŌĆö No-load parameters:**

Shunt field current:
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1\ \text{A}$$

No-load armature current:
$$I_{a0} = I_{L,NL} - I_{sh} = 5 - 1 = 4\ \text{A}$$

No-load back-EMF:
$$E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 250 - 4(0.3) = 250 - 1.2 = 248.8\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Machine constant $k_E \Phi$:**

$$k_E \Phi = \frac{E_{b0}}{N_0} = \frac{248.8}{1200} = 0.20733\ \text{V/rpm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Full-load parameters:**

Full-load armature current:
$$I_{a1} = I_{L,FL} - I_{sh} = 60 - 1 = 59\ \text{A}$$

Full-load back-EMF:
$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a = 250 - 59(0.3) = 250 - 17.7 = 232.3\ \text{V}$$

**Full-load speed:**
$$N_1 = \frac{E_{b1}}{k_E \Phi} = \frac{232.3}{0.20733} = 1120.45\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Speed regulation:**

$$\text{Reg} = \frac{N_0 - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\% = \frac{1200 - 1120.45}{1120.45} \times 100\% = 7.09\%$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Developed torque at full-load:**

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi N_1}{60} = \frac{2\pi (1120.45)}{60} = 117.31\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$P_m = E_{b1} \cdot I_{a1} = 232.3 \times 59 = 13705.7\ \text{W}$$

$$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_1} = \frac{13705.7}{117.31} = 116.83\ \text{Nm}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Efficiency at full-load:**

$$P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot} = 13705.7 - 600 = 13105.7\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_{L,FL} = 250 \times 60 = 15000\ \text{W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{13105.7}{15000} \times 100\% = 87.37\%$$

---

### **Question A ŌĆö Model Solution (b):**

**Construction & Principle:**

A DC compound motor has *two* field windings on each pole: a **shunt field** (many turns of thin wire) and a **series field** (few turns of thick wire). The shunt field is connected in parallel with the armature; the series field is in series. When current flows, the two fluxes combine.

**Cumulative Compound:**

- Series field flux *aids* shunt field flux
- $\Phi_{total} = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$
- High starting torque (better than shunt, less than pure series)
- Definite safe no-load speed (because shunt flux exists)
- Torque-speed curve: drops with speed, but less steeply than series
- Applications: Elevators, presses, shears, rolling mills, conveyor drives

**Differential Compound:**

- Series field flux *opposes* shunt field flux
- $\Phi_{total} = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}$
- Very poor starting torque (series flux reduces total flux at high currents)
- Risk of speed runaway if series flux exceeds shunt flux
- Rarely used in practice (only in special applications like winding machines where constant tension is required)

**Comparison Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    CC["CUMULATIVE COMPOUND"] --> CC1["Phi_total equals Phish plus Phise"]
    CC1 --> CC2["Higher starting torque than shunt"]
    CC2 --> CC3["Safe no-load speed"]
    CC3 --> CC4["T drops gradually with N"]
    DC["DIFFERENTIAL COMPOUND"] --> DC1["Phi_total equals Phish minus Phise"]
    DC1 --> DC2["Poor starting torque"]
    DC2 --> DC3["Risk of speed runaway"]
    DC3 --> DC4["Rarely used in practice"]
```

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks (Choice Option B)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 200 V DC series motor has $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$ and $R_{se} = 0.2\ \Omega$. At rated load, it draws 80 A and runs at 800 rpm. Calculate:
1. The back-EMF at rated load
2. The developed torque
3. The speed when the current is reduced to 40 A, assuming magnetic linearity ($\Phi \propto I_a$)
4. Comment on the safety aspect at no-load.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the condition for maximum mechanical power developed in a DC motor, and explain the significance of the armature reaction and commutation in DC motor classification.

---

### **Question B ŌĆö Model Solution (a):**

**Given:** $V = 200$ V, $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.2\ \Omega$, $I_{a1} = 80$ A, $N_1 = 800$ rpm.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Back-EMF at rated load:**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1}(R_a + R_{se}) = 200 - 80(0.4 + 0.2) = 200 - 48 = 152\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Developed torque at rated load:**

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi N_1}{60} = \frac{2\pi (800)}{60} = 83.78\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$P_{m1} = E_{b1} \cdot I_{a1} = 152 \times 80 = 12160\ \text{W}$$

$$T_1 = \frac{P_{m1}}{\omega_1} = \frac{12160}{83.78} = 145.14\ \text{Nm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Speed at $I_{a2} = 40$ A:**

At $I_{a2} = 40$ A:
$$E_{b2} = 200 - 40(0.6) = 200 - 24 = 176\ \text{V}$$

Using the back-EMF proportionality (linear region):
$$E_b = k_E \Phi N = k_E k_f I_a N$$

From the first operating point:
$$k_E k_f = \frac{E_{b1}}{I_{a1} N_1} = \frac{152}{80 \times 800} = 2.375 \times 10^{-3}$$

At the second point:
$$176 = 2.375 \times 10^{-3} \times 40 \times N_2$$
$$N_2 = \frac{176}{0.095} = 1852.63\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Safety at no-load:**

At no-load, the armature current drops drastically (only the magnetizing current). With $I_a \to 0$, $\Phi \to 0$, and from $N = E_b / (k_E \Phi)$, the speed tends to *infinity* theoretically. The centrifugal forces will tear the armature winding apart. Therefore, a series motor is **never started without a mechanical load** (e.g., gear, belt, or chain) to ensure sufficient armature current and a safe operating speed.

---

### **Question B ŌĆö Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation: Condition for Maximum Power Developed**

Mechanical power developed in a DC motor:
$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = (V - I_a R_a) \cdot I_a = V I_a - I_a^2 R_a$$

To find the maximum, differentiate $P_m$ with respect to $I_a$ and set to zero:
$$\frac{dP_m}{dI_a} = V - 2 I_a R_a = 0$$

$$\therefore I_a = \frac{V}{2 R_a}$$

Substituting back:
$$P_{m,\max} = V \left(\frac{V}{2 R_a}\right) - \left(\frac{V}{2 R_a}\right)^2 R_a = \frac{V^2}{2R_a} - \frac{V^2}{4R_a} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_a}$$

At maximum power, the back-EMF is:
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = V - \frac{V}{2} = \frac{V}{2}$$

> **Significance:** Maximum power transfer occurs when **back-EMF equals half the supply voltage**, equivalently when the internal drop $I_a R_a$ equals $E_b$. This is the DC analog of the **maximum power transfer theorem** in network theory (source impedance = load impedance). In practice, motors are *not* operated at this point because the efficiency is only 50% ŌĆö half the input power is wasted in armature copper losses.

**Armature Reaction and Commutation:**

- **Armature Reaction:** When current flows in the armature conductors, it produces its own magnetic field (cross-magnetizing flux) that distorts the main field flux. This causes commutation sparking, reduces effective flux, and shifts the neutral plane. In classification, **interpoles (commutating poles)** and **compensating windings** are used in larger motors to counteract this effect.
- **Commutation:** The process of reversing the current in the armature coil as it passes under the brushes. Poor commutation causes sparking at the brushes, which limits the motor's rating. DC series motors for traction use specially designed commutators and brush gear to handle high currents.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing A for lap/wave:** A = P for lap, A = 2 for wave. Wrong A ŌåÆ wrong $E_b$ and wrong torque. (Penalty: 2ŌĆō3 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting $I_{sh}$ in $I_L$:** In shunt and compound motors, $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$. Students often set $I_L = I_a$, losing 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 3. **Series motor no-load assumption:** Saying "speed is high" is incomplete. State the *mathematical* limit ($N \to \infty$) and the *physical* consequence (mechanical destruction).
> 4. **Mixing long-shunt and short-shunt equations:** The shunt field current and voltage drop expressions are different. Be precise.
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Always report $T$ in Nm, $\omega$ in rad/s, $P$ in W. Mixing units is a 1-mark penalty.
> 6. **Showing $E_b$ and $V$ with wrong sign:** Motor equation is $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, *not* $V - E_b = I_a R_a$ ŌĆö both are equivalent, but write it in the standard form.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions:** "Assume $\Phi$ is constant" or "linear magnetic region" must be written explicitly to get full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DC Motor classifications** (4 types): Separately Excited, Shunt, Series, Compound (Cumulative / Differential, Long-shunt / Short-shunt).
- **Universal equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (motor), with $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$.
- **Universal torque equation:** $T = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A} \Phi I_a = k_T \Phi I_a$.
- **Universal power equation:** $P_m = E_b I_a = T \omega$ where $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
- **Shunt motor:** $\Phi$ is constant ŌåÆ near-constant speed, low starting torque, good regulation.
- **Series motor:** $\Phi \propto I_a$ (linear region) ŌåÆ very high starting torque ($T \propto I_a^2$), inverse speed characteristic.
- **Cumulative compound:** $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$ ŌåÆ moderate starting torque, safe no-load, used in elevators.
- **Differential compound:** $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}$ ŌåÆ poor starting torque, rarely used.
- **Long-shunt:** $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$; $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$.
- **Short-shunt:** $I_{sh} = (V - I_{se} R_{se}) / R_{sh}$; $V = E_b + I_a R_a + I_{se} R_{se}$.
- **Maximum power condition:** $E_b = V/2$, $I_a = V/(2R_a)$, $P_{m,\max} = V^2/(4R_a)$.
- **Speed regulation formula:** $\dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$.
- **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{V I_L} = \dfrac{V I_L - \text{all losses}}{V I_L}$.
- **Armature reaction** distorts main flux; **commutation** must be smooth to avoid sparking.
- **Series motor danger:** Never run at no-load ŌĆö flux collapse causes runaway.
- **Always state the assumption** "linear magnetic region" or "constant flux" before solving numericals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When the question asks "compare" or "distinguish", always make a **two-column table**. KTU examiners award easy marks for visual presentation. When asked to "derive", show every algebraic step. When asked to "explain with neat sketch", include a Mermaid or hand-drawn block diagram ŌĆö these earn 1ŌĆō2 extra presentation marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of DC Motors and Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET303 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of DC Motors and Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DC Motor Characteristics** are the graphically represented performance curves that depict the functional relationship between key operating variables of a DC motor ŌĆö namely **armature current (IŌéÉ)**, **torque (T)**, and **speed (N)** ŌĆö under steady-state operating conditions. They are classified into **Electrical Characteristics** (T vs IŌéÉ, N vs IŌéÉ) and **Mechanical Characteristics** (N vs T).

The study of these characteristics is essential for **selection of the appropriate DC motor** for a given industrial application, since the load requirement dictates the variation of torque and speed during operation.

## 1.2 Types of DC Motors and Their Internal Field Configurations

DC motors are broadly classified based on the manner in which the **field winding is excited** relative to the armature winding:

| S.No | Motor Type | Field Connection | Flux Behavior |
|------|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **DC Shunt Motor** | Field winding in parallel with armature | Flux (Žå) Ōēł constant |
| 2 | **DC Series Motor** | Field winding in series with armature | Žå ŌłØ IŌéÉ (until saturation) |
| 3 | **DC Compound Motor** | Both shunt + series field windings | Combination of above |
| 3a | Cumulative Compound | Series flux aids shunt flux | Higher flux at load |
| 3b | Differential Compound | Series flux opposes shunt flux | Lower flux at load |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö Three Friends Pulling a Cart ­¤ÜŚ**

Imagine a cart being pulled by three different "drivers":

1. **Shunt Motor** is like an experienced cyclist who maintains a **constant pedaling pace** regardless of the slope ŌĆö slow uphill, fast downhill, but always steady. The speed barely drops with load. Good for **fans, lathes, machine tools**.

2. **Series Motor** is like a bullock-cart driver who **pulls harder and harder as the load increases**. At heavy loads, it slows down and produces enormous pulling force (high torque). But if the cart is empty, the bullock runs away (no-load runaway speed). Excellent for **trains, cranes, elevators**.

3. **Compound Motor** is like a hybrid ŌĆö having a steady driver with a backup assistant who pulls harder when needed. It offers a **balanced blend of speed regulation and starting torque**. Perfect for **rolling mills, punches, shears**.

## 1.4 Why These Characteristics Matter in Practice

> [!TIP]
> An engineer selecting a motor for a given application must study:
> - How much **torque** is needed at **starting** and during operation.
> - How the **speed** varies with **load**.
> - Whether the motor can safely handle the load without dangerous runaway conditions.

The characteristic curves serve as the **"fingerprint"** of each motor type, allowing engineers to match the motor to the load profile.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Governing Equations (Pre-requisites)

Before deriving characteristics, recall these essential equations for any DC motor:

The **back EMF** equation:

$$E_b = \frac{\phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = k \cdot \phi \cdot N$$

where k = (P┬ĘZ)/(60┬ĘA) is the **machine construction constant**.

The **armature voltage** equation (from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied to the armature circuit):

$$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$

The **electromagnetic torque** developed by the armature:

$$T_a = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\omega} = k \cdot \phi \cdot I_a \quad (\text{in N┬Ęm})$$

In practical engineering units (where T is in N┬Ęm and P in watts):

$$T_a = 9.55 \times \frac{P_{\text{developed}}}{N} \quad \text{where } N \text{ is in RPM}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Shaft Torque (T_sh)** differs from armature torque (T_a) by the mechanical losses:
> $$T_{sh} = T_a - \text{friction and windage torque (in terms of torque)}$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas ŌĆö they appear in 90% of numerical problems.**

| S.No | Quantity | Formula | Conditions | Units |
|------|----------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Back EMF | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | All motors | Volts |
| 2 | Back EMF | $E_b = k \phi N$ | All motors | Volts |
| 3 | Armature Torque | $T_a = k \phi I_a$ | All motors | N┬Ęm |
| 4 | Speed | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \phi}$ | All motors | RPM |
| 5 | Power developed | $P_d = E_b \cdot I_a$ | All motors | Watts |
| 6 | Shaft power | $P_{sh} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ | All motors | Watts |
| 7 | Shunt field flux | $\phi \approx \text{const}$ | $I_f = V / R_{sh}$ const | ŌĆö |
| 8 | Series flux | $\phi \propto I_a$ | Below saturation | ŌĆö |
| 9 | Series motor T | $T_a \propto I_a^2$ | Below saturation | ŌĆö |
| 10 | Series motor N | $N \propto \dfrac{1}{I_a}$ | Below saturation | ŌĆö |
| 11 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | All motors | % |
| 12 | Input power | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | All motors | Watts |

> [!WARNING]
> Use **kŽå** consistently as a single constant when ratio-based problems are solved (no need to find k separately unless absolute values are asked).

## 2.3 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of Each Characteristic

### A. DC SHUNT MOTOR

**Assumption:** Field flux Žå is constant because V and R_sh are constant, so I_f is constant.

#### Characteristic 1: T vs IŌéÉ (Electrical Characteristic)
Since $T_a = k \phi I_a$, with Žå constant:

$$T_a \propto I_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Straight line through origin}$$

#### Characteristic 2: N vs IŌéÉ (Electrical Characteristic)

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \phi} = \frac{V}{k\phi} - \frac{R_a}{k\phi} \cdot I_a$$

This is a **straight line with negative slope** ŌĆö speed drops slightly as IŌéÉ increases due to the $I_a R_a$ drop. Typical speed drop from no-load to full-load is only **5 to 15%** (excellent regulation).

#### Characteristic 3: N vs T (Mechanical Characteristic)
Combining $T_a = k\phi I_a$ and N equation:

$$N = N_0 - \frac{R_a}{(k\phi)^2} \cdot T_a$$

where $N_0 = V / (k\phi)$ is the **no-load speed** (ideal, ignoring friction).

This is also a **straight line** ŌĆö the shunt motor is a near-constant speed motor.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Use:** Machine tools, lathes, drilling machines, centrifugal pumps, fans, blowers ŌĆö anywhere constant speed is required.

---

### B. DC SERIES MOTOR

**Critical Relationship:** Field current I_f = Armature current I_a (since field winding is in series with armature).

#### Below Magnetic Saturation: Žå ŌłØ IŌéÉ

So $\phi = c \cdot I_a$ where c is a proportionality constant.

##### Torque Equation:

$$T_a = k \phi I_a = k \cdot (c I_a) \cdot I_a = k c I_a^2$$

> **T ŌłØ IŌéÉ┬▓ ŌĆö Parabolic curve.**

##### Speed Equation:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \phi} = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k c I_a}$$

$$N = \frac{V}{k c I_a} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k c}$$

> **N ŌłØ 1/IŌéÉ ŌĆö Inverse hyperbolic curve.**

As load decreases (IŌéÉ decreases), N increases rapidly ŌĆö leading to **dangerous no-load overspeed**. Hence series motors are **never started without load** and **never belt-connected** to loads.

#### Above Magnetic Saturation: Žå Ōēł constant

Then $T_a \propto I_a$ (linear) and N vs IŌéÉ becomes nearly linear (similar to shunt).

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU problems, unless stated otherwise, assume the series motor operates **below saturation** and use $T \propto I_a^2$.

---

### C. DC COMPOUND MOTOR

Two windings contribute to total flux:

$$\phi_{\text{total}} = \phi_{sh} \pm \phi_{se}$$

where ŌĆś+ŌĆÖ for cumulative and ŌĆśŌłÆŌĆÖ for differential.

#### Cumulative Compound Motor:
- Series flux **aids** shunt flux ŌåÆ flux increases with load.
- Speed drops more rapidly than shunt but much less than series.
- Starting torque **higher than shunt** but **lower than pure series**.
- Has **definite no-load speed** (no runaway).
- Most commonly used industrial motor.

#### Differential Compound Motor:
- Series flux **opposes** shunt flux ŌåÆ flux decreases with load.
- Speed **rises** with load ŌĆö unstable characteristic.
- Very limited applications; rarely used (special constant-speed applications).

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Chart for Engineers:**
>
> | Application | Recommended Motor |
> |-------------|-------------------|
> | Electric traction (trains, trams) | **Series** (high starting torque) |
> | Cranes, hoists, lifts | **Cumulative Compound** |
> | Lathes, drills, machine tools | **Shunt** (constant speed) |
> | Rolling mills, shears, punches | **Cumulative Compound** |
> | Centrifugal pumps, fans | **Shunt** |
> | Electric vehicles | **Series / Compound** |
> | Constant speed under variable load | **Shunt** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Expression for Speed of DC Shunt Motor

Starting from the armature voltage equation:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Substituting the EMF equation $E_b = k \phi N$:

$$V = k \phi N + I_a R_a$$

Solving for N (speed in RPM):

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \phi}$$

At no-load, IŌéÉ is small but not zero (some armature current needed to overcome rotational losses):

$$N_0 = \frac{V - I_{a0} R_a}{k \phi} \approx \frac{V}{k \phi}$$

The drop in speed from no-load to full-load:

$$\Delta N = \frac{I_{a(FL)} \cdot R_a}{k \phi}$$

Speed regulation:

$$\% \text{ Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque-Speed Relation for Shunt Motor

We have:
- $T_a = k \phi I_a$ ŌåÆ $I_a = T_a / (k\phi)$
- $N = (V - I_a R_a)/(k\phi)$

Substituting IŌéÉ:

$$N = \frac{V}{k\phi} - \frac{R_a}{(k\phi)^2} \cdot T_a$$

This linear equation in $T_a$ confirms that shunt motor has a **straight-line (drooping) N-T characteristic**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Series Motor Speed Equation (Below Saturation)

Total voltage drop equation:

$$V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = k \phi N + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

For below-saturation: $\phi = c \cdot I_a$ (linear region of magnetization curve)

$$V = k \cdot (c I_a) \cdot N + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

Dividing throughout by $I_a$:

$$\frac{V}{I_a} = k c N + (R_a + R_{se})$$

Solving for N:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k c I_a} = \frac{V}{k c I_a} - \frac{R_a + R_{se}}{k c}$$

> **Key Observation:** As $I_a \to 0$, the first term $\to \infty$. This confirms **dangerous overspeed at no-load**.

## 3.4 Comprehensive Solved Numerical Problems (KTU Board Exam Pattern)

### Numerical Problem 1: Shunt Motor Speed and Torque Calculation

**Problem:** A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of **0.5 ╬®** and shunt field resistance of **250 ╬®**. It draws a total current of **20 A** at full load. Calculate:
(i) Armature current at full load
(ii) Back EMF at full load
(iii) Speed in RPM if the machine constant kŽå = 0.05 V┬Ęs/rad equivalent, and the speed constant relates to kŽå = 0.05 V/(RPM) (assume a simple form for clarity: N = E_b/(kŽå))

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Field Current:**

$$I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Armature Current at Full Load:**

$$I_a = I_L - I_f = 20 - 1 = 19 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Back EMF:**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (19 \times 0.5) = 250 - 9.5 = 240.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Speed (using $N = E_b / (k\phi)$):**

Given $k\phi = 0.05$ V/RPM (assumed simplified constant):

$$N = \frac{240.5}{0.05} = 4810 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Developed Torque:**

$$T_a = k \phi I_a = 0.05 \times 19 = 0.95 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Alternate method:** $T_a = \dfrac{60 \cdot E_b \cdot I_a}{2\pi N} = \dfrac{60 \times 240.5 \times 19}{2\pi \times 4810} \approx 0.91$ N┬Ęm (slight difference due to rounding of kŽå).

---

### Numerical Problem 2: Series Motor Performance Analysis

**Problem:** A 200 V DC series motor takes **25 A** and runs at **1000 RPM**. Armature and series field resistances total **0.8 ╬®**. Calculate the speed when the current rises to **40 A**, assuming the field is unsaturated and flux is proportional to current.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö At 25 A condition:**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1}(R_a + R_{se}) = 200 - (25 \times 0.8) = 200 - 20 = 180 \text{ V}$$

$$E_{b1} = k \phi_1 N_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k\phi_1 = \frac{E_{b1}}{N_1} = \frac{180}{1000} = 0.18 \text{ V/RPM}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö At 40 A condition (unsaturated, so ŽåŌéé/ŽåŌéü = IŌéé/IŌéü):**

$$\phi_2 = \phi_1 \times \frac{I_{a2}}{I_{a1}} = \phi_1 \times \frac{40}{25} = 1.6 \, \phi_1$$

$$k\phi_2 = 1.6 \times k\phi_1 = 1.6 \times 0.18 = 0.288 \text{ V/RPM}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Back EMF at 40 A:**

$$E_{b2} = 200 - (40 \times 0.8) = 200 - 32 = 168 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Speed at 40 A:**

$$N_2 = \frac{E_{b2}}{k\phi_2} = \frac{168}{0.288} = 583.33 \text{ RPM}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** When current doubled (25 ŌåÆ 40 A, ratio 1.6), speed dropped from 1000 RPM to ~583 RPM. The motor **slows down significantly** as load increases ŌĆö confirming the steeply drooping characteristic of series motor.

---

### Numerical Problem 3: Torque Comparison in Series Motor

**Problem:** A DC series motor draws **15 A** and produces a torque of **25 N┬Ęm**. If load increases and current becomes **30 A** (unsaturated region), find the new torque.

**Solution:**

For an unsaturated series motor: $T_a \propto I_a^2$

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{I_{a2}}{I_{a1}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{30}{15}\right)^2 = 4$$

$$T_2 = 4 \times 25 = 100 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Doubling the armature current **quadruples** the torque ŌĆö this is why series motors are unbeatable for **traction and heavy-load starting** applications.

---

### Numerical Problem 4: Compound Motor Numerical

**Problem:** A 400 V cumulative compound DC motor has: $R_{sh} = 200 \, \Omega$, $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.1 \, \Omega$. Total input current = 50 A. Iron losses = 600 W, friction & windage losses = 400 W. Calculate:
(i) Net flux as a percentage of shunt flux
(ii) Armature torque
(iii) Shaft torque
(iv) Efficiency

Assume at load: $\phi_{se} = 0.3 \, \phi_{sh}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Field Currents:**

$$I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{400}{200} = 2 \text{ A}$$

$$I_a = I_L - I_f = 50 - 2 = 48 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Net Flux:**

$$\phi = \phi_{sh} + \phi_{se} = 1.0 \, \phi_{sh} + 0.3 \, \phi_{sh} = 1.3 \, \phi_{sh}$$

> **Net flux is 130% of shunt flux.**

**Step 3 ŌĆö Back EMF:**

$$E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 400 - 48(0.3) = 400 - 14.4 = 385.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Power Developed:**

$$P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = 385.6 \times 48 = 18{,}508.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Armature Torque (need speed first):**

Assuming $k\phi_{sh}$ at rated condition gives $N_0 = 1000$ RPM at no-load:

$$k\phi_{sh} = \frac{V - I_{a0}(R_a + R_{se})}{N_0} \approx \frac{400}{1000} = 0.4 \text{ V/RPM (assumed)}$$

$$k\phi = 1.3 \times 0.4 = 0.52 \text{ V/RPM}$$

$$N = \frac{E_b}{k\phi} = \frac{385.6}{0.52} = 741.5 \text{ RPM}$$

$$T_a = k\phi \cdot I_a = 0.52 \times 48 = 24.96 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Shaft Torque:**

Mechanical losses: $P_{mech} = 400$ W (friction) + rotational losses portion

$$P_{mech} = 400 \text{ W (just friction given)}$$

$$T_f = \frac{60 \cdot P_{mech}}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 400}{2\pi \times 741.5} = 5.15 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_f = 24.96 - 5.15 = 19.81 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Efficiency:**

$$P_{out} = P_d - \text{iron loss} - \text{friction loss} = 18508.8 - 600 - 400 = 17{,}508.8 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 400 \times 50 = 20{,}000 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{17508.8}{20000} \times 100 = 87.54\%$$

---

### Numerical Problem 5: Speed Regulation Problem

**Problem:** A DC shunt motor runs at **1500 RPM** at no-load and at **1420 RPM** at full load. Calculate:
(i) Speed regulation
(ii) Comment on the type of motor characteristic.

**Solution:**

$$\% \text{ Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1500 - 1420}{1420} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{80}{1420} \times 100 = 5.63\%$$

> **Conclusion:** With a speed regulation of only ~5.6%, this motor exhibits excellent **constant-speed characteristic** ŌĆö characteristic of a **shunt motor**.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Shunt | Series | Cumulative Compound | Differential Compound |
|-----------|-------|--------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Flux variation | Nearly constant | ŌłØ IŌéÉ (till saturation) | Increases moderately | Decreases moderately |
| T vs IŌéÉ | Straight line | Parabolic | Between shunt & series | Slightly rises |
| N vs IŌéÉ | Slight drop | Inverse hyperbolic | Moderate drop | Rises |
| N vs T | Nearly straight | Steep hyperbolic droop | Moderate droop | Rising (unstable) |
| Speed regulation | **Excellent (5-15%)** | Very poor | Good | Poor (unstable) |
| Starting torque | Low to moderate | **Very high (2-3├Ś full load)** | High | Low |
| No-load safety | Safe | **Dangerous (runaway)** | Safe | Safe |
| Applications | Constant speed loads | Traction, hoists | Presses, elevators | Special uses only |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Sketch and explain the speed-armature current characteristics of a DC shunt motor."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The speed-armature current characteristic of a DC shunt motor is derived from:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \phi}$$

Since V, R_a, and Žå are essentially constant (field current I_f = V/R_sh remains constant), the equation represents a **straight line with a small negative slope**.

- At no-load: N Ōēł NŌéĆ = V/(kŽå) ŌĆö maximum speed
- As load increases: IŌéÉ increases, causing a small drop in speed due to IŌéÉRŌéÉ drop.
- The characteristic is a **slightly drooping straight line**, indicating **good speed regulation** (typically 5-15%).

**Diagram:** A near-horizontal slightly downward-sloping line from NŌéĆ at I_a = 0 to N_FL at full-load current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking tip:** Award 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the explanation, and 1 mark for the diagram.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**"Why is a DC series motor never started without load?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A DC series motor has the field winding in series with the armature, so I_f = I_a. Therefore:

$$\phi \propto I_a \quad \text{(below saturation)}$$

The speed equation becomes:

$$N = \frac{V}{k \phi} - \text{constant} \propto \frac{1}{I_a}$$

At no-load, I_a ŌåÆ very small (only to overcome rotational losses). Hence:

$$N \to \infty \quad \text{(theoretically)}$$

Practically, the motor achieves **dangerously high speeds** that can destroy the armature windings due to **centrifugal forces**. Hence, **series motors must never be operated without mechanical load** and are **never connected via belts** to the load.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the condition for maximum efficiency of a DC motor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V, 1000 RPM DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.05 ╬® and shunt field resistance of 110 ╬®. The armature current at no-load is 8 A. If the full-load armature current is 200 A, calculate:
(i) Speed at full load
(ii) Developed torque
(iii) Shaft torque, given rotational losses = 1500 W **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Maximum Efficiency Derivation

**Efficiency of a DC motor:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V I_L - \text{Losses}}{V I_L}$$

The **constant losses** (iron loss + friction loss) = $P_c$ (independent of load).
The **variable loss** (armature copper loss) = $I_a^2 R_a$.

At maximum efficiency, **variable loss = constant loss**:

$$\boxed{I_a^2 R_a = P_c}$$

This is the condition for maximum efficiency. At this point, differentiating ╬Ę with respect to I_a and setting to zero yields this condition.

**Explanation (for 7 marks):**

| Step | Marks |
|------|-------|
| Definition of efficiency equation | 1 |
| Expression for constant and variable losses | 2 |
| Differentiating ╬Ę with respect to IŌéÉ and equating to zero | 3 |
| Final condition I_a┬▓R_a = P_c | 1 |

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- V = 220 V, NŌéĆ = 1000 RPM (no-load speed)
- R_a = 0.05 ╬®, R_sh = 110 ╬®
- I_{a(NL)} = 8 A, I_{a(FL)} = 200 A
- Rotational losses = 1500 W

**Step 1 ŌĆö No-Load Back EMF and kŽå:**

$$E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - (8 \times 0.05) = 220 - 0.4 = 219.6 \text{ V}$$

$$k\phi = \frac{E_{b0}}{N_0} = \frac{219.6}{1000} = 0.2196 \text{ V/RPM}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Speed at Full Load:**

$$E_{b(FL)} = V - I_{a(FL)} R_a = 220 - (200 \times 0.05) = 220 - 10 = 210 \text{ V}$$

$$N_{FL} = \frac{E_{b(FL)}}{k\phi} = \frac{210}{0.2196} = 956.28 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Developed Torque:**

$$T_a = k\phi \cdot I_{a(FL)} = 0.2196 \times 200 = 43.92 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Shaft Torque:**

$$P_{mech} = 1500 \text{ W (rotational losses including friction)}$$

$$T_f = \frac{60 \times P_{mech}}{2\pi N_{FL}} = \frac{60 \times 1500}{2\pi \times 956.28} = 14.98 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_f = 43.92 - 14.98 = 28.94 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i)** Speed at full load Ōēł **956.3 RPM**
- **(ii)** Developed torque = **43.92 N┬Ęm**
- **(iii)** Shaft torque Ōēł **28.94 N┬Ęm**

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

### [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the torque-armature current and speed-armature current characteristics of a DC series motor. Mention the role of saturation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 250 V DC series motor has armature and field resistances of 0.1 ╬® and 0.05 ╬® respectively. At a certain load, the motor draws 60 A and runs at 800 RPM. If the load is increased such that the motor draws 90 A (unsaturated), calculate:
(i) New speed
(ii) Ratio of new torque to original torque **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Series Motor Characteristics

**Torque vs Armature Current (Below Saturation):**

Since Žå ŌłØ IŌéÉ, then $T_a = k\phi I_a \Rightarrow T_a \propto I_a^2$. The curve is **parabolic** (concave-up parabola starting from origin).

**Speed vs Armature Current (Below Saturation):**

$$N = \frac{V}{k\phi} - \text{const} \propto \frac{1}{I_a}$$

This is an **inverse hyperbolic curve** ŌĆö speed is very high at low current and drops rapidly as load increases.

**Role of Saturation:**

Above magnetic saturation, Žå becomes nearly constant, and the characteristics transform:
- $T_a \propto I_a$ (linear instead of parabolic)
- N vs IŌéÉ becomes nearly linear (more gentle drop)

**Diagrams (Sketches needed):**
1. T vs IŌéÉ: parabolic (below saturation) merging into straight line (above saturation)
2. N vs IŌéÉ: steep hyperbolic curve below saturation, flattening above saturation

**Mark Distribution:**
| Step | Marks |
|------|-------|
| Derivation with equations | 2 |
| T vs IŌéÉ explanation | 1.5 |
| N vs IŌéÉ explanation | 1.5 |
| Role of saturation | 1 |
| Sketches | 1 |

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- V = 250 V, R_a = 0.1 ╬®, R_se = 0.05 ╬®
- At load 1: I_{a1} = 60 A, N_1 = 800 RPM

**Step 1 ŌĆö Back EMF at Initial Condition:**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1}(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 60(0.15) = 250 - 9 = 241 \text{ V}$$

$$k\phi_1 = \frac{E_{b1}}{N_1} = \frac{241}{800} = 0.30125 \text{ V/RPM}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö At I_{a2} = 90 A (unsaturated):**

Flux ratio: $\phi_2/\phi_1 = I_{a2}/I_{a1} = 90/60 = 1.5$

$$k\phi_2 = 1.5 \times k\phi_1 = 1.5 \times 0.30125 = 0.451875 \text{ V/RPM}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö New Back EMF:**

$$E_{b2} = 250 - 90(0.15) = 250 - 13.5 = 236.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö New Speed:**

$$N_2 = \frac{E_{b2}}{k\phi_2} = \frac{236.5}{0.451875} \approx 523.4 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Torque Ratio:**

For unsaturated series motor:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{I_{a2}}{I_{a1}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{90}{60}\right)^2 = 2.25$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i)** New speed Ōēł **523.4 RPM**
- **(ii)** Torque ratio = **2.25 : 1** (torque increased by 125%)

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract field current** (I_f = V/R_sh) when finding armature current in shunt motors. The total line current is NOT the armature current.
>
> 2. **Using I_a instead of I_L** in the efficiency equation P_in = V┬ĘI_L ŌĆö efficiency calculation goes wrong.
>
> 3. **Not stating "below saturation" assumption** for series motor problems ŌĆö examiners expect this declaration explicitly.
>
> 4. **Mixing up shaft torque (T_sh) and armature torque (T_a)** ŌĆö T_a is the developed torque; T_sh is after subtracting mechanical (rotational) losses.
>
> 5. **Drawing characteristics without axes labels and units** ŌĆö KTU board examiners deduct at least 1 mark for unlabeled diagrams.
>
> 6. **Wrong formula application**: Using $T = 9.55 \times P/N$ without verifying units ŌĆö remember P should be in **watts** and N in **RPM**.
>
> 7. **Confusing cumulative and differential compound motors** ŌĆö clearly state which one and explain the direction of series flux.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Quick-Reference Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**
>
> - ŌŁÉ DC motor characteristics show **T vs IŌéÉ**, **N vs IŌéÉ**, and **N vs T** behavior.
> - ŌŁÉ **Shunt motor:** Žå constant ŌåÆ T ŌłØ IŌéÉ (linear), N nearly constant ŌåÆ used for **constant speed** applications.
> - ŌŁÉ **Series motor:** Žå ŌłØ IŌéÉ (below saturation) ŌåÆ T ŌłØ IŌéÉ┬▓ (parabolic), N ŌłØ 1/IŌéÉ (hyperbolic) ŌåÆ used for **traction**.
> - ŌŁÉ **Cumulative compound:** Series flux aids shunt flux ŌåÆ moderate drooping characteristic, high starting torque.
> - ŌŁÉ **Differential compound:** Series flux opposes shunt flux ŌåÆ unstable, rarely used.
> - ŌŁÉ Master equations: $E_b = V - I_a R_a$, $E_b = k\phi N$, $T_a = k\phi I_a$, $N = (V - I_a R_a)/(k\phi)$.
> - ŌŁÉ **Maximum efficiency condition:** Variable loss (IŌéÉ┬▓RŌéÉ) = Constant loss (iron + friction).
> - ŌŁÉ **Speed regulation:** $(N_{NL} - N_{FL}) / N_{FL} \times 100$%.
> - ŌŁÉ Series motor has **dangerous overspeed at no-load** ŌĆö always operate with load.
> - ŌŁÉ **No-load speed of shunt motor:** NŌéĆ Ōēł V/(kŽå).
> - ŌŁÉ **Shaft torque:** $T_{sh} = T_a - T_{friction}$.
> - ŌŁÉ When current doubles in unsaturated series motor, **torque quadruples**.
> - ŌŁÉ Forgot the field current? Always compute $I_f = V/R_{sh}$ first in shunt motors.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET303 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Starting of DC Motors - 3-Point Starter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ­¤öī Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## ­¤ōś Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Point Starter (DC Motor Starting Resistance Box):**
> A **3-point starter** is an electromechanical protective device used to start a **DC shunt** or **DC compound motor** safely. It performs three critical functions simultaneously:
> 1. **Limits the starting armature current** by inserting a calibrated external resistance in series with the armature at the instant of switching ON.
> 2. **Progressively cuts out** this starting resistance in steps as the motor accelerates and develops back EMF.
> 3. **Automatically disconnects the motor** from the supply in the event of supply failure (No-Volt protection) or excessive mechanical load (Overload protection).

It is called a **"3-point"** starter because it has **three main external terminals** that connect to the external circuit:
- **L** (Line) ŌĆö from positive of supply
- **A** (Armature) ŌĆö to armature winding
- **F** (Field) ŌĆö to shunt field winding
*(Plus a fourth auxiliary terminal, but the name originates from the three primary connection points.)*

---

## ­¤¦Ā Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are **riding a bicycle from a dead stop up a steep hill**. If you try to pedal at full power the moment you start, the chain will snap because the entire force is suddenly transferred to a stationary wheel. So what do you do? You **start in the lowest gear** ŌĆö pedaling is easy, but the wheel turns slowly. As the bicycle gains momentum, you **shift up through the gears one by one**, until finally you're cruising in the top gear at full speed.

A **3-point starter does exactly the same thing for a DC motor**:
- **Low gear** = High external resistance (motor gets just enough current to "budge" without burning out)
- **High gear** = Zero external resistance (motor runs at full speed, naturally)
- **Auto-protection** = If you fall off (supply fails) or the hill becomes too steep (overload), the starter **automatically kicks the bike back to neutral (OFF)**, preventing damage.

---

## ŌÜĪ Why is a Starter Necessary at All? ŌĆö The Physics Behind It

When a DC motor is at rest, its **back EMF ($E_b$) is zero**, because $E_b \propto N$ (speed). The supply voltage $V$ then drives current only against the tiny **armature resistance $R_a$** (which is often less than **1 ╬®** in practical machines).

$$I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{V}{R_a} \quad \text{(at starting, } E_b = 0\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This starting current can be **10 to 20 times** the rated full-load current. Such a surge would:
> - **Burn the armature windings** due to $I^2R$ heating.
> - **Distort the commutator brush arrangement** due to mechanical shock.
> - **Cause severe voltage dip** in the supply line, affecting other connected loads.
> - **Demagnetize the field poles** due to excessive armature reaction.

A **3-point starter is therefore a mandatory protective accessory** ŌĆö not an optional luxury ŌĆö for any DC motor rated above a fraction of a horsepower.

---

## ­¤¦® Key Physical Components of a 3-Point Starter

| # | Component | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Starting Resistance ($R_{st}$)** | A graded resistance box with multiple studs/contacts to limit starting current. |
| 2 | **No-Volt Coil (NVC) / Hold-on Coil** | An electromagnet connected in series with the shunt field. Keeps the handle in ON position. |
| 3 | **Overload Release (OLR) Coil** | A series electromagnet that trips the starter if armature current exceeds a safe limit. |
| 4 | **Brass Arc / Copper Strip** | Provides a low-resistance path bypassing the last resistance stud so the circuit is complete even when the handle is in the final ON position. |
| 5 | **Soft Iron Plunger** | Attached to the handle; pulled up by NVC when energized. |
| 6 | **Return Spring** | Pulls the handle back to the OFF position if NVC is de-energized. |
| 7 | **Studs (1, 2, 3, ŌĆ” n)** | Tapping points on the starting resistance; each successive stud cuts out one resistance section. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## ­¤ö¼ Operational Breakdown ŌĆö The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

### ­¤ö╣ Step 1: Initial Switching ON (Handle at Stud 1)
- The starter handle is moved from the **OFF position** to the **first stud**.
- The **entire starting resistance $R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \dots$** is now in series with the armature.
- Armature current is limited to a safe value (typically **1.5 to 2 times** rated full-load current).
- The motor begins to rotate slowly, generating a small back EMF $E_b$.

### ­¤ö╣ Step 2: Acceleration Phase (Handle Moves Across Studs)
- As the handle slides across studs 2, 3, 4, ŌĆ” the **resistance in the armature circuit is progressively reduced**.
- The motor accelerates, $E_b$ rises, and the difference $(V - E_b)$ falls.
- This naturally causes armature current to drop, which is exactly what we want.

### ­¤ö╣ Step 3: Final ON Position (Stud 'n' / Run Position)
- All starting resistance is **fully cut out**.
- The handle is held magnetically by the energized **No-Volt Coil (NVC)**.
- Current flows from **L ŌåÆ Brass Arc ŌåÆ Armature ŌåÆ A** (direct connection).
- A small current continues to flow through the **NVC ŌåÆ Shunt Field ŌåÆ F** loop.

### ­¤ö╣ Step 4: Steady-State Running
- The motor runs at its natural no-load or loaded speed.
- $E_b$ adjusts itself to balance the applied voltage and the $I_a R_a$ drop.
- No further operator action is required.

---

## ­¤Ü© Protection Mechanisms ŌĆö How the Starter "Watches Over" the Motor

### ­¤ģ░’ĖÅ No-Volt Protection (NVC Action)
- The NVC is connected **in series with the shunt field winding** across the supply.
- During normal operation, the NVC produces a magnetic flux strong enough to **hold the soft iron plunger** (and hence the handle) in the upward (ON) position.
- If the **supply voltage fails or drops severely**:
  - Field current $\downarrow$ ŌåÆ NVC flux $\downarrow$ ŌåÆ Plunger is released.
  - The **return spring** yanks the handle back to OFF.
  - This prevents the motor from **suddenly restarting** when supply returns, which could endanger workers and machinery.

### ­¤ģ▒’ĖÅ Overload Protection (OLR Action)
- The OLR coil is connected **in series with the armature**.
- It carries the **full armature current** at all times.
- If the mechanical load suddenly increases (jam, blockage), $I_a$ spikes.
- When $I_a$ exceeds a preset threshold, the OLR magnetic flux **lifts an iron lever**, which **short-circuits the NVC** (via a brass stud).
- The NVC de-energizes ŌåÆ handle springs back to OFF ŌåÆ motor is disconnected.
- To restart, the operator must **manually reset the brass stud** and re-engage the handle.

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Major Drawback of the 3-Point Starter (KTU-Favorite Question!)

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Field-Weakening" Problem:**
> The NVC and the shunt field are connected in **series**. When the operator deliberately weakens the field (to achieve speeds **above rated speed** for shunt motor speed control), the NVC current also drops.
> At very weak field conditions, the NVC flux may become **insufficient to hold the plunger**, even though the supply is healthy.
> The handle will **spuriously release**, disconnecting the motor at the very moment the operator wants high-speed operation.
>
> **Solution:** Use a **4-point starter**, where the NVC is connected **directly across the supply** (independent of the field), so that field weakening does not affect NVC operation.

---

## ­¤ōŖ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Back EMF equation (armature loop) | V, A, ╬® |
| 2 | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | EMF equation from first principles | V, Wb, rpm |
| 3 | $I_{a,start} = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ | Starting current **without** starter (at $E_b = 0$) | A |
| 4 | $I_{a,start} = \dfrac{V}{R_a + R_{st}}$ | Starting current **with** starter (at $E_b = 0$) | A |
| 5 | $I_{a,start} = x \cdot I_{a,FL}$ | Desired starting current is $x$ times full-load current (usually $x = 1.5$ to $2$) | A |
| 6 | $R_{st} = \dfrac{V}{x \cdot I_{a,FL}} - R_a$ | Required starting resistance to limit current to $x \cdot I_{FL}$ | ╬® |
| 7 | $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi}$ | Speed equation of a DC motor | rpm |
| 8 | $N_2 = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \cdot \dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}$ | Speed relation between two load points (neglecting $I_a R_a$) | rpm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):**
> Students are expected to derive the **back EMF equation**, apply the **starting resistance formula** in numerical problems, and explain the **role of NVC and OLR** in the starter. The 4-point starter is covered as a **comparative follow-up topic** and is often asked as a 7-mark sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# ­¤ōÉ Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## ­¤öŹ Derivation 1: Back EMF Equation of a DC Machine ($E_b = \Phi Z N P / 60A$)

This is the **most fundamental derivation** in DC machine theory. It expresses back EMF in terms of physical machine parameters and operating speed.

**Step 1: Consider one armature conductor.**

According to **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the EMF induced in a single conductor moving through a magnetic field is:

$$e = B \cdot l \cdot v$$

where:
- $B$ = Flux density in Wb/m┬▓
- $l$ = Active length of the conductor in metres
- $v$ = Linear velocity of the conductor in m/s

**Step 2: Express velocity in terms of rotational speed.**

The conductor lies on the surface of the armature at a distance $D/2$ from the axis. If the armature rotates at $N$ rpm, the linear velocity is:

$$v = \pi D \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

Substituting:

$$e = B \cdot l \cdot \pi D \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

**Step 3: Replace $B \cdot l \cdot \pi D$ with total flux per pole.**

We know that the **flux per pole** $\Phi$ is the area under the flux-density curve over the pole-pitch. Mathematically:

$$\Phi = B \cdot A_{pole} = B \cdot l \cdot \pi D / P$$

Therefore:

$$B \cdot l \cdot \pi D = \Phi \cdot P$$

**Step 4: Substitute back into the conductor EMF equation.**

$$e = \Phi \cdot P \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

This is the EMF generated in **one single conductor**.

**Step 5: Consider the full armature winding.**

The total number of conductors in the armature is $Z$. Since the conductors are connected in **$A$ parallel paths** (where $A = 2$ for wave winding and $A = P$ for lap winding), the number of conductors in **one series path** is $Z/A$. The conductors in one series path add their EMFs in series.

So the **total EMF per parallel path** (which is the back EMF across the brushes) is:

$$E_b = e \cdot \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right) = \Phi \cdot P \cdot \frac{N}{60} \cdot \frac{Z}{A}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}}$$

> **Why this matters in starters:** As the motor speeds up, $N \uparrow$, and therefore $E_b \uparrow$. This rising back EMF is what naturally limits the armature current, allowing the starter handle to cut out resistance gradually.

---

## ­¤öŹ Derivation 2: Required Starting Resistance $R_{st}$ for a Given Starting Current

This is the **classic KTU 14-mark numerical** on starters. Let's derive the working formula.

**Step 1: Write the armature circuit equation at the instant of starting.**

At $t = 0$ (motor at rest), $N = 0$, so $E_b = 0$. With the starter resistance $R_{st}$ in series:

$$I_{a,start} = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a + R_{st}} = \frac{V}{R_a + R_{st}}$$

**Step 2: Express the desired starting current as a multiple of full-load current.**

Let the design constraint be:

$$I_{a,start} = x \cdot I_{a,FL}$$

where $x$ is typically **1.5, 2, or 2.5** (as specified in the problem).

**Step 3: Equate and solve for $R_{st}$.**

$$\frac{V}{R_a + R_{st}} = x \cdot I_{a,FL}$$

$$R_a + R_{st} = \frac{V}{x \cdot I_{a,FL}}$$

$$\boxed{R_{st} = \frac{V}{x \cdot I_{a,FL}} - R_a}$$

> **Units reminder:** $V$ in volts, $I_{a,FL}$ in amperes, $R_a$ in ohms ŌåÆ $R_{st}$ in ohms. The answer must be **positive**; if negative, the motor does not need any starter (very rare in practice).

---

## ­¤öŹ Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A **220 V DC shunt motor** has the following nameplate and test data:
> - Rated full-load armature current: $I_{a,FL} = 50$ A
> - Armature resistance: $R_a = 0.4 \, \Omega$
> - Shunt field resistance: $R_{sh} = 110 \, \Omega$
> - Rated speed: $N = 1000$ rpm
>
> Calculate:
> 1. The starting current **without** any starter.
> 2. The starting current **with** a starter, if the starting current is to be limited to **1.5 times** the full-load armature current.
> 3. The required starting resistance $R_{st}$.

### **Solution:**

**Part (1):** Starting current without a starter.

At the instant of starting, $E_b = 0$, so all of $V$ drops across $R_a$:

$$I_{a,start} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{220}{0.4}$$

$$I_{a,start} = 550 \text{ A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **11 times** the rated full-load current of 50 A. Such a surge would instantly destroy the armature ŌĆö a starter is absolutely essential.

**Part (2):** Starting current with starter (limited to 1.5 ├Ś $I_{a,FL}$).

$$I_{a,start} = 1.5 \times 50 = 75 \text{ A}$$

**Part (3):** Required starting resistance.

Using the derived formula:

$$R_{st} = \frac{V}{x \cdot I_{a,FL}} - R_a$$

$$R_{st} = \frac{220}{1.5 \times 50} - 0.4$$

$$R_{st} = \frac{220}{75} - 0.4$$

$$R_{st} = 2.933 - 0.4$$

$$\boxed{R_{st} = 2.533 \, \Omega}$$

> **Verification:** Total circuit resistance at start $= 0.4 + 2.533 = 2.933 \, \Omega$. Current $= 220 / 2.933 = 75$ A Ō£ō

---

## ­¤öŹ Analytical Decision Matrix: Why 3-Point vs 4-Point?

| Feature | 3-Point Starter | 4-Point Starter |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of terminals** | 3 (L, A, F) | 4 (L, A, F, NVC) |
| **NVC connection** | Series with shunt field | Across supply (independent) |
| **Field weakening impact on NVC** | ŌØī NVC may release at high speeds | Ō£ģ NVC unaffected |
| **Suitability for field-weakening control** | Limited (only ~1.5├Ś rated speed) | Excellent (any speed range) |
| **Construction complexity** | Simpler | Slightly more complex |
| **Cost** | Lower | Slightly higher |
| **KTU 2024 emphasis** | **High ŌĆö primary topic** | High ŌĆö comparative question |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# ­¤ōØ Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ­¤ģ░’ĖÅ Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023 Model]**
*Why is a starter necessary for a DC motor? Why can't a DC motor be started directly across the supply line?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

At the instant of starting, the armature of a DC motor is at rest, so its **back EMF $E_b$ is zero**. The supply voltage $V$ is then applied entirely across the very small armature resistance $R_a$ (typically less than 1 ╬®). The resulting starting current $I_{a,start} = V / R_a$ becomes **10 to 20 times the rated full-load current**.

This excessive current:
1. **Overheats the armature winding** (excessive $I^2R$ losses), damaging the insulation.
2. **Produces a huge starting torque** (since $T \propto \Phi I_a$), causing dangerous mechanical shock to the shaft, coupling, and load.
3. **Causes a severe voltage dip** in the supply, affecting other consumers on the same line.

To prevent this, a **starter** (such as a 3-point starter) is used to insert a **resistance in series with the armature** at starting. This limits $I_{a,start}$ to a safe value (usually 1.5 to 2 times the rated current). The resistance is cut out in steps as the motor accelerates and $E_b$ builds up.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022 Model]**
*What is the function of the No-Volt Coil (NVC) in a 3-point starter?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **No-Volt Coil (NVC)**, also called the **hold-on coil**, is an electromagnet connected in series with the shunt field winding across the supply. It serves two purposes:

1. **Holding function:** When the supply voltage is normal, the current through the NVC produces a magnetic flux strong enough to attract and hold the soft-iron plunger attached to the starter handle, keeping it in the **ON (run) position** against the pull of the return spring.

2. **No-volt protection:** If the supply voltage fails or drops below a safe threshold, the NVC flux collapses, the plunger is released, and the **return spring** automatically pulls the handle back to the **OFF position**, isolating the motor. This prevents the motor from suddenly and dangerously restarting when supply is restored.

> **Disadvantage:** Because the NVC is in series with the shunt field, **weakening the field** (for above-rated speed control) reduces NVC current, which may cause spurious release of the handle. This is the main reason for using a **4-point starter** in such applications.

---

## ­¤ģ▒’ĖÅ Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Two alternative choices are provided. Attempt **either** Choice A **or** Choice B in full.

---

### Ō£Å’ĖÅ Choice A ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a 3-point starter used for a DC shunt motor. Also explain the functions of the No-Volt Coil (NVC) and Overload Release (OLR).*

**Sub-part (a): Construction and Working of 3-Point Starter (7 marks)**

**Construction:**
A 3-point starter consists of the following components housed in a single protective enclosure:

1. **Three external terminals**: L (Line from positive supply), A (connected to armature), F (connected to shunt field).
2. **Starting resistance** $R_{st}$: A graded wire-wound resistance with multiple tapping studs (1, 2, 3, ŌĆ”, n) on a circular arc. Each successive stud cuts out one section of the resistance.
3. **No-Volt Coil (NVC)**: An electromagnet wound with many turns of fine wire, connected in series with the shunt field across the supply.
4. **Overload Release (OLR) coil**: A few-turns coil of thick wire, connected in series with the armature.
5. **Brass arc / copper strip**: Provides a permanent low-resistance path between L and A in the final ON position, bypassing the last stud.
6. **Handle** with a soft-iron plunger attached; pivots across the studs and is held in position by NVC.
7. **Return spring**: Pulls the handle back to OFF when NVC de-energizes.
8. **Reset lever for OLR**: A brass strip that, when tripped, must be manually reset.

**Working:**

- When the operator moves the handle from OFF to stud 1, the **entire starting resistance** is in series with the armature. The armature current is limited to a safe value (Ōēł 1.5 ├Ś $I_{FL}$), and the motor starts rotating slowly.
- As the motor accelerates, **back EMF $E_b$ builds up**, and the armature current naturally decreases.
- The operator moves the handle progressively to studs 2, 3, 4, ŌĆ” n, each step **cutting out a section of the starting resistance**.
- At the final stud (run position), the brass arc short-circuits the remaining resistance, and the NVC holds the handle magnetically in the ON position. The motor now runs on its natural $E_b$ŌĆō$I_a$ equilibrium.

**Sub-part (b): Functions of NVC and OLR (7 marks)**

| Coil | Connection | Function | Failure Mode Handled |
|------|------------|----------|----------------------|
| **NVC (No-Volt Coil)** | Series with shunt field, across supply | (i) Holds the handle in ON position by magnetic attraction; (ii) Releases the handle to OFF via spring if supply fails. | Supply failure, under-voltage, sudden restoration of supply. |
| **OLR (Overload Release)** | Series with armature | When $I_a$ exceeds safe limit, lifts an iron lever that **short-circuits the NVC**, de-energizing it and tripping the handle to OFF. | Mechanical jam, sudden overload, short-circuit in load. |

**Drawback:** Since NVC is in series with the shunt field, deliberate field weakening (for speeds above rated) reduces NVC current, causing spurious release of the handle. Hence, for motors requiring **wide-range speed control by field weakening**, a **4-point starter** is preferred.

---

### Ō£Å’ĖÅ Choice B ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**

*Derive the EMF equation of a DC machine. A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 ╬® and a field winding resistance of 250 ╬®. When driving a load, the motor draws an armature current of 30 A. Calculate:*
*(i) the back EMF, (ii) the starting current without any starter, and (iii) the starting resistance needed to limit the starting current to 1.5 times the rated armature current.*

**Sub-part (a): Derivation of EMF Equation (7 marks)**

Let the machine have:
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole in Wb
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $P$ = number of poles
- $A$ = number of parallel paths ($A = 2$ for wave, $A = P$ for lap)
- $N$ = speed in rpm

EMF induced in **one conductor** (by Faraday's law):

$$e = B \cdot l \cdot v$$

With $v = \pi D \cdot N / 60$ and $B \cdot l \cdot \pi D = \Phi P$:

$$e = \Phi P \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

Conductors in **one series path** = $Z / A$, and these add in series:

$$E_b = e \cdot \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

**Sub-part (b): Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** $V = 250$ V, $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250 \, \Omega$, $I_a = 30$ A (running condition).

**(i) Back EMF at running condition:**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (30)(0.5) = 250 - 15$$

$$E_b = 235 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Starting current without any starter (at $E_b = 0$):**

$$I_{a,start} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{250}{0.5}$$

$$I_{a,start} = 500 \text{ A}$$

> This is **500 / 30 Ōēł 16.7 times** the running armature current ŌĆö clearly destructive without a starter.

**(iii) Required starting resistance $R_{st}$ to limit $I_{a,start}$ to $1.5 \times 30 = 45$ A:**

Using the formula:

$$R_{st} = \frac{V}{x \cdot I_{a,FL}} - R_a = \frac{250}{1.5 \times 30} - 0.5$$

$$R_{st} = \frac{250}{45} - 0.5 = 5.556 - 0.5$$

$$\boxed{R_{st} = 5.056 \, \Omega}$$

> **Verification:** Total starting resistance = $0.5 + 5.056 = 5.556 \, \Omega$. Starting current = $250 / 5.556 = 45$ A Ō£ō

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make ŌĆö and lose marks for:**
> 1. **Confusing "armature current" with "line current"** in a shunt motor. Remember: $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$. For numerical problems, **read carefully** whether the given current is the armature current or the total line current.
> 2. **Forgetting the field circuit** when drawing the 3-point starter. The NVC is in **series with the shunt field** ŌĆö this is the single most important point examiners test.
> 3. **Using $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ instead of $E_b = V - I_a R_a$** (or vice-versa). Sign convention matters.
> 4. **Stating the starter is needed "to reduce voltage"** ŌĆö this is **wrong**. The starter is for **limiting current**, not reducing voltage. Examiners specifically deduct for this.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the brass arc** in the construction sketch. It's a 1-mark item that students often miss.
> 6. **In numerical problems**, not writing the **unit** (╬®, A, V) ŌĆö a 0.5 mark penalty each time.

---

## ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 10 Takeaways for KTU 2024 Exam ŌĆö Module 2 / 3-Point Starter:**

- ŌŁÉ A DC motor **cannot be started directly** across the supply because $E_b = 0$ at rest, causing destructive inrush current ($I_{a,start} = V / R_a$, often 10ŌĆō20├Ś rated).
- ŌŁÉ A **3-point starter** is a protective device that **limits starting current** by inserting series resistance, **progressively cuts it out** as $E_b$ builds, and provides **automatic no-volt + overload protection**.
- ŌŁÉ The starter has **three primary terminals**: **L (Line)**, **A (Armature)**, **F (Field)**.
- ŌŁÉ The **No-Volt Coil (NVC)** is connected in **series with the shunt field**. It holds the handle ON magnetically; releases it on supply failure.
- ŌŁÉ The **Overload Release (OLR) coil** is connected in **series with the armature**. It trips the starter if $I_a$ exceeds a safe limit by short-circuiting the NVC.
- ŌŁÉ The **brass arc / copper strip** provides a permanent low-resistance path from L to A in the final ON position.
- ŌŁÉ The **return spring** restores the handle to OFF when NVC de-energizes.
- ŌŁÉ **Key formula for starting resistance:** $R_{st} = \dfrac{V}{x \cdot I_{a,FL}} - R_a$, where $x$ is typically 1.5 to 2.
- ŌŁÉ **EMF equation:** $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$ ŌĆö derived from Faraday's law and the geometry of the armature.
- ŌŁÉ **Major drawback of 3-point starter:** Spurious release during field-weakening speed control. **Solution:** 4-point starter (NVC connected directly across supply, independent of the field).

---

*End of Module 2 ŌĆö 3-Point Starter Notes | PCEET303 | KTU 2024 Scheme*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Braking - Regenerative Braking, Dynamic Braking and Plugging (Concepts only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET303 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Braking - Regenerative Braking, Dynamic Braking and Plugging (Concepts only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Electrical Braking of DC Motors

In industrial drive applications, it is frequently necessary to **stop a motor quickly, reverse its direction, or limit its speed** under specific load conditions. While mechanical friction brakes are common, **electrical braking** offers smoother, faster, and wear-free stopping, which is critical for elevators, electric trains, cranes, machine tools, and electric vehicles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> *Electrical Braking of a DC motor is the technique of converting the kinetic energy stored in the rotating armature (and its connected load) into electrical energy, which is either returned to the source, dissipated as heat in a resistor, or counteracted by reversed supply voltage ŌĆö thereby producing a braking torque that opposes the motion.*

The fundamental principle behind all three methods is the same: **make the motor act as a generator** (or oppose the supply) so that electromagnetic torque reverses direction and decelerates the shaft.

## 1.2 The Three Standard Braking Methods

### A. Regenerative Braking (Regenerative Dynamic Braking)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Regenerative Braking is the method in which the DC motor, while still connected to the supply, is driven by the load (or by its own inertia above no-load speed) at a speed greater than its no-load speed. The back EMF (Eb) exceeds the supply voltage (V), reversing the current and returning energy to the source.*

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are cycling downhill. Gravity speeds you up beyond your pedaling speed. If you back-pedal in a geared bicycle, the chain drives the rear wheel faster than your legs, and the wheel now drives the chain (like a generator feeding back). You are essentially **pumping energy back into the system** instead of wasting it as heat.

**Key Condition:** $E_b > V$ and $N > N_0$ (operating speed exceeds ideal no-load speed)

---

### B. Dynamic Braking (Rheostatic Braking)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Dynamic Braking is the method in which the DC motor is disconnected from the supply, and the armature is connected across a braking resistor. The motor, driven by the kinetic energy of the load, now acts as a separately excited (shunt) or self-excited generator, dissipating the generated energy as heat in the resistor.*

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a treadmill with a magnetic damper. When you switch off the motor but keep the belt moving, a strong magnetic field resists the motion and the energy of motion gets converted into heat in the resistance coils. The motor becomes a **"controlled energy-dissipating generator."**

**Key Condition:** Armature is **isolated from supply** and connected to a resistor $R_B$.

---

### C. Plugging (Reverse Current Braking / Counter-current Braking)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Plugging is the method in which the armature terminals of a running motor are reversed (i.e., the supply polarity to the armature is reversed) while the field remains unchanged. The back EMF Eb and supply voltage V now add up, producing a very high reverse current that creates a strong reverse torque, rapidly decelerating the motor.*

**Intuitive Analogy:** Picture yourself walking forward at speed, and a wall suddenly appears in front. You instinctively push backward against the wall with your legs (in the direction opposite to motion) ŌĆö the wall "pushes" you backwards while you are still moving forwards, creating a sharp deceleration. Here, the **reversed supply acts like that wall**, opposing the forward motion of the armature.

**Key Condition:** Armature terminals are **reversed**; must be disconnected at near-zero speed to avoid reversal.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Value (Shunt Motor) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Back EMF Constant | $K_a$ | V┬Ęs/rad | Machine-specific |
| Flux per pole | $\Phi$ | Weber (Wb) | 0.01 ŌĆō 0.05 Wb |
| No-load speed | $N_0$ | RPM | 1000 ŌĆō 3000 |
| Armature resistance | $R_a$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | 0.1 ŌĆō 1.0 |
| Braking resistor | $R_B$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | 2 ŌĆō 10 ├Ś $R_a$ |
| Switching time | $t_s$ | seconds (s) | 0.1 ŌĆō 2.0 s |

> [!NOTE]
> All three braking methods **retain the field excitation unchanged** in a DC shunt motor ŌĆö only the **armature circuit configuration** is altered.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Back EMF Principle ŌĆö Foundation of All Braking

For any DC motor, the back EMF induced in the armature is governed by:

$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$$

The steady-state armature current is governed by Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) on the armature loop:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

$$\boxed{I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a}}$$

**Why does this matter for braking?**
The electromagnetic torque developed is $T = K_a \Phi I_a$. To **reverse the torque** (i.e., produce braking), the **sign of $I_a$ must reverse**. The three braking methods are simply three different ways of forcing $I_a$ to become negative.

---

## 2.2 Regenerative Braking ŌĆö Analytical Breakdown

**Step 1 ŌĆö Condition for regeneration:**
When the motor is overloaded or driven mechanically (e.g., descending elevator car, electric locomotive on a downhill gradient), the speed $N$ rises above the no-load speed $N_0$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Back EMF becomes dominant:**
At $N > N_0$, the induced $E_b$ exceeds the applied $V$, making $E_b - V > 0$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Current reverses:**
From the armature KVL, $I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a}$ becomes **negative** ŌĆö current flows *from the armature back into the supply*.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Torque reverses:**
Negative $I_a$ with unchanged $\Phi$ produces a negative torque, opposing the rotation. The motor now operates as a **generator feeding power back to the mains**.

> [!NOTE]
> In a **DC shunt motor**, regeneration occurs naturally when speed rises above $N_0 = V/(K_a \Phi)$. Reducing field flux (weakening) also causes $N$ to rise and can trigger regeneration.

**Speed relation during regeneration:**

$$N = \frac{V + I_a R_a}{K_a \Phi}$$

(Note the **sign change** compared to motoring: $V + I_a R_a$ instead of $V - I_a R_a$, because $I_a$ is now negative for generator action.)

---

## 2.3 Dynamic Braking ŌĆö Analytical Breakdown

**Step 1 ŌĆö Isolation from supply:**
The contactor disconnects the armature from the DC supply, and the armature terminals are switched across a braking resistor $R_B$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Machine acts as generator:**
The kinetic energy of the rotating armature (and load) maintains the rotation, so $E_b$ continues to be induced. The motor now behaves as a **separately excited DC generator** (field is still energized by the supply via the shunt winding).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Current flows through $R_B$:**
Applying KVL to the armature loop (now with $V = 0$):

$$0 = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_B)$$

$$I_a = -\frac{E_b}{R_a + R_B}$$

The negative sign indicates that current flows in the **opposite direction** to the motoring current, producing a **reverse (braking) torque**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Energy dissipation:**
The braking power $P_b = I_a^2 (R_a + R_B)$ is dissipated as heat in $R_B$. The motor decelerates exponentially as kinetic energy depletes.

---

## 2.4 Plugging ŌĆö Analytical Breakdown

**Step 1 ŌĆö Armature polarity reversal:**
The armature supply connections are swapped using a DPDT (Double Pole Double Throw) switch. The field connections remain **as is**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Back EMF and supply now add:**
With armature reversed, $E_b$ and $V$ are now in **series-aiding** (same direction in the loop). The net driving voltage becomes $V + E_b$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Very high reverse current:**
From KVL:

$$-V = E_b + I_a R_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a = \frac{-(V + E_b)}{R_a}$$

The magnitude is roughly **twice** the starting current if no extra resistance is added. Hence a **current-limiting resistor** $R_{add}$ is mandatory.

With external resistance added:

$$I_a = \frac{-(V + E_b)}{R_a + R_{add}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sharp braking torque:**
The large negative $I_a$ produces a strong reverse torque that rapidly decelerates the motor.

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL EXAMINATION POINT:** In plugging, the motor must be **disconnected from the supply the instant speed reaches zero**. Otherwise, the motor will **accelerate in the reverse direction** ŌĆö this is the principle exploited in **reversing drives**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

| # | Concept | Formula | Sign Convention | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Back EMF | $E_b = K_a \Phi N$ | Always positive (induced) | V |
| 2 | No-load speed (Shunt) | $N_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_a \Phi}$ | ŌĆö | rad/s or RPM |
| 3 | Motoring current | $I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a}$ | Positive in motoring | A |
| 4 | Regenerative braking current | $I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a}$ (negative) | Negative ŌĆö flows to source | A |
| 5 | Dynamic braking current | $I_a = -\dfrac{E_b}{R_a + R_B}$ | Negative ŌĆö dissipative | A |
| 6 | Plugging current | $I_a = -\dfrac{V + E_b}{R_a + R_{add}}$ | Strongly negative | A |
| 7 | Electromagnetic torque | $T = K_a \Phi I_a$ | Sign of $T$ = sign of $I_a$ | N┬Ęm |
| 8 | Braking power (Dynamic) | $P_b = I_a^2 (R_a + R_B)$ | Always positive (heat) | W |
| 9 | Speed during regeneration | $N = \dfrac{V + \vert I_a \vert R_a}{K_a \Phi}$ | $N > N_0$ | RPM |
| 10 | Equivalent resistance (Plug) | $R_{eq} = R_a + R_{add}$ | $R_{add} \approx (V+E_b)/I_{max}$ | $\Omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed-Torque Characteristics:** All three braking methods have a **negative slope** linear characteristic passing through specific intercepts. Regenerative braking operates in the **second quadrant** of the V-I plane, dynamic braking in the **fourth quadrant** (decelerating region).

---

## 2.6 Comparative Real-World Engineering Utility

| Braking Method | Real-World Application | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| Regenerative | Electric trains (regen on downhill), EVs, elevators descending | Energy saving ŌĆö power returned to grid |
| Dynamic | Industrial cranes, hoists holding load, DC traction holding | Smooth, controlled, no mechanical wear |
| Plugging | Planer machines, frequent-reversal press tools, small motors | Quickest stop; reversed drive capability |
