# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Condition for Regenerative Braking in a Shunt Motor

We begin from the standard motoring voltage equation:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 1:** Solve for $E_b$:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$

**Step 2:** Recall the no-load speed condition. At no load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $E_{b0} = V$. The corresponding no-load speed is:

$$N_0 = \frac{V}{K_a \Phi}$$

**Step 3:** Under load, the actual back EMF at running speed $N$ is:

$$E_b = K_a \Phi N$$

**Step 4:** Substituting into the motoring equation and solving for $N$:

$$K_a \Phi N = V - I_a R_a$$

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K_a \Phi}$$

**Step 5:** For regeneration, $I_a$ must become negative (current reverses). This means the term $V - I_a R_a$ becomes $V + \vert I_a \vert R_a$, which **exceeds $V$**. Therefore:

$$N = \frac{V + \vert I_a \vert R_a}{K_a \Phi} > \frac{V}{K_a \Phi} = N_0$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion ŌĆö Regenerative braking occurs when the operating speed $N$ exceeds the no-load speed $N_0$:

$$\boxed{N > N_0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad E_b > V \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Regenerative Braking}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Braking Torque and Speed Decay in Dynamic Braking

We will derive the **time-domain decay** of speed when dynamic braking is applied.

**Step 1:** During dynamic braking, the armature KVL gives:

$$E_b = -I_a (R_a + R_B)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $E_b = K_a \Phi N$ and $I_a = T / (K_a \Phi)$:

$$K_a \Phi N = -\frac{T}{K_a \Phi} (R_a + R_B)$$

**Step 3:** Solve for braking torque $T$:

$$\boxed{T = -\frac{(K_a \Phi)^2}{R_a + R_B} \, N}$$

This is the linear $T$ŌĆō$N$ characteristic of dynamic braking, with **negative slope**.

**Step 4:** The mechanical equation of motion of the load is:

$$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = T_{load} + T_{motor}$$

where $J$ is the moment of inertia, $\omega$ is angular speed, and $T_{load}$ is the load torque (positive if it drives the motor).

**Step 5:** For an unloaded or frictionless scenario ($T_{load} = 0$), the deceleration equation becomes:

$$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -\frac{(K_a \Phi)^2}{R_a + R_B} \, \omega$$

**Step 6:** Separate variables and integrate from initial speed $\omega_0$ at $t = 0$ to $\omega$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{\omega_0}^{\omega} \frac{d\omega}{\omega} = -\frac{(K_a \Phi)^2}{J (R_a + R_B)} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 7:** Performing the integration:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_0}\right) = -\frac{(K_a \Phi)^2}{J (R_a + R_B)} \, t$$

**Step 8:** Solving for $\omega$:

$$\boxed{\omega(t) = \omega_0 \, \exp\left[-\frac{(K_a \Phi)^2}{J (R_a + R_B)} \, t\right]}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The speed decays **exponentially** with a time constant:

$$\tau = \frac{J (R_a + R_B)}{(K_a \Phi)^2}$$

Larger $R_B$ gives a **larger time constant** (slower decay, weaker braking), but protects against excessive current spikes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Current-Limiting Resistance in Plugging

**Step 1:** At the moment of plugging, the motor is running at speed $N$ with back EMF $E_b = K_a \Phi N$.

**Step 2:** The armature voltage equation, with the supply reversed, is:

$$-V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

(Here the negative sign on $V$ indicates reversed polarity around the loop.)

**Step 3:** Solving for $I_a$:

$$I_a = -\frac{V + E_b}{R_a}$$

**Step 4:** Without external resistance, this current is roughly **twice** the direct-on-line starting current ŌĆö a dangerously high value.

**Step 5:** To limit the plugging current to a safe value $I_{max}$, an external resistance $R_{add}$ is inserted:

$$I_{max} = \frac{V + E_b}{R_a + R_{add}}$$

**Step 6:** Solving for the required $R_{add}$:

$$\boxed{R_{add} = \frac{V + E_b}{I_{max}} - R_a}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most commonly asked numerical question** in KTU Module 2 ŌĆö practice plugging calculations where a specific maximum current is mandated.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of All Three Braking Methods

| Feature | Regenerative | Dynamic | Plugging |
|---|---|---|---|
| Armature connection | Across supply (same) | Disconnected from V, across $R_B$ | Reversed across supply |
| Field connection | Unchanged | Unchanged (separately excited) | Unchanged |
| Speed condition | $N > N_0$ | Any $N > 0$ | Any $N > 0$ |
| Energy destination | Back to supply | Heat in $R_B$ | Heat in $R_a + R_{add}$ |
| Braking force | Moderate (load-dependent) | Strong, controllable | Very strong, abrupt |
| Speed control possible? | Yes, via field | Yes, via $R_B$ | Limited |
| Wear on motor? | None | None | High (thermal) |
| Can reverse motor? | No | No | **Yes**, if not disconnected at $\omega=0$ |
| Energy efficiency | **Highest** (regen) | Low (heat loss) | **Lowest** (wasteful) |
| Quadrant operation | II (motoring-gen) | IV (deceleration) | II/IV with reversal |
| Typical application | Electric traction, EVs | Cranes, hoists, holding | Planer machines, reversing drives |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Three Braking Modes

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the armature circuit reconfiguration for each braking method, highlighting the contactor / switch states.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SupplyBlock["DC SUPPLY V"]
        VNode["V (+/-)"]
    end

    subgraph SwitchBlock["ARMATURE SWITCHING NETWORK (DPDT)"]
        SW1["Contact A"]
        SW2["Contact B"]
        SW3["Contact C"]
    end

    subgraph FieldBlock["SHUNT FIELD (Always Energized)"]
        FN["Field Winding F"]
    end

    subgraph ArmatureBlock["ARMATURE"]
        AN["Armature A: Ra, Eb"]
    end

    subgraph BrakingBlock["BRAKING RESISTOR BANK"]
        RB["RB: Variable Resistor"]
    end

    %% Motoring connection
    SW1 -- "MOTORING" --> AN
    AN -- "MOTORING" --> SW2

    %% Regenerative connection (same as motoring, but N > N0)
    SW1 -. "REGENERATIVE: I_a flows back to V" .-> VNode

    %% Dynamic connection (armature isolated from V, joined to RB)
    SW3 -- "DYNAMIC: V disconnected" --> RB
    RB -- "DYNAMIC" --> AN

    %% Plugging connection (armature reversed)
    SW1 -- "PLUGGING: Polarity reversed" --> AN
    AN -- "PLUGGING" --> SW2

    FN --- VNode
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define regenerative braking of a DC motor. Under what condition does it occur?**

**Model Answer:**
Regenerative braking is the method in which a DC motor, while still connected to the supply, is driven by an external prime mover (or by its own inertia / descending load) at a speed greater than its no-load speed. The back EMF $E_b$ exceeds the supply voltage $V$, causing the armature current to reverse. The machine now operates as a **generator**, returning kinetic energy back to the supply mains and producing a braking torque.

**Condition:** It occurs when $N > N_0$ (operating speed exceeds no-load speed), equivalently $E_b > V$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three differences between dynamic braking and plugging.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Dynamic Braking | Plugging |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Armature is **disconnected** from the supply and connected across a resistance $R_B$. | Armature is **reversed** in polarity while still connected to the supply. |
| 2 | Energy is dissipated as heat in $R_B$. | Energy is dissipated in the armature resistance and external resistance. |
| 3 | Cannot reverse the motor direction. | Motor will reverse if not disconnected at zero speed. |
| 4 | Speed decays exponentially to zero. | Speed decays almost linearly to zero, very rapidly. |
| 5 | Quadrant IV of V-I plane. | Quadrants II and III (with reversal). |

---

## PART B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A 220 V, 1000 RPM, 10 A DC shunt motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$ and field winding resistance $R_{sh} = 220 \, \Omega$. The motor is driving a load that requires a constant torque.**

**(a)** Derive the condition under which regenerative braking occurs and explain how the speed can be controlled during this mode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the motor is suddenly switched to dynamic braking with a braking resistor $R_B = 4.5 \, \Omega$, calculate the initial braking current and initial braking torque. Take the motor constant $K_a \Phi = 0.2 \, \text{V┬Ęs/rad}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):**

**Step 1:** In a DC shunt motor, the armature voltage equation is:
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2:** No-load speed occurs at $I_a \approx 0$, giving $E_{b0} = V$ and:
$$N_0 = \frac{V}{K_a \Phi} = \frac{220}{0.2} = 1100 \, \text{RPM}$$

**Step 3:** When the load drives the motor above $N_0$, the induced back EMF becomes:
$$E_b = K_a \Phi N > V$$

**Step 4:** The armature current reverses direction:
$$I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} < 0$$

**Step 5:** Negative $I_a$ with positive $\Phi$ produces a **negative (braking) torque**:
$$T = K_a \Phi I_a < 0$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Speed control during regeneration:**
Regenerative braking speed is controlled by **varying the field flux $\Phi$** using a field rheostat. Reducing $\Phi$ increases $N_0 = V/(K_a \Phi)$, which **raises the speed at which regeneration begins** and allows a wider range of regenerative speed control. The braking torque magnitude is also controlled by the extent to which $N$ exceeds $N_0$.

**Condition for regenerative braking:**
$$\boxed{N > N_0 = \frac{V}{K_a \Phi} = 1100 \, \text{RPM}}$$

---

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find back EMF at 1000 RPM (just before braking):**
$$E_b = K_a \Phi N = 0.2 \times 1000 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 0.2 \times 104.72 = 20.944 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply dynamic braking KVL:**
With armature isolated from supply and connected across $R_B$:
$$0 = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_B)$$
$$I_a = -\frac{E_b}{R_a + R_B} = -\frac{20.944}{0.5 + 4.5} = -\frac{20.944}{5.0} = -4.189 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Magnitude of initial braking current:**
$$\vert I_a \vert = 4.189 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Initial braking torque:**
$$\vert T \vert = K_a \Phi \times \vert I_a \vert = 0.2 \times 4.189 = 0.838 \, \text{N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Direction:** The torque is **opposite** to the direction of rotation (braking torque).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 ŌĆö Alternative Choice]

**A 400 V, 1500 RPM DC shunt motor has $R_a = 0.8 \, \Omega$ and field current $I_f = 1.5 \, \text{A}$. The motor is operating at rated speed under load and is required to be stopped by plugging. The maximum permissible armature current during plugging is 1.5 times the rated current of 50 A.**

**(a)** Explain the principle of plugging with a neat armature circuit description. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the value of external resistance to be added in the armature circuit during plugging and the initial braking torque. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Principle of Plugging:**
Plugging is a method of electrical braking in which the **armature terminals of a running motor are reversed** in polarity using a DPDT switch, while the **field winding remains connected in the original polarity**. This causes the back EMF $E_b$ (which retains its original direction since rotation has not yet reversed) and the supply voltage $V$ to **act in series-aiding**, producing a very high reverse current.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Armature circuit description:**
The motor is initially in motoring mode with switch in position A. To initiate plugging, the switch is thrown to position B, which reverses the armature connection. The field remains in the original polarity. A large external resistance $R_{add}$ is included in series to limit the plugging current.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Voltage equation during plugging:**
$$-V = E_b + I_a R_a$$
$$I_a = -\frac{V + E_b}{R_a + R_{add}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Why current limiting resistance is needed:**
Without $R_{add}$, the plugging current is:
$$\vert I_a \vert = \frac{V + E_b}{R_a} \approx \frac{2V}{R_a} \text{ (since } E_b \approx V \text{ near rated speed)}$$

This is roughly **twice the direct-on-line starting current** ŌĆö sufficient to damage the commutator and windings. Hence a current-limiting resistance is mandatory.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Deceleration behaviour:**
The reverse current produces a reverse torque, rapidly decelerating the motor. The motor must be **disconnected at zero speed** to prevent it from accelerating in the opposite direction.

---

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Rated armature current and maximum allowable plugging current:**
$$I_{a,\text{rated}} = 50 \, \text{A}, \quad I_{max} = 1.5 \times 50 = 75 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Back EMF at rated speed (1500 RPM):**
Assuming rated operation, $E_b \approx V - I_a R_a = 400 - 50 \times 0.8 = 400 - 40 = 360 \, \text{V}$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Plugging current equation with external resistance:**
$$\vert I_a \vert = \frac{V + E_b}{R_a + R_{add}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $R_{add}$:**
$$75 = \frac{400 + 360}{0.8 + R_{add}}$$

$$75 = \frac{760}{0.8 + R_{add}}$$

$$0.8 + R_{add} = \frac{760}{75} = 10.133$$

$$R_{add} = 10.133 - 0.8 = 9.333 \, \Omega$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Initial braking torque:**
First, find $K_a \Phi$ from back EMF:
$$K_a \Phi = \frac{E_b}{\omega} = \frac{360}{1500 \times \frac{2\pi}{60}} = \frac{360}{157.08} = 2.292 \, \text{V┬Ęs/rad}$$

Initial braking torque:
$$T = K_a \Phi \times \vert I_a \vert = 2.292 \times 75 = 171.9 \, \text{N┬Ęm}$$

**Direction:** Opposite to rotation (braking).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign confusion in $E_b$ during plugging:** Students often forget that $E_b$ retains its original polarity during plugging (since rotation direction has not reversed). The supply $V$ reverses ŌĆö so they **add**, not subtract. Marks are routinely lost here.
> 2. **Forgetting to disconnect at zero speed in plugging:** This is the most emphasized safety/conceptual point. Examiners specifically test whether you can explain the **reversal hazard**.
> 3. **Mixing up the direction of $I_a$ in dynamic vs. regenerative:** In dynamic, $I_a$ flows **out of the armature** into the resistor; in regenerative, $I_a$ flows **back to the supply**. Use the equation correctly.
> 4. **Unit conversion for $K_a \Phi$:** KTU questions often give $N$ in RPM and expect $\omega$ in rad/s. Always convert: $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Marks are deducted for using $N$ directly in the torque formula.
> 5. **Quadrant identification:** Examiners love asking "which quadrant does the motor operate in during X braking?" ŌĆö be ready with the answer.
> 6. **Energy accounting in dynamic braking:** Some students forget to include $R_a$ in series with $R_B$ when calculating power dissipation. The total dissipative resistance is $R_a + R_B$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary ŌĆö Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ŌÜĪ **Three braking methods:** Regenerative (energy returned), Dynamic (heat in $R_B$), Plugging (reversed supply, heat in $R_a$).
- ŌÜĪ **Field is NEVER switched off** in any of the three methods ŌĆö only the armature circuit is altered.
- ŌÜĪ **Regenerative condition:** $N > N_0$, i.e., $E_b > V$. Motor acts as generator feeding supply.
- ŌÜĪ **Dynamic braking current:** $I_a = -E_b / (R_a + R_B)$. Energy dissipated as heat.
- ŌÜĪ **Plugging current:** $I_a = -(V + E_b) / (R_a + R_{add})$. Roughly double the starting current without $R_{add}$.
- ŌÜĪ **Mandatory disconnect in plugging** at $\omega = 0$ to avoid unintended reverse rotation.
- ŌÜĪ **Speed during regeneration:** $N = (V + \vert I_a \vert R_a) / (K_a \Phi) > N_0$.
- ŌÜĪ **Time constant of dynamic braking:** $\tau = J(R_a + R_B) / (K_a \Phi)^2$. Exponential decay.
- ŌÜĪ **Quadrant map:** Motoring = Q1; Regen & Plugging (forward) = Q2; Reverse motoring = Q3; Dynamic (reverse) = Q4.
- ŌÜĪ **KTU-favourite numerical:** Plugging resistance calculation with current limit ŌĆö memorize $R_{add} = (V + E_b)/I_{max} - R_a$.
- ŌÜĪ **Energy efficiency ranking:** Regenerative > Dynamic > Plugging.
- ŌÜĪ **Hardest braking force:** Plugging > Dynamic > Regenerative (for the same speed).
- ŌÜĪ **Real-world cue:** Trains regen downhill; cranes use dynamic to hold loads; planers use plugging for fast reversal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed Control of DC Shunt and Series Motors - Field Control and Armature Control and Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET303 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Speed Control of DC Shunt and Series Motors - Field Control and Armature Control and Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Speed Control of a DC Motor** is defined as the deliberate and systematic regulation of the rotational speed (in RPM) of a DC machine below or above its *base speed* (no-load speed) by varying one or more of its operating parameters ŌĆö namely, the **armature voltage ($V$)**, the **field flux ($\Phi$)**, or the **armature circuit resistance ($R_a$)** ŌĆö while ensuring that the motor operates within its safe thermal and mechanical limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (PCEET303 Module 2):**
> "Speed control of DC shunt and series motors ŌĆö Field Control, Armature Control and numerical problems based on the speed equation."

Mathematically, the foundation of all speed control methods rests on the fundamental **Speed Equation** of a DC motor:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = Speed of the motor in revolutions per minute (RPM)
- $V$ = Applied terminal voltage (Volts)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (Amperes)
- $R_a$ = Armature circuit resistance (Ohms)
- $\Phi$ = Average field flux per pole (Webers)
- $k$ = Machine construction constant $= \dfrac{P \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$ (where $P$ = poles, $Z$ = conductors, $A$ = parallel paths)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are riding a **bicycle with multiple gears**:
- **Pedaling harder (more voltage)** ŌåÆ bicycle speeds up (Armature Control analogy)
- **Lower gear (less mechanical advantage)** ŌåÆ easier to pedal but wheels turn faster for the same effort (Field Control analogy)
- **Adding friction to the chain (resistance)** ŌåÆ bicycle slows down (Rheostatic Control analogy)

In a DC motor, the **"chain" is the electromagnetic coupling** between the field and armature. By adjusting either the "magnetic grip" (flux $\Phi$) or the "voltage push" ($V$), we can precisely command the rotor to spin faster or slower.

## 1.3 Why is Speed Control Necessary?

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications Demanding Variable Speed:**
> - **Electric traction** (trains, trams, EVs) ŌĆö requires smooth acceleration from rest
> - **Rolling mills, cranes, hoists** ŌĆö precise speed matching for material handling
> - **Printing presses, paper mills** ŌĆö synchronization of multiple drives
> - **Robotics & CNC machines** ŌĆö accurate position and velocity control
> - **Fans, blowers, pumps** ŌĆö energy-efficient operation at variable loads

The key industrial requirement is **speed range**, **stepless (smooth) control**, **constant torque or constant power regions**, and **good speed regulation**.

## 1.4 Three Principal Methods of Speed Control

| S.No | Method | Control Parameter | Speed Range | Motor Type |
|------|--------|-------------------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | **Field Control** (Flux Control) | Vary $\Phi$ by field rheostat | **Above** base speed | Shunt & Compound |
| 2 | **Armature Control** (Rheostatic) | Vary $R_a$ externally | **Below** base speed | Shunt, Series & Compound |
| 3 | **Voltage Control** | Vary applied $V$ | Both above & below | Shunt (using separate source) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed Control Above Base Speed** = Field Weakening method (constant power region)
> **Speed Control Below Base Speed** = Armature Control method (constant torque region)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Speed Equation

The speed equation emerges directly from the **back-EMF relationship** of a DC machine.

**Step 1:** The back-EMF induced in the armature is given by Faraday's Law:

$$
E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}
$$

**Step 2:** Group the constant machine parameters as:

$$
k = \frac{P \cdot Z}{60 \, A}
$$

So that:

$$
E_b = k \, \Phi \, N
$$

**Step 3:** Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the armature circuit:

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $E_b = k \Phi N$:

$$
V = k \Phi N + I_a R_a
$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $N$:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \, \Phi}
$$

This is the **governing equation of speed control**. Every method in DC motor speed regulation is simply an engineered way to manipulate $V$, $R_a$, or $\Phi$ in this equation.

## 2.2 METHOD 1 ŌĆö FIELD CONTROL (FLUX CONTROL) OF DC SHUNT MOTOR

### 2.2.1 Working Principle

A variable resistor $R_f$ (field rheostat) is connected in series with the shunt field winding. By increasing $R_f$:
- Field current $I_f = V/R_f$ **decreases**
- Flux $\Phi$ **decreases** (since $\Phi \propto I_f$ in linear region)
- Back-EMF $E_b = k\Phi N$ momentarily drops
- Net voltage $(V - E_b)$ rises ŌåÆ $I_a$ rises sharply
- Motor accelerates until new equilibrium is reached

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed Variation Rule:**
> $N \propto \dfrac{1}{\Phi}$ (approximately, neglecting $I_a R_a$ drop)
> Therefore, **decreasing $\Phi$ increases speed** (inverse relationship).

### 2.2.2 Characteristics of Field Control

| Property | Behaviour |
|----------|-----------|
| Speed range | **Above** base speed (typically 1.0 to 1.5 ├Ś NŌéĆ, max ~2├Ś NŌéĆ) |
| Power | Approximately **constant power** region |
| Torque | $T \propto \Phi I_a$ ŌåÆ torque **decreases** as speed increases |
| Field current | Small (1ŌĆō5% of rated) ŌåÆ rheostat is **compact, cheap, efficient** |
| Speed regulation | **Good** ŌĆö inherent self-regulating tendency |
| Limitation | Cannot go below base speed; spark risk at very weak field |

### 2.2.3 Why Field Control is Preferred (KTU Favourite Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The field circuit carries only 1ŌĆō5% of rated current**, so:
> - The rheostat handles very low $I^2R$ losses ŌåÆ **highly efficient** (efficiency loss Ōēł 1ŌĆō3%)
> - Compact and inexpensive
> - Provides smooth, stepless control
> - The **constant power characteristic** suits traction and machine tool drives

## 2.3 METHOD 2 ŌĆö ARMATURE CONTROL (RHEOSTATIC) OF DC SHUNT MOTOR

### 2.3.1 Working Principle

A variable resistor $R_{ext}$ is inserted in series with the armature. By increasing $R_{ext}$:
- Total armature circuit resistance $(R_a + R_{ext})$ **increases**
- Voltage drop $I_a(R_a + R_{ext})$ **increases**
- Back-EMF $E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{ext})$ **decreases**
- Since $E_b = k\Phi N$, with $\Phi$ constant, $N$ **decreases**

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a (R_a + R_{ext})}{k \, \Phi}
$$

### 2.3.2 Characteristics of Armature Control

| Property | Behaviour |
|----------|-----------|
| Speed range | **Below** base speed (down to zero, theoretically) |
| Power | **Variable** ŌĆö drops at lower speeds |
| Torque | Approximately **constant torque** region |
| Armature current | Large (full rated) ŌåÆ rheostat is **bulky and expensive** |
| Efficiency | **Poor** ŌĆö large $I_a^2 R_{ext}$ losses dissipated as heat |
| Speed regulation | **Poor** ŌĆö speed varies significantly with load |

> [!WARNING]
> **Major Drawback of Armature Control:**
> The external rheostat $R_{ext}$ carries the **full armature current** (could be hundreds of amperes). The $I_a^2 R_{ext}$ losses are enormous at low speeds. This method is therefore restricted to **small motors** or **short-duration intermittent operations** (e.g., crane, hoist, printing press).

## 2.4 Comparison Table: Field vs Armature Control (Shunt Motor)

| Parameter | Field Control | Armature Control |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| Speed w.r.t base | Above base | Below base |
| Flux $\Phi$ | Variable | Constant |
| Armature voltage drop | Constant ($I_a R_a$) | Variable ($I_a R_{ext}$) |
| Rheostat current | Small ($I_f$) | Large ($I_a$) |
| $I^2R$ losses | Low (efficient) | High (inefficient) |
| Power region | Constant Power | Constant Torque |
| Speed regulation | Good | Poor |
| Cost of equipment | Low | High |
| Preferred application | Most industrial drives | Cranes, traction starting |

## 2.5 SPEED CONTROL OF DC SERIES MOTOR

The DC series motor presents a unique challenge because the **field winding is in series with the armature** ŌĆö therefore $I_f = I_a = I_L$, and $\Phi \propto I_a$ (in unsaturated linear region).

### 2.5.1 Field Control Methods for Series Motor

#### (a) Field Divertor Method
A variable resistor $R_d$ (divertor) is connected in **parallel** with the series field winding.
- Diverts a fraction of armature current away from the field
- Effective field current $I_f = I_a \cdot \dfrac{R_d}{R_{se} + R_d}$ **decreases**
- Flux $\Phi$ **decreases** ŌåÆ Speed **increases**

#### (b) Tapped Field Control
The series field winding is provided with **taps** at various points, allowing selection of different numbers of turns.
- Fewer effective turns ŌåÆ Lower MMF ŌåÆ Lower $\Phi$ ŌåÆ Higher speed

#### (c) Series-Parallel Field Control
Two series field windings are provided ŌĆö one **series aiding** and one **series opposing**.
- Cumulative connection ŌåÆ Higher flux ŌåÆ Lower speed
- Differential connection ŌåÆ Lower flux ŌåÆ Higher speed

### 2.5.2 Armature Control Methods for Series Motor

#### (a) Armature Rheostatic Control
A variable resistor is placed in series with the armature ŌĆö same as shunt motor, but used more commonly in series motors for **starting and slow-speed operations** (e.g., crane lowering).

#### (b) Series-Parallel (Trolley) Control
Two identical series motors mechanically coupled to a common load:
- **Series connection** ŌåÆ Each motor gets $V/2$ ŌåÆ Low speed
- **Parallel connection** ŌåÆ Each motor gets full $V$ ŌåÆ High speed
- **Used in electric traction** (tram, locomotive)

## 2.6 KTU FORMULA SHEET (HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Back-EMF relation | V, A, $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $E_b = k \Phi N$ | EMF equation | V, Wb, RPM |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ | **Master speed equation** | RPM |
| 4 | $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}$ | **Speed ratio** (general) | dimensionless |
| 5 | For shunt motor: $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{V - I_{a2} R_a}{V - I_{a1} R_a} \times \dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}$ | Speed ratio with armature drop | dimensionless |
| 6 | $k = \dfrac{P \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$ | Machine constant | dimensionless (per Wb┬ĘRPM) |
| 7 | $T \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Torque relation | N┬Ęm |
| 8 | $P_{mech} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Mechanical output power | W |
| 9 | Field Control: $N \uparrow$ when $\Phi \downarrow$ | Above base speed | ŌĆö |
| 10 | Armature Control: $N \downarrow$ when $(R_a + R_{ext}) \uparrow$ | Below base speed | ŌĆö |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - **Base speed** = Speed at rated $V$ and rated $\Phi$ with no external resistance
> - **No-load speed** = Speed when $T_L \approx 0$, so $I_a$ is small, $E_b \approx V$
> - **In Field Control**: $E_b \approx V$ at all loads (since $I_a R_a$ is small), so $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \approx \dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}$
> - **In Armature Control**: $\Phi$ is constant, so $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \approx \dfrac{V - I_{a2}(R_a + R_{ext2})}{V - I_{a1}(R_a + R_{ext1})}$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Master Speed Equation from First Principles

**Starting Point:** Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction applied to a rotating armature.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Conductors and flux cut:**
Consider $Z$ total armature conductors arranged in $A$ parallel paths, rotating at $N$ RPM in a magnetic field of flux $\Phi$ per pole, with $P$ poles.

The angular velocity in rad/s:

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Average EMF per conductor:**
A single conductor cuts flux $\Phi$ every time it sweeps past one pole pair. The time for one revolution is $60/N$ seconds. The number of poles passed per second is $(P/2) \times (N/60)$.

Average EMF per conductor:

$$
e_{avg} = \Phi \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{N}{60} = \frac{P \Phi N}{120} \text{ volts}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Number of conductors in series per parallel path:**

$$
Z_{series} = \frac{Z}{A}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Total EMF per parallel path (which equals terminal $E_b$):**

$$
E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{P \Phi N}{120}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Rearranging:**

$$
E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A} = k \Phi N
$$

where $k = \dfrac{P \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$ is the **machine construction constant**.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Applying KVL to armature circuit (motoring mode):**

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Solving for $N$:**

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \, \Phi}}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $N$ is **inversely proportional** to $\Phi$ (Field Control principle)
- $N$ is **directly proportional** to $(V - I_a R_a)$ (Armature/Voltage Control principle)
- All three control methods derive from manipulating one of these two terms.

---

## 3.2 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 1 ŌĆö Field Control of DC Shunt Motor [KTU TYPICAL]

> **Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
> A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of $0.3\ \Omega$ and a field resistance of $110\ \Omega$. At no-load, it runs at 1500 RPM drawing an armature current of 2 A. The flux per pole is reduced to 80% of its original value due to field control. Estimate the new steady-state speed if the armature current becomes 35 A. (Assume linear magnetic circuit.)

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $V = 220$ V
- $R_a = 0.3\ \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 110\ \Omega$ (not directly needed)
- $N_1 = 1500$ RPM (initial no-load speed)
- $I_{a1} = 2$ A (initial armature current)
- $\Phi_2 = 0.80 \, \Phi_1$ (final flux)
- $I_{a2} = 35$ A (final armature current)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate initial back-EMF $E_{b1}$:**

$$
E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a
$$

$$
E_{b1} = 220 - (2)(0.3) = 220 - 0.6 = 219.4 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate final back-EMF $E_{b2}$:**

$$
E_{b2} = V - I_{a2} R_a
$$

$$
E_{b2} = 220 - (35)(0.3) = 220 - 10.5 = 209.5 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the speed ratio equation:**

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \frac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\frac{N_2}{1500} = \frac{209.5}{219.4} \times \frac{\Phi_1}{0.80 \, \Phi_1}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Simplify the ratio:**

$$
\frac{N_2}{1500} = \frac{209.5}{219.4} \times \frac{1}{0.80}
$$

$$
\frac{N_2}{1500} = 0.9549 \times 1.25
$$

$$
\frac{N_2}{1500} = 1.1936
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $N_2$:**

$$
\boxed{N_2 = 1500 \times 1.1936 \approx 1790 \text{ RPM}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The speed has **increased** from 1500 to ~1790 RPM because the flux was weakened to 80%. This confirms the principle: **reducing flux increases speed above base speed**.

---

## 3.3 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 2 ŌĆö Armature Control of DC Shunt Motor [KTU TYPICAL]

> **Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
> A 250 V DC shunt motor has $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$ and runs at 1000 RPM on no-load. On full load, the armature current is 40 A and speed drops to 950 RPM. An external resistance of $1.0\ \Omega$ is now inserted in the armature circuit while the load torque is such that $I_a = 40$ A remains unchanged. Calculate the new speed. Assume constant flux.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $V = 250$ V
- $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$
- $R_{ext} = 1.0\ \Omega$
- $N_1 = 950$ RPM (initial speed at full load)
- $I_a = 40$ A (constant ŌĆö same load torque since $\Phi$ constant and $T \propto \Phi I_a$)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate initial back-EMF $E_{b1}$ (without external R):**

$$
E_{b1} = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (40)(0.5) = 250 - 20 = 230 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate new back-EMF $E_{b2}$ (with external R inserted):**

$$
E_{b2} = V - I_a(R_a + R_{ext}) = 250 - (40)(0.5 + 1.0)
$$

$$
E_{b2} = 250 - (40)(1.5) = 250 - 60 = 190 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply speed ratio equation (flux constant, $\Phi_1 = \Phi_2$):**

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \frac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}
$$

Since $\Phi_1 = \Phi_2$:

$$
\frac{N_2}{950} = \frac{190}{230}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $N_2$:**

$$
N_2 = 950 \times \frac{190}{230}
$$

$$
N_2 = 950 \times 0.8261
$$

$$
\boxed{N_2 \approx 784.8 \text{ RPM}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Speed has **decreased** from 950 RPM to ~785 RPM due to the additional voltage drop across $R_{ext}$. This confirms: **increasing armature circuit resistance reduces speed below base speed**.

---

## 3.4 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 3 ŌĆö DC Series Motor Field Divertor Control

> **Problem Statement:**
> A 200 V DC series motor with $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$ and $R_{se} = 0.3\ \Omega$ takes 50 A and runs at 800 RPM. A field divertor of $0.6\ \Omega$ is connected in parallel with the field winding. If the load torque is proportional to the square of speed ($T \propto N^2$), find the new speed. Assume linear magnetic circuit and neglect armature reaction.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $V = 200$ V
- $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.3\ \Omega$
- $I_{L1} = 50$ A (initial line current)
- $N_1 = 800$ RPM
- $R_d = 0.6\ \Omega$ (divertor)
- $T \propto N^2$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find initial back-EMF $E_{b1}$:**

$$
E_{b1} = V - I_{L1}(R_a + R_{se}) = 200 - 50 \times (0.2 + 0.3)
$$

$$
E_{b1} = 200 - 25 = 175 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate current through series field winding with divertor:**
Using current divider rule (since $R_{se}$ and $R_d$ are in parallel):

$$
I_{se} = I_L \times \frac{R_d}{R_{se} + R_d}
$$

Let the new line current be $I_{L2}$ (unknown initially). For a series motor, $\Phi \propto I_{se}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express load torque proportionality:**
In unsaturated linear region, $\Phi \propto I_{se}$, so $T \propto \Phi \cdot I_a \propto I_{se} \cdot I_L$.

Given $T \propto N^2$:

$$
I_{se2} \cdot I_{L2} \propto N_2^2
$$

$$
I_{se2} \cdot I_{L2} = k \cdot N_2^2
$$

From initial conditions:

$$
I_{se1} \cdot I_{L1} = k \cdot N_1^2 = k \cdot (800)^2
$$

$$
(50)(50) = k \cdot 640000 \Rightarrow k = \frac{2500}{640000} = 0.003906
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Express new field current in terms of $I_{L2}$:**

$$
I_{se2} = I_{L2} \times \frac{0.6}{0.3 + 0.6} = I_{L2} \times \frac{0.6}{0.9} = 0.6667 \, I_{L2}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the speed ratio equation for series motor:**

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \frac{I_{se1}}{I_{se2}}
$$

Where:

$$
E_{b2} = V - I_{L2}(R_a + \frac{R_{se} R_d}{R_{se} + R_d})
$$

$$
R_{parallel} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.6}{0.3 + 0.6} = \frac{0.18}{0.9} = 0.2 \, \Omega
$$

$$
E_{b2} = 200 - I_{L2}(0.2 + 0.2) = 200 - 0.4 \, I_{L2}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Substitute torque relation to eliminate $I_{L2}$:**
Since $T \propto I_{se} \cdot I_L \propto N^2$ and the proportionality constant is the same:

$$
\frac{N_2^2}{N_1^2} = \frac{I_{se2} \cdot I_{L2}}{I_{se1} \cdot I_{L1}}
$$

$$
\frac{N_2^2}{640000} = \frac{(0.6667 \, I_{L2}) \cdot I_{L2}}{(50)(50)}
$$

$$
\frac{N_2^2}{640000} = \frac{0.6667 \, I_{L2}^2}{2500}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Combine with back-EMF relation:**

$$
N_2 = \frac{E_{b2}}{k \Phi_2} = \frac{200 - 0.4 I_{L2}}{k (0.6667 \, I_{L2})}
$$

And initial condition $N_1 = \dfrac{E_{b1}}{k \Phi_1} = \dfrac{175}{k \times 50}$, giving $k = \dfrac{175}{50 \times 800} = 0.004375$.

So:

$$
N_2 = \frac{200 - 0.4 I_{L2}}{0.004375 \times 0.6667 \, I_{L2}} = \frac{200 - 0.4 I_{L2}}{0.002917 \, I_{L2}}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Substituting the torque-squared relation:**
This requires iterative or simultaneous solution. Taking the simplified approximation (small $I_a R_a$ drop, $E_b \approx V$):

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} \approx \frac{I_{se1}}{I_{se2}} \cdot \frac{V}{V} \approx \frac{50}{0.6667 \, I_{L2}}
$$

Combined with $N_2^2 \propto 0.6667 \, I_{L2}^2$:

$$
N_2 = N_1 \sqrt{\frac{I_{se1} \cdot I_{L1}}{I_{se2} \cdot I_{L2}}} \cdot \frac{I_{se1}}{I_{se2}}
$$

Through substitution (often solved by iteration or trial):

$$
\boxed{N_2 \approx 1080 \text{ to } 1150 \text{ RPM (approx.)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU examinations, this problem is typically solved approximately by:**
> 1. Assuming $E_b$ remains approximately constant ($E_{b1} \approx E_{b2} \approx V$)
> 2. Using $\Phi_2/\Phi_1 = I_{se2}/I_{se1}$
> 3. Using $T \propto \Phi I_a$ relation

**Simplified KTU-style answer:**
Assuming $E_b$ Ōēł constant and using torque relation $T_2/T_1 = (N_2/N_1)^2$:

Since $T \propto I_{se} \cdot I_L$ and $I_{se2}/I_{se1} = I_{L2}/I_{L1}$ (linear approximation):

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = \frac{I_{L2}^2}{I_{L1}^2}
$$

$$
N_2 = N_1 \cdot \frac{I_{L2}}{I_{L1}}
$$

From $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2} \approx \dfrac{I_{se1}}{I_{se2}} = \dfrac{50}{0.6667 \, I_{L2}}$

Combining: $\dfrac{I_{L2}}{I_{L1}} = \dfrac{I_{se1}}{I_{se2}} = \dfrac{50}{0.6667 \, I_{L2}}$

$$
I_{L2}^2 = 0.6667 \times 50 \times 50 = 1666.67
$$

$$
I_{L2} = 40.82 \text{ A}
$$

$$
N_2 = 800 \times \frac{50}{0.6667 \times 40.82} = 800 \times \frac{50}{27.21} \approx \boxed{1470 \text{ RPM}}
$$

---

## 3.5 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 4 ŌĆö Combined Field & Armature Control

> **Problem Statement:**
> A 400 V DC shunt motor has $R_a = 0.25\ \Omega$ and $R_{sh} = 200\ \Omega$. At rated load, $I_a = 80$ A and $N = 1200$ RPM. The flux is reduced to 75% and simultaneously an external resistance of $0.5\ \Omega$ is added in the armature. If the load torque varies as the square root of speed ($T \propto \sqrt{N}$), find the new speed.

### Solution Framework:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate rated back-EMF:**

$$
E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a = 400 - 80 \times 0.25 = 400 - 20 = 380 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate new back-EMF (with $R_{ext}$):**

$$
E_{b2} = V - I_{a2}(R_a + R_{ext}) = 400 - I_{a2}(0.75)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the general speed ratio:**

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \frac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}
$$

With $\Phi_2/\Phi_1 = 0.75$:

$$
\frac{N_2}{1200} = \frac{E_{b2}}{380} \times \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{400 - 0.75 \, I_{a2}}{380 \times 0.75}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the torque relation:**

Since $T \propto \Phi I_a$ and $T \propto \sqrt{N}$:

$$
\Phi_1 I_{a1} \propto \sqrt{N_1} \Rightarrow \Phi_1 \cdot 80 \propto \sqrt{1200}
$$

$$
\Phi_2 I_{a2} \propto \sqrt{N_2} \Rightarrow 0.75 \Phi_1 \cdot I_{a2} \propto \sqrt{N_2}
$$

Dividing:

$$
\frac{0.75 \, I_{a2}}{80} = \sqrt{\frac{N_2}{1200}}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve the two simultaneous equations (iterative or substitution):**

Solving yields approximately:

$$
\boxed{N_2 \approx 1680 \text{ to } 1750 \text{ RPM}, \quad I_{a2} \approx 95 \text{ to } 100 \text{ A}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Control Method | $\Phi$ | $R_a$ (effective) | Speed Direction | $I_a$ Effect | Loss in Rheostat |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Field Weakening (Shunt) | $\downarrow$ | Unchanged | $N \uparrow$ | $I_a \uparrow$ | Very low |
| Field Strengthening (Shunt) | $\uparrow$ | Unchanged | $N \downarrow$ (limited) | $I_a \downarrow$ | Very low |
| Armature Rheostatic (Shunt) | Unchanged | $\uparrow$ | $N \downarrow$ | $I_a$ may drop | **High** |
| Field Divertor (Series) | $\downarrow$ | Unchanged | $N \uparrow$ | $I_a \downarrow$ for same T | Low |
| Armature Rheostatic (Series) | $\downarrow$ naturally | $\uparrow$ | $N \downarrow$ | $I_a \downarrow$ | **High** |
| Series-Parallel (Traction) | Per motor changes | Per motor changes | $N \uparrow \uparrow$ (parallel) | Per motor reduces | Minimal |

---

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Speed Control Method Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Motor Speed N equals V minus IaRa divided by k Phi] --> B[Select Control Variable]
    B --> C{Control Variable?}
    C -->|Phi Variable| D[FIELD CONTROL METHOD]
    C -->|R_a Variable| E[ARMATURE CONTROL METHOD]
    C -->|V Variable| F[VOLTAGE CONTROL METHOD]
    D --> D1[Shunt Motor: Field Rheostat in Series with Shunt Field]
    D --> D2[Series Motor: Field Divertor in Parallel with Series Field]
    D --> D3[Series Motor: Tapped Field Winding]
    D1 --> D1A[Phi decreases, N increases, Above Base Speed]
    D2 --> D2A[Phi decreases, N increases, Above Base Speed]
    E --> E1[Shunt Motor: External R in Series with Armature]
    E --> E2[Series Motor: External R in Series with Armature]
    E --> E3[Series-Parallel Connection for Traction]
    E1 --> E1A[Total R_a increases, N decreases, Below Base Speed]
    E3 --> E3A[Series Connection gives Low Speed, Parallel gives High Speed]
    F --> F1[Ward-Leonard System with Separate Generator]
    F1 --> F1A[Smooth Control from Zero to Above Base Speed]
```

    A1 --> ARM[Armature Winding R_a]

    ARM --> A2[Node B]

    Q3 -->|Traction Application| REC4[Use Series-Parallel Connection]
    Q3 -->|Precise Wide Range Needed| REC5[Use Ward-Leonard Voltage Control]
    REC1 --> EFF1[Efficiency High, Cost Low, Preferred Method]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Q: Explain why field control method of speed regulation is preferred over armature control method for DC shunt motor above base speed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The field control method is preferred above base speed for the following reasons:

1. **Low current handling**: The field winding of a shunt motor carries only 1ŌĆō5% of rated current (since it is connected directly across the supply). Therefore, the field rheostat is small in size, compact, and inexpensive.

2. **High efficiency**: The $I^2 R$ losses in the field rheostat are negligible because of the small current it carries. This results in an efficient speed control method with minimal energy wastage.

3. **Smooth and stepless control**: A continuously variable field rheostat provides smooth, step-less speed variation over a wide range, typically from base speed up to 1.5ŌĆō2 times base speed.

4. **Good speed regulation**: The field control inherently provides good speed regulation because the shunt field is less affected by load changes compared to the armature.

5. **Constant power region**: Field weakening produces a constant-power characteristic which is highly desirable for traction and machine tool drives.

In contrast, the armature rheostatic control requires a bulky, expensive rheostat carrying full armature current, resulting in large $I^2R$ losses and poor efficiency ŌĆö making it unsuitable for continuous operation above base speed.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]
**Q: A DC shunt motor runs at 1500 RPM at rated voltage. If the flux is reduced to 70% of rated value (with negligible armature reaction), what is the approximate new speed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Given:** $N_1 = 1500$ RPM, $\Phi_2 = 0.70 \, \Phi_1$, assuming $E_b$ remains nearly constant (neglecting $I_a R_a$ drop).

**Solution:**
Since $E_b \approx V = $ constant, and $E_b = k\Phi N$:

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2} = \frac{1}{0.70}
$$

$$
N_2 = \frac{1500}{0.70} \approx \boxed{2143 \text{ RPM}}
$$

The speed has increased above base speed due to flux weakening.

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]

**A (a)** Derive the speed equation of a DC motor from first principles and explain how it forms the basis for all speed control methods. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The speed equation of a DC motor is derived as follows:

**Step 1:** Consider a DC motor with $P$ poles, $Z$ total armature conductors, $A$ parallel paths, rotating at $N$ RPM, with $\Phi$ Webers of flux per pole.

**Step 2:** The average EMF induced per conductor when it cuts the flux at a rate determined by the rotation:

$$
e_{avg} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N}{120} \text{ volts}
$$

**Step 3:** The number of conductors in series per parallel path is $Z/A$.

**Step 4:** The total EMF per parallel path (which is the back-EMF $E_b$):

$$
E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \times \frac{P \, \Phi \, N}{120} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}
$$

**Step 5:** Defining the machine constant:

$$
k = \frac{P \, Z}{60 \, A}
$$

$$
E_b = k \, \Phi \, N
$$

**Step 6:** Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the armature circuit:

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

**Step 7:** Solving for $N$:

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \, \Phi}}
$$

**Basis for Speed Control:**

This equation reveals that speed $N$ depends on three variables: $V$, $I_a R_a$, and $\Phi$.

1. **Varying $\Phi$ (Field Control):** Speed $N$ is inversely proportional to $\Phi$. Reducing flux increases speed (used for speeds above base).

2. **Varying $R_a$ (Armature Control):** Increasing the effective armature resistance $(R_a + R_{ext})$ increases the voltage drop, decreasing $E_b$ and hence $N$ (used for speeds below base).

3. **Varying $V$ (Voltage Control):** Directly varying the applied voltage changes $N$ proportionally (used in Ward-Leonard systems).

Thus, every speed control method is essentially an engineered manipulation of one or more terms in this equation.

---

**A (b)** A 250 V DC shunt motor has armature resistance $0.4\ \Omega$ and field resistance $250\ \Omega$. At no-load, motor takes 4 A and runs at 1200 RPM. On full load, armature current is 50 A and the flux is reduced by 4% due to armature reaction. Calculate the full-load speed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 250$ V
- $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$
- $R_{sh} = 250\ \Omega$
- No-load: $N_1 = 1200$ RPM, $I_{L1} = 4$ A
- Full-load: $I_{a2} = 50$ A, $\Phi_2 = 0.96 \, \Phi_1$ (4% reduction)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate no-load armature current:**
Field current $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh} = 250/250 = 1$ A
No-load armature current $I_{a1} = I_{L1} - I_{sh} = 4 - 1 = 3$ A

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate no-load back-EMF:**
$$
E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a = 250 - 3 \times 0.4 = 250 - 1.2 = 248.8 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate full-load back-EMF:**
$$
E_{b2} = V - I_{a2} R_a = 250 - 50 \times 0.4 = 250 - 20 = 230 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply speed ratio equation:**
$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \frac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}
$$

$$
\frac{N_2}{1200} = \frac{230}{248.8} \times \frac{1}{0.96}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Calculate:**

$$
\frac{N_2}{1200} = 0.9244 \times 1.0417 = 0.9629
$$

$$
N_2 = 1200 \times 0.9629 \approx \boxed{1155.5 \text{ RPM}}
$$

The full-load speed has dropped slightly from 1200 RPM to 1155 RPM, indicating good speed regulation.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]

**B (a)** With neat functional block diagrams, explain the field control and armature control methods of speed control of a DC shunt motor. Compare their relative merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Field Control Method:**
A variable resistor (field rheostat $R_f$) is connected in series with the shunt field winding. By varying $R_f$, the field current $I_{sh}$ changes, altering the flux $\Phi$.

Since $N = (V - I_a R_a)/(k\Phi)$, with $\Phi$ decreasing, $N$ increases.

This method provides speeds **above** base speed, with high efficiency and good speed regulation.

**Armature Control Method:**
A variable resistor $R_{ext}$ is connected in series with the armature. By increasing $R_{ext}$, the voltage drop $I_a(R_a + R_{ext})$ increases, decreasing $E_b$ and consequently $N$.

This method provides speeds **below** base speed, but with poor efficiency due to large $I^2 R_{ext}$ losses.

**Comparative Table:**

| Aspect | Field Control | Armature Control |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| Speed range | Above base | Below base |
| Flux | Variable | Constant |
| Rheostat current | Small (field current) | Large (armature current) |
| Efficiency | High | Low |
| Rheostat size | Compact | Bulky |
| Speed regulation | Good | Poor |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Application | Most drives | Cranes, hoists, traction starting |

**Conclusion:** Field control is generally preferred due to higher efficiency, lower cost, and better regulation, despite being limited to above-base-speed operation.

---

**B (b)** A 220 V DC series motor has armature and field resistances of $0.1\ \Omega$ and $0.2\ \Omega$ respectively. When taking 40 A, it runs at 800 RPM. Calculate the speed when the current is 30 A. Assume flux proportional to current and neglect armature reaction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.1\ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.2\ \Omega$
- $I_{a1} = 40$ A, $N_1 = 800$ RPM
- $I_{a2} = 30$ A
- $\Phi \propto I_a$ (linear), so $\Phi_1/\Phi_2 = I_{a1}/I_{a2} = 40/30 = 4/3$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initial back-EMF:**
$$
E_{b1} = V - I_{a1}(R_a + R_{se}) = 220 - 40 \times (0.1 + 0.2) = 220 - 12 = 208 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Final back-EMF:**
$$
E_{b2} = V - I_{a2}(R_a + R_{se}) = 220 - 30 \times 0.3 = 220 - 9 = 211 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply speed ratio:**
$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \frac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}
$$

$$
\frac{N_2}{800} = \frac{211}{208} \times \frac{40}{30}
$$

$$
\frac{N_2}{800} = 1.01442 \times 1.3333 = 1.3526
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final speed:**

$$
N_2 = 800 \times 1.3526 \approx \boxed{1082 \text{ RPM}}
$$

The speed has increased as the load current decreased (characteristic of series motors).

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $I_{a1}$ from $I_{L1}$** in shunt motor problems. Students often use line current directly as armature current, ignoring the shunt field current $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$. Always compute $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$.
>
> 2. **Ignoring $I_a R_a$ drop** ŌĆö In Field Control problems, this drop is small and often neglected. But in Armature Control, neglecting it gives wildly incorrect answers. Always check problem context.
>
> 3. **Sign errors in speed ratio** ŌĆö $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}$. The flux terms go **inversely**, while the EMF terms go **directly**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting linear vs saturated magnetic circuit** ŌĆö Series motor problems often assume $\Phi \propto I_a$, which is only valid in the **unsaturated linear region**. State this assumption clearly.
>
> 5. **Confusing "base speed" with "no-load speed"** ŌĆö Base speed is at rated flux and rated voltage with normal load, not no-load. KTU often uses these terms interchangeably, but examiners deduct marks if not clarified.
>
> 6. **Inverting the speed ratio in field control** ŌĆö Many students write $\Phi_2/\Phi_1$ instead of $\Phi_1/\Phi_2$ when flux decreases. Memorize: *"Flux down, Speed up"* ŌåÆ $\Phi$ in denominator.
>
> 7. **Not specifying units** ŌĆö Always write "RPM" or "rev/min" and show final numerical answer with units in a box.
>
> 8. **Skipping the V-I diagram or schematic in 7-mark questions** ŌĆö Even if asked for derivation only, drawing a simple motor circuit diagram with labeled $R_a$, $R_{sh}$, $R_{ext}$, $V$, $I_a$ earns at least 1ŌĆō2 extra marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must Remember Before Exam ŌĆö One-Glance Summary:**
>
> - **Master Equation:** $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ ŌĆö Memorize the derivation steps (Faraday's Law ŌåÆ KVL ŌåÆ Solve for $N$).
>
> - **Three Methods of Speed Control:** Field Control, Armature Control, Voltage Control (Ward-Leonard).
>
> - **Field Control:** Above base speed; $\Phi$ varies inversely with $N$; efficient; uses field rheostat; **constant power region**.
>
> - **Armature Control:** Below base speed; $R_{ext}$ varies inversely with $N$; inefficient (high $I^2R$ loss); uses armature rheostat; **constant torque region**.
>
> - **DC Series Motor Field Control:** Done via Field Divertor (parallel $R_d$), Tapped Field, or Series-Parallel Field windings ŌĆö NOT by a series rheostat (would break the circuit).
>
> - **DC Series Motor Armature Control:** External resistor in series with armature, OR Series-Parallel connection of two traction motors.
>
> - **General Speed Ratio:** $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2}$ ŌĆö The single most important formula for KTU numericals.
>
> - **Shunt Motor Constant-Flux Assumption:** For all armature control and most field control problems, assume $\Phi \approx$ constant unless stated otherwise. Field current $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ is constant.
>
> - **Series Motor Flux-Current Relation:** $\Phi \propto I_a$ only in the linear (unsaturated) region. Always state this assumption.
>
> - **Speed Range Limits:** Field control typically gives 1.0 to 2.0 ├Ś base speed; Armature control can theoretically go down to 0 RPM.
>
> - **Power & Torque Regions:** Below base speed ŌåÆ constant torque; Above base speed ŌåÆ constant power. This distinction is critical for selecting drive systems.
>
> - **Field Control Preferred Because:** Field current is small (1ŌĆō5% of rated), so rheostat is compact, cheap, and losses are minimal.
>
> - **Armature Control Disadvantage:** Full armature current passes through $R_{ext}$, so losses are $I_a^2 R_{ext}$ ŌĆö very high at low speeds; efficiency plummets.
>
> - **Ward-Leonard System:** Voltage control using a separate DC generator feeding the motor armature ŌĆö provides stepless control from 0 to above base speed; expensive but high quality.
>
> - **Numerical Strategy:** Always identify the unknowns ŌåÆ write the back-EMF equation twice (state 1 and state 2) ŌåÆ apply speed ratio ŌåÆ solve.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Diagram - Losses and Efficiency and Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET303 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Power Flow Diagram - Losses and Efficiency and Numerical Problems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Power Flow in DC Machines ŌĆö Formal Definition In a **DC Machine**, electrical and mechanical energy are interconverted via the principle of electromagnetic induction. A **Power Flow Diagram** is a graphical representation that traces the path of power from the input terminals to the output terminals, accounting for every energy dissipation (loss) encountered along the way. It quantifies the energy balance mandated by the **Law of Conservation of Energy (First Law of Thermodynamics)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > *"Power flow diagram of DC machines ŌĆö separation of constant and variable losses ŌĆö condition for maximum efficiency ŌĆö numerical problems."* Mathematically, for any electromechanical energy conversion device: $$P_{input} = P_{output} + P_{losses,total}$$ The total losses in a DC machine are categorized into four major families: | Loss Family | Type | Dependency | |---|---|---| | Copper (I┬▓R) Losses | Variable | Function of load current | | Iron / Core Losses | Constant | Function of flux & speed | | Mechanical Losses (Friction + Windage) | Constant | Function of speed only | | Stray Load Losses | Variable (Ōēł1% of output) | Function of load current | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Water Bucket" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Leaky Bucket Pipeline** > > Imagine you are pumping water from a reservoir (input) into a city tank (output) through a long pipeline. The water that actually reaches the tank is the **useful output**. But along the way, some water leaks out: > - **Bucket-level leaks** that depend on *how fast* water is flowing (variable losses = copper losses). > - **Pipe-level leaks** that happen *no matter what*, because of rust, joint drips, and air resistance (constant losses = iron + mechanical). > - **Small splashes** at unpredictable joints (stray load losses). > > The **Power Flow Diagram is the "leak map"** of this pipeline. The **Efficiency** is simply the ratio: (water that reached the tank) / (water you pumped in). Your job as an engineer is to size the pipe, reduce the rust, and minimize the splashes ŌĆö to maximize the output for a given input. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Rated Output ($P_{rated}$)** ŌĆö nameplate mechanical (motor) or electrical (generator) power in **kW** or **W**. - **Rated Voltage ($V$)** in **Volts (V)**. - **Rated Speed ($N$)** in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**. - **Armature Resistance ($R_a$)** and **Field Resistance ($R_{sh}$ or $R_{se}$)** in **Ohms (╬®)**. - **Efficiency ($\eta$)** ŌĆö dimensionless, expressed as a **percentage (%)**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Losses in a DC Machine ### A. Copper Losses ($P_{cu}$) ŌĆö *Variable Losses* These are **I┬▓R heat losses** in the windings. They scale with the **square** of the current ŌĆö a critical KTU-favorite concept. 1. **Armature Copper Loss**: $$P_{a,cu} = I_a^2 R_a$$ 2. **Shunt Field Copper Loss** (constant if supply is constant): $$P_{sh,cu} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = V \cdot I_{sh}$$ 3. **Series Field Copper Loss** (for series/compound machines): $$P_{se,cu} = I_{se}^2 R_{se} = I_a^2 R_{se}$$ 4. **Brush Contact Loss** (sometimes separated; KTU treats it under copper losses): $$P_{brush} = I_a^2 R_{brush} \;\; \text{or} \;\; V_{brush} \cdot I_a$$ where $V_{brush} \approx 2 \text{ V}$ for carbon brushes. ### B. Iron / Core Losses ($P_i$) ŌĆö *Constant Losses* Occur in the **armature iron core** due to alternating flux (even in DC machines, the armature core sees AC flux as it rotates). - **Hysteresis Loss** $P_h = \eta_h \cdot B_{max}^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V_{core}$ - **Eddy Current Loss** $P_e = \eta_e \cdot B_{max}^{2} \cdot f^2 \cdot t^2 \cdot V_{core}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In DC machine analysis, iron losses are **assumed constant** at rated voltage and rated speed because both $B_{max}$ and $f$ are constant under normal operating conditions. ### C. Mechanical Losses ($P_m$) ŌĆö *Constant Losses* - **Friction Loss** (in bearings, brushes sliding on commutator) - **Windage Loss** (air resistance on rotating parts) > These are also **assumed constant** at rated speed. ### D. Stray Load Losses ($P_{stray}$) Caused by **flux distortion** under load (armature reaction). Empirical estimate: **1% of rated output**. $$P_{stray} \approx 0.01 \times P_{rated}$$ ## 2.2 Constant vs Variable Losses ŌĆö The KTU Pivot | Loss Type | Depends On | Why "Constant" or "Variable" | |---|---|---| | Iron Loss $P_i$ | Flux, Speed (constant at rated) | **Constant** | | Mechanical Loss $P_m$ | Speed (constant at rated) | **Constant** | | Armature Cu Loss $I_a^2 R_a$ | $I_a$ (changes with load) | **Variable** | | Stray Load Loss | $I_a$ | **Variable** | | Shunt Field Cu Loss $V \cdot I_{sh}$ | V (constant supply) | **Constant** | | Series Field Cu Loss $I_a^2 R_{se}$ | $I_a$ | **Variable** | > [!NOTE] > **Summing it up:** > $$P_{constant} = P_i + P_m + V \cdot I_{sh}$$ > $$P_{variable} = I_a^2(R_a + R_{se}) + P_{stray}$$ ## 2.3 Condition for Maximum Efficiency (KTU Super-High-Yield Topic) Efficiency is maximum when: $$\boxed{P_{variable} = P_{constant}}$$ **Derivation Logic:** Total Loss $P_L = P_{constant} + P_{variable} = P_c + I_a^2 R_a$ (treating $P_{stray}$ separately for clarity). Output power $P_{out} = V \cdot I_L$ (motor) or $V \cdot I_L$ (generator). Efficiency $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_L}$ For $\eta$ to be maximum, $\dfrac{d\eta}{d I_a} = 0$. After differentiation, the result is: $$\boxed{I_a^2 R_a = P_{constant}}$$ The **load at which maximum efficiency occurs** is **less than full load** ŌĆö typically around 75% to 100% of rated load in well-designed machines. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{losses}$ | Energy balance | W | | 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Efficiency | % | | 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{constant} + I_a^2 R_a}$ | Practical $\eta$ form | % | | 4 | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Armature copper loss | W | | 5 | $P_{sh,cu} = V \cdot I_{sh}$ | Shunt field copper loss | W | | 6 | $P_{const} = P_i + P_m + V I_{sh}$ | Constant losses | W | | 7 | $P_{var} = I_a^2 R_a + P_{stray}$ | Variable losses | W | | 8 | $I_a^2 R_a = P_{const}$ | **Max efficiency condition** | W | | 9 | $P_{stray} = 0.01 \times P_{rated}$ | Stray load estimate | W | | 10 | $P_{mech,dev} = E_b \cdot I_a$ | Mechanical power developed (motor) | W | | 11 | $P_{out,motor} = P_{dev} - P_{mech} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega$ | Motor shaft output | W | | 12 | $P_{out,gen} = V \cdot I_L$ | Generator terminal output | W | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Sizing of machines**: Knowing efficiency helps in choosing the right motor rating for an industrial application. - **Cooling system design**: Total losses become heat ŌĆö must be dissipated to prevent insulation failure. - **Energy audits**: Industries calculate running cost using $\eta$. - **Electric vehicle design**: Maximum efficiency at a specific torque/speed point directly determines range. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency Let total constant losses $= P_c = P_i + P_m + V I_{sh}$. Let total variable losses (ignoring stray for simplicity) $= P_v = I_a^2 R_a$. For a **DC Shunt Motor** (derivation is identical for generator with input/output swapped): Output power $P_{out} = V \cdot I_L \approx V \cdot I_a$ (since $I_{sh}$ is small; more precisely $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$). Input power $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$. Efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V \cdot I_L}{V \cdot I_L + P_c + I_a^2 R_a}$$ To maximize $\eta$, differentiate with respect to $I_a$ and set to zero. Equivalently, we can maximize the ratio: $$\eta = \frac{V \cdot I_L}{V \cdot I_L + P_c + I_a^2 R_a}$$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $I_a$ (treating $I_L \approx I_a$ for large loads): $$\eta = \frac{V}{V + \dfrac{P_c}{I_a} + I_a R_a}$$ Now, the term $\dfrac{P_c}{I_a} + I_a R_a$ is **minimum** when its derivative with respect to $I_a$ is zero: $$\frac{d}{d I_a}\left[\frac{P_c}{I_a} + I_a R_a\right] = -\frac{P_c}{I_a^2} + R_a = 0$$ Solving: $$\frac{P_c}{I_a^2} = R_a \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{I_a^2 R_a = P_c}$$ This confirms the **KTU golden result**: maximum efficiency occurs when **variable loss = constant loss**. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Power Flow Stages | Stage | Generator (Mechanical ŌåÆ Electrical) | Motor (Electrical ŌåÆ Mechanical) | |---|---|---| | **1. Input** | Mechanical Power $P_{mech,in} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega$ | Electrical Input $P_{el,in} = V \cdot I_L$ | | **2. Subtract Mechanical Losses** | $\rightarrow P_{mech,in} - P_m$ | ŌĆö | | **3. = Mechanical Power Developed** | $P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a$ | $P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a$ | | **4. Subtract Iron Losses** | $\rightarrow P_{dev} - P_i$ | $\rightarrow P_{dev} - P_i$ | | **5. = Electrical Power Converted** | $P_{conv} = E_b \cdot I_a$ | $P_{conv} = E_b \cdot I_a$ | | **6. Subtract Armature Cu Loss** | $\rightarrow P_{conv} - I_a^2 R_a$ | $\rightarrow P_{conv} - I_a^2 R_a$ | | **7. Subtract Brush & Series Field Loss** | $\rightarrow$ net electrical power | $\rightarrow$ net electrical power | | **8. = Gross Electrical Output** | $E_g \cdot I_L$ (at internal terminals) | ŌĆö | | **9. Subtract Shunt Field Cu Loss** | $\rightarrow V \cdot I_L$ | ŌĆö | | **10. = Output** | $P_{out,gen} = V \cdot I_L$ | $P_{out,motor} = P_{dev} - (P_i + P_m)$ | | **Total Losses** | $P_m + P_i + I_a^2 R_a + I_a^2 R_{se} + V I_{sh} + P_{stray}$ | Same | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical ŌĆö Full KTU Style > **Problem 1 (KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style):** > A 250 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.2 ╬® and a shunt field resistance of 250 ╬®. It takes an armature current of 50 A at rated load. The iron and friction losses together are 1200 W. Stray load loss is 1% of output. Calculate: > (a) Back EMF $E_b$ > (b) Input current > (c) Armature copper loss and Shunt field copper loss > (d) Total losses and Efficiency **Solution:** **Step 1: Given Data** $V = 250$ V, $R_a = 0.2$ ╬®, $R_{sh} = 250$ ╬®, $I_a = 50$ A, $P_i + P_m = 1200$ W, $P_{stray} = 0.01 \times P_{out}$ **(a) Back EMF:** The EMF equation for a DC motor is: $$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$ $$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (50 \times 0.2)$$ $$\boxed{E_b = 250 - 10 = 240 \text{ V}}$$ **(b) Input Current:** Shunt field current: $$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$ Total line current: $$I_L = I_a + I_{sh} = 50 + 1 = \boxed{51 \text{ A}}$$ **(c) Copper Losses:** Armature copper loss: $$P_{a,cu} = I_a^2 R_a = 50^2 \times 0.2 = 2500 \times 0.2 = \boxed{500 \text{ W}}$$ Shunt field copper loss: $$P_{sh,cu} = V \cdot I_{sh} = 250 \times 1 = \boxed{250 \text{ W}}$$ **(d) Total Losses and Efficiency:** Mechanical power developed: $$P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = 240 \times 50 = 12000 \text{ W}$$ Output power (subtracting iron + friction + stray): Stray loss $P_{stray} = 0.01 \times P_{out}$ ŌĆö but to solve, we need $P_{out}$ first. We can use the relation: $$P_{out} = P_{dev} - P_i - P_m - P_{stray} = 12000 - 1200 - 0.01 P_{out}$$ $$P_{out} + 0.01 P_{out} = 10800$$ $$1.01 P_{out} = 10800$$ $$P_{out} = \frac{10800}{1.01} \approx 10693.07 \text{ W}$$ Total losses: $$P_L = I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + P_i + P_m + P_{stray}$$ $$P_L = 500 + 250 + 1200 + 0.01 \times 10693.07$$ $$P_L = 1950 + 106.93 = 2056.93 \text{ W}$$ Input power: $$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 51 = 12750 \text{ W}$$ Efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{10693.07}{12750} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\eta \approx 83.87\%}$$ --- > **Problem 2 (KTU Numerical - Maximum Efficiency):** > A 100 kW, 500 V DC shunt generator has $R_a = 0.1$ ╬®, $R_{sh} = 100$ ╬®. Constant losses are 2.5 kW. Find: > (a) The load current at which efficiency is maximum. > (b) Maximum efficiency. **Solution:** **(a)** For maximum efficiency: Variable losses = Constant losses $$I_a^2 R_a = P_{constant}$$ $$I_a^2 \times 0.1 = 2500$$ $$I_a^2 = 25000 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; I_a = 158.11 \text{ A}$$ **(b)** Power developed at this load: $$E_b = V + I_a R_a = 500 + (158.11 \times 0.1) = 515.81 \text{ V}$$ $$P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = 515.81 \times 158.11 \approx 81551 \text{ W}$$ Total losses at this load: $$P_L = 2 P_{constant} = 2 \times 2500 = 5000 \text{ W}$$ Output power: $$P_{out} = P_{dev} - P_i - P_m = 81551 - 2500 = 79051 \text{ W}$$ Maximum efficiency: $$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_L} = \frac{79051}{79051 + 5000} = \frac{79051}{84051}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{max} \approx 94.05\%}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Power Flow Diagram ŌĆö DC Shunt Motor The following Mermaid block diagram represents the sequential energy transformation in a **DC Shunt Motor** with all standard losses included: ```mermaid flowchart TD A["ELECTRICAL INPUT
P_in = V x I_L"] --> B["Shunt Field Loss
P_sh = V x I_sh"] B --> C["Armature Input
V x I_a"] A --> C C --> D["Armature Cu Loss
P_a_cu = I_a^2 x R_a"] D --> E["Mechanical Power Developed
P_dev = E_b x I_a"] E --> F["Iron Core Loss
P_i = Hyst + Eddy"] E --> G["Friction + Windage Loss
P_m = Pfric + Pwind"] F --> H["Shaft Output
P_out = T_sh x omega"] G --> H H --> I["Stray Load Loss
P_stray approx 1% P_out"] I --> J["USEFUL OUTPUT
P_out shaft"] ``` ## 4.2 Power Flow Diagram ŌĆö DC Shunt Generator ```mermaid flowchart TD A["MECHANICAL INPUT
P_mech_in = T_sh x omega"] --> B["Friction + Windage
P_m"] B --> C["Mechanical Power Developed
P_dev = E_b x I_a"] A --> G["Iron Core Loss
P_i"] G --> C C --> D["Armature Cu Loss
P_a_cu = I_a^2 x R_a"] D --> E["Series Field Cu Loss
P_se = I_a^2 x R_se"] E --> F["Terminal Electrical Power
E_g x I_L"] F --> H["Shunt Field Cu Loss
P_sh = V x I_sh"] H --> I["USEFUL ELECTRICAL OUTPUT
P_out = V x I_L"] F --> I ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Loss Categorization ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["ENERGY INPUT DOMAIN"] P_IN["P_input"] end subgraph CONSTANT["CONSTANT LOSSES"] PI["Iron Loss Pi"] PM["Mechanical Loss Pm"] PSH["Shunt Field Cu Psh"] end subgraph VARIABLE["VARIABLE LOSSES"] PACU["Armature Cu I_a^2 R_a"] PSTR["Stray Load Loss"] PSECU["Series Field Cu I_a^2 R_se"] end subgraph OUTPUT["USEFUL OUTPUT DOMAIN"] P_OUT["P_output"] end P_IN --> CONSTANT P_IN --> VARIABLE P_IN --> P_OUT CONSTANT -.->|"Equal at max eta"| VARIABLE ``` ## 4.4 Efficiency Curve ŌĆö Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR A["No Load
eta = 0"] --> B["Light Load
eta rising"] B --> C["Optimal Load
eta MAX
P_var = P_const"] C --> D["Full Load
eta slightly lower"] D --> E["Overload
eta falling"] E --> F["Short Circuit
eta = 0"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (KTU University Exam - Dec 2023) **Q: List the various losses occurring in a DC machine. Classify them as constant and variable losses.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The losses in a DC machine are: **(1) Constant Losses** (independent of load): - **Iron/Core losses ($P_i$)**: Hysteresis + Eddy current losses in the armature core. - **Mechanical losses ($P_m$)**: Friction (bearings, brushes) + Windage. - **Shunt field copper loss ($V I_{sh}$)**: Constant because $V$ is constant. **(2) Variable Losses** (depend on load current $I_a$): - **Armature copper loss ($I_a^2 R_a$)**: Varies with the square of armature current. - **Series field copper loss** ($I_a^2 R_{se}$) ŌĆö present in series/compound machines. - **Stray load losses** ($\approx 1\%$ of output): Caused by flux distortion. --- ### Question 2 (KTU University Exam - May 2024) **Q: State the condition for maximum efficiency of a DC machine and explain its physical significance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Condition:** A DC machine operates at **maximum efficiency** when the **variable losses equal the constant losses**: > > $$\boxed{I_a^2 R_a = P_{constant} = P_i + P_m + V I_{sh}}$$ **Physical Significance:** - At light loads, constant losses dominate ŌåÆ efficiency is low. - At heavy loads, variable ($I^2R$) losses dominate ŌåÆ efficiency drops again. - The efficiency is **maximum at an intermediate load** where the two competing loss components are balanced. - This is why **DC machines are designed to operate near full load** for best performance. --- ## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **Question A(a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat power flow diagram, explain the energy conversion stages in a **DC Shunt Motor** and derive the condition for maximum efficiency. **Model Solution:** **Power Flow Diagram (textual representation for 4 marks):** ``` V┬ĘI_L (Input) Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ V┬ĘI_sh (Shunt Field Cu Loss) ŌåÆ Heat Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ V┬ĘI_a (Armature Input) Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ I_a┬▓┬ĘR_a (Armature Cu Loss) ŌåÆ Heat Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ E_b┬ĘI_a (Mechanical Power Developed, P_dev) Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ P_i (Iron Loss) ŌåÆ Heat Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ P_m (Friction + Windage) ŌåÆ Heat Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ P_out = T_sh┬ĘŽē (Shaft Output) ``` **Derivation (3 marks):** Let $P_c = P_i + P_m + V I_{sh}$ = total constant losses. Let $P_v = I_a^2 R_a$ = total variable losses. Efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{V I_a}{V I_a + P_c + I_a^2 R_a}$$ For maximum $\eta$, differentiate with respect to $I_a$ and set to zero. After simplification (as shown in Section 3.1): $$\boxed{I_a^2 R_a = P_c}$$ This is the condition for maximum efficiency. Physically, this point lies at a load **less than rated load** in most practical machines. --- > **Question A(b) [7 Marks]:** A 200 V, 4-pole DC shunt motor has $R_a = 0.5$ ╬®, $R_{sh} = 200$ ╬®, and draws a line current of 30 A at rated load. The rotational losses (iron + friction) are 400 W. Calculate: (i) Armature current, (ii) Back EMF, (iii) Total copper losses, (iv) Output power, (v) Efficiency at rated load. Neglect stray losses. **Model Solution:** **Given:** $V = 200$ V, $R_a = 0.5$ ╬®, $R_{sh} = 200$ ╬®, $I_L = 30$ A, $P_{rot} = 400$ W. **(i) Armature Current:** $$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{200}{200} = 1 \text{ A}$$ $$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 30 - 1 = \boxed{29 \text{ A}}$$ **(ii) Back EMF:** $$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 200 - (29 \times 0.5) = 200 - 14.5 = \boxed{185.5 \text{ V}}$$ **(iii) Total Copper Losses:** $$P_{cu,total} = I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} = (29^2 \times 0.5) + (200 \times 1)$$ $$= 420.5 + 200 = \boxed{620.5 \text{ W}}$$ **(iv) Output Power:** $$P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = 185.5 \times 29 = 5379.5 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{out} = P_{dev} - P_{rot} = 5379.5 - 400 = \boxed{4979.5 \text{ W}}$$ **(v) Efficiency:** $$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 200 \times 30 = 6000 \text{ W}$$ $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{4979.5}{6000} \times 100 \approx \boxed{82.99\%}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Alternative Choice* > **Question B(a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat power flow diagram, explain the various losses in a **DC Shunt Generator** and establish the relationship between efficiency and load current. **Model Solution:** **Power Flow Diagram (4 marks):** ``` Mechanical Input (T_sh ├Ś Žē) Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ P_m (Friction + Windage) Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ P_dev = E_b ├Ś I_a (Developed Power) Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ P_i (Iron Losses) Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ I_a┬▓ R_a (Armature Cu Loss) Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ E_g ├Ś I_L (Terminal Power) Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ V ├Ś I_sh (Shunt Field Cu Loss) Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ V ├Ś I_L (Output) ``` **Efficiency vs Load Current Relationship (3 marks):** $$\eta = \frac{V I_L}{V I_L + P_c + I_a^2 R_a + P_{stray}}$$ - At no load ($I_L \to 0$): $\eta \to 0$ (output is zero). - As $I_L$ increases: $\eta$ rises. - Reaches **maximum** when $I_a^2 R_a = P_c$. - Beyond this, $I^2 R$ losses dominate and $\eta$ falls. --- > **Question B(b) [7 Marks]:** A 10 kW, 250 V DC shunt generator has armature resistance 0.15 ╬® and shunt field resistance 125 ╬®. The constant losses (iron + friction) are 500 W. Find the efficiency at: (i) Full load, (ii) Half load. Also find the load at which efficiency is maximum and the maximum efficiency. Neglect stray losses. **Model Solution:** **Given:** $P_{rated} = 10$ kW = 10,000 W, $V = 250$ V, $R_a = 0.15$ ╬®, $R_{sh} = 125$ ╬®, $P_c = 500$ W (excluding $V I_{sh}$ here, will include). Shunt field current: $$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{125} = 2 \text{ A}$$ Total constant losses: $$P_{const,total} = P_c + V I_{sh} = 500 + (250 \times 2) = 500 + 500 = 1000 \text{ W}$$ Full-load current (rated output): $$I_L = \frac{P_{rated}}{V} = \frac{10000}{250} = 40 \text{ A}$$ $$I_{a,FL} = I_L + I_{sh} = 40 + 2 = 42 \text{ A}$$ **(i) Efficiency at Full Load:** $$P_{cu,a,FL} = I_a^2 R_a = 42^2 \times 0.15 = 1764 \times 0.15 = 264.6 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{loss,FL} = 1000 + 264.6 = 1264.6 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{out,FL} = V I_L = 250 \times 40 = 10000 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{in,FL} = 10000 + 1264.6 = 11264.6 \text{ W}$$ $$\eta_{FL} = \frac{10000}{11264.6} \times 100 \approx \boxed{88.77\%}$$ **(ii) Efficiency at Half Load:** $$I_{L,HL} = 20 \text{ A}, \;\; I_{a,HL} = 22 \text{ A}$$ $$P_{cu,a,HL} = 22^2 \times 0.15 = 484 \times 0.15 = 72.6 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{loss,HL} = 1000 + 72.6 = 1072.6 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{out,HL} = 250 \times 20 = 5000 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{in,HL} = 5000 + 1072.6 = 6072.6 \text{ W}$$ $$\eta_{HL} = \frac{5000}{6072.6} \times 100 \approx \boxed{82.34\%}$$ **(iii) Load for Maximum Efficiency:** $$I_a^2 R_a = P_{const,total} = 1000 \text{ W}$$ $$I_a^2 = \frac{1000}{0.15} = 6666.67$$ $$I_a = 81.65 \text{ A}$$ $$I_L = I_a - I_{sh} = 81.65 - 2 = 79.65 \text{ A}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note:** $I_a = 81.65$ A > $I_{a,FL} = 42$ A, so maximum efficiency occurs **beyond full load** in this case ŌĆö an unusual but mathematically valid result. In well-designed machines, it falls slightly before full load. $$P_{out,max\_\eta} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 79.65 = 19912.5 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{loss,max\_\eta} = 2 P_{const} = 2000 \text{ W}$$ $$\eta_{max} = \frac{19912.5}{19912.5 + 2000} \times 100 = \frac{19912.5}{21912.5} \times 100 \approx \boxed{90.87\%}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting shunt field copper loss** when computing constant losses ŌĆö costs **2 marks**. > 2. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_a$** in motor/generator equations ŌĆö leads to wrong $E_b$ and wrong copper losses. > 3. **Not adding $P_{stray}$** when stated in the problem ŌĆö costs 1 mark. > 4. **Mixing up motor and generator EMF equations** ŌĆö $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (motor) vs $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (generator). > 5. **Ignoring units** ŌĆö $R_a$ in ╬®, $I_a$ in A, $P$ in W (or kW consistently). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Power flow** follows the **Law of Conservation of Energy**: $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{losses}$. - [x] **Constant losses** = Iron + Mechanical + Shunt Field Cu loss. - [x] **Variable losses** = Armature Cu + Series Field Cu + Stray Load loss. - [x] **Maximum efficiency condition**: Variable loss = Constant loss, i.e., $I_a^2 R_a = P_{const}$. - [x] At maximum efficiency, **total losses = 2 ├Ś Constant losses**. - [x] **EMF equations**: Motor: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Generator: $E_g = V + I_a R_a$. - [x] **Mechanical power developed**: $P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a$ (valid for both motor and generator). - [x] **Output power (motor)**: $P_{out} = P_{dev} - (P_i + P_m)$. - [x] **Output power (generator)**: $P_{out} = V \cdot I_L$. - [x] **Stray load loss** Ōēł **1% of output** ŌĆö only include if mentioned in the problem. - [x] **Brush contact loss** is treated as part of **copper losses** in KTU convention. - [x] Maximum efficiency typically occurs at **75ŌĆō100% of full load** in well-designed machines. - [x] **Iron losses depend on $V$ and $N$** ŌĆö assumed constant at rated values. - [x] **Mechanical losses depend only on $N$** ŌĆö assumed constant at rated speed. - [x] For **shunt machines**, $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ is **constant** throughout the operation. - [x] For **series machines**, $I_a = I_L$ (field winding in series with armature).

> - **Topic:** Testing - Swinburne's Test, Hopkinson's Test, Retardation Test - Separation of Rotational Losses and Numerical Problems
