## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Fundamental Need for Indirect Testing

Direct loading of large DC machines (often rated from tens to hundreds of kilowatts) in a laboratory is **economically and physically impractical**. To load a **500 kW** machine at full rating, the test bench must dissipate **500 kW** as heat, which demands massive water-cooled load resistors, dedicated three-phase infrastructure, and elaborate safety arrangements. To avoid this wastage, electrical engineers developed a family of **indirect testing methods** that compute full-load efficiency from no-load or light-load measurements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> "Testing of DC machines ŌĆō Swinburne's test, Hopkinson's test and Retardation test. Separation of rotational losses into iron, friction and windage losses. Numerical problems."

### 1.2 The Three Tests at a Glance

| Test | Purpose | Machine Count | Loading Method |
|------|---------|---------------|----------------|
| **Swinburne's Test** | Predetermine efficiency of a *single* shunt machine at any load | One | None (no-load run) |
| **Hopkinson's Test** | Predetermine full-load efficiency of *two identical* machines | Two coupled | Regenerative (back-to-back) |
| **Retardation Test (Run-down Test)** | Separate rotational losses into iron, friction, and windage | One | None (deceleration) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Three Doctors

Imagine three doctors diagnosing a runner's performance:

1. **Swinburne's Doctor (The No-Load Examiner)**: Instead of making the runner sprint (full load), he simply measures the runner's resting heart rate and breathing at standstill. From this baseline, he *extrapolates* how much energy the runner would burn during a real sprint.

2. **Hopkinson's Doctors (The Twin Marathon)**: Two identical athletes are tied together by a rope. Athlete A pulls, Athlete B resists. The race is run without anyone actually moving forward ŌĆö yet both experience *real* running effort. A small push from outside is enough to keep the system alive.

3. **Retardation Doctor (The Coasting Cyclist)**: A cyclist rolls down a hill with the engine off. By clocking how fast he slows down, the doctor calculates how much energy friction and air drag steal from him ŌĆö *without ever burning fuel on purpose*.

> [!NOTE]
> All three tests exploit the key insight: **in a DC shunt machine, the core (iron) losses and mechanical (friction + windage) losses are practically constant from no-load to full-load**, because the shunt field current and hence the flux $\phi$ remain essentially unchanged.

### 1.4 Constant Losses vs. Variable Losses ŌĆö The Critical Distinction

$$\boxed{P_{\text{constant}} = P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{friction}} + P_{\text{windage}}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{variable}} = I_a^2 R_a + I_a^2 R_{se} \;\;(\text{copper losses}) + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} \;\;(\text{shunt Cu loss})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Swinburne and Hopkinson tests assume **stray load losses are negligible** (Ōēł1% of output) ŌĆö a KTU-assumed default unless specifically stated otherwise. Stray load loss = **1% of full-load output** is added as a separate row in the efficiency calculation table.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Swinburne's Test ŌĆö Detailed Operational Logic

**Test Condition:** The machine is run as a *shunt motor* at **no-load** (or light load), supplied from its **rated voltage $V$**.

**Step-by-Step Measured Quantities:**
- No-load supply current $I_{L0}$
- No-load armature current $I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh}$
- No-load supply voltage $V$
- Armature resistance $R_a$ (measured cold; hot value used during calculation)
- Shunt field resistance $R_{sh}$

**The Core Logic:**

At no-load, the machine develops just enough torque to overcome rotational losses. Hence the **electromagnetic torque = loss torque**, meaning the gross mechanical power developed equals the rotational losses.

$$P_{\text{in,no-load}} = V \cdot I_{L0}$$

This input is split into:
- Shunt field copper loss: $P_{sh} = V \cdot I_{sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$
- Constant rotational loss: $W_0 = V \cdot I_{L0} - I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} - I_{a0}^2 R_a$

Since $I_{a0}$ is tiny (typically 2ŌĆō5% of full-load current), $I_{a0}^2 R_a$ is negligible and the standard KTU formula simplifies to:

$$\boxed{W_0 = V I_{L0} - I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} \;\;\approx\;\; V I_{L0} - V I_{sh} = V(I_{L0} - I_{sh})}$$

**Efficiency at Any Predetermined Load Current $I_L$:**

$$\eta = \frac{V I_L - \left[ I_a^2 R_a + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} + W_0 + P_{\text{stray}} \right]}{V I_L} \times 100\,\%$$

where $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ and $P_{\text{stray}} = 0.01 \times V I_{L,\text{full}}$ (if full load).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Works:** Because $W_0$ is treated as *constant* at *all* loads, the test (a no-load experiment) is used to predict efficiency at *any* load. The KTU examiner routinely asks the student to compute efficiency at $\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{3}{4}, 1\tfrac{1}{4}$ load to verify this constant-loss assumption.

### 2.2 Hopkinson's Test ŌĆö Detailed Operational Logic

**Setup Conditions:**
- Two *identical* DC shunt machines are **mechanically coupled** to the same shaft.
- Machine **M** acts as a **motor** (driven from the DC supply).
- Machine **G** acts as a **generator** (driven mechanically by M).
- Generator terminals are connected back to the supply bus through an ammeter (regenerative feedback).
- Generator field is *weakened* slightly and motor field *strengthened* slightly so that generator EMF exceeds supply voltage by a small margin ŌĆö enabling current to flow from G back to the supply.

**Measured Quantities at a Given Load:**
- Supply line current $I_L$
- Motor armature current $I_{am}$
- Generator armature current $I_{ag}$
- Motor field current $I_{f1}$ and generator field current $I_{f2}$
- Supply voltage $V$

**The Core Logic (Conservation of Power):**

Total input from supply = Total losses of the *combined system* (both machines).

$$V I_L = \text{Total losses} = P_{\text{cu,am}} + P_{\text{cu,ag}} + P_{\text{cu,shunt}} + 2W_0 + P_{\text{stray}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total losses} = I_{am}^2 R_{am} + I_{ag}^2 R_{ag} + V(I_{f1}+I_{f2}) + 2W_0 + P_{\text{stray}}}$$

**Separation Procedure:**
1. Compute total losses from $V I_L$.
2. Subtract the *known* $I^2R$ copper losses and shunt field copper losses.
3. The remainder is $2W_0 + P_{\text{stray}}$.

**Individual Machine Efficiency:**

For the **motor**, output = Input ŌłÆ Losses:

$$P_{\text{out,motor}} = V I_{am} - \left[I_{am}^2 R_{am} + I_{f1}^2 R_{sh1} + W_0 + P_{\text{stray,m}}\right]$$

For the **generator**, output is delivered to the bus:

$$P_{\text{out,gen}} = V I_{ag}$$

$$\eta_{\text{gen}} = \frac{P_{\text{out,gen}}}{P_{\text{out,gen}} + I_{ag}^2 R_{ag} + I_{f2}^2 R_{sh2} + W_0 + P_{\text{stray,g}}}$$

### 2.3 Retardation (Run-Down) Test ŌĆö Detailed Operational Logic

**Test Condition:** The machine is run as a *shunt motor* at a speed **slightly above rated** (e.g., ~10% above $N_r$), then the supply is **suddenly cut off**. The machine is allowed to coast down freely; nothing is connected to the shaft.

**Physical Principle:**

With the field winding *still energized from an independent supply* (or residual magnetism for very rough estimates), the only torque opposing motion is the **rotational loss torque** $T_r$.

$$T_r = J \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

where $J$ = moment of inertia of the rotating part (kg┬Ęm┬▓) and $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s).

**Power Equation:**

The rotational power loss equals the rate at which the kinetic energy of the rotor decreases:

$$P_r = T_r \cdot \omega = J \omega \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

Converting $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$\boxed{P_r = J \left(\frac{2\pi}{60}\right)^2 N \frac{dN}{dt} \;\; \text{Watts}}$$

**Practical Measurement:**

A tachometer records speeds $N_1$ (initial) and $N_2$ (final) and the time $t$ taken:

$$P_r = J \left(\frac{2\pi}{60}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{N_1 - N_2}{t} \cdot N_{\text{avg}}$$

Approximation for small speed drop: $N_{\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{N_1 + N_2}{2}$.

### 2.4 Separation of Iron, Friction, and Windage Losses

Iron loss depends on flux density $B$ and frequency $f$. For a DC machine with constant field current, $B$ is approximately constant and $f$ (electrical frequency) is **proportional to speed $N$**. Thus $P_{\text{iron}} \propto N$.

Friction and windage losses depend on speed cubed (for windage, $P_w \propto N^3$; for bearing friction, $P_f \propto N^{1.5}$ approximately), but for engineering purposes:

$$P_{\text{friction+windage}} \propto N^k \quad (k \approx 2 \text{ to } 3)$$

**The Two-Run Method:**
1. **Run 1 (Field excited at rated value):** Measure rotational loss at speed $N_1$ ŌåÆ gives $P_{r1} = P_{\text{iron1}} + P_{\text{f+w1}}$.
2. **Run 2 (Field unexcited, residual magnetism only):** Measure rotational loss at speed $N_1'$ ŌåÆ iron loss is much smaller (because $B$ from residual magnetism $\ll B_{\text{rated}}$). The mechanical losses remain essentially unchanged.

By running Run 2 at *the same speed* as Run 1, the friction + windage loss is equal in both runs. Hence:

$$P_{\text{iron,rated}} = P_{r1} - P_{r2}$$

$$P_{\text{friction+windage}} = P_{r2} \;\;(\text{or extrapolated})$$

**The Two-Speed Method (KTU Standard):**

Run the machine at speeds $N_1$ and $N_2$ with rated field. Measure rotational losses $P_{r1}$ and $P_{r2}$.

Assume $P_r = A N + B N^2$ (linear + quadratic approximation):

$$\begin{cases} P_{r1} = A N_1 + B N_1^2 \\ P_{r2} = A N_2 + B N_2^2 \end{cases}$$

Solving:
$$A = \frac{P_{r1} N_2^2 - P_{r2} N_1^2}{N_1 N_2^2 - N_2 N_1^2}, \quad B = \frac{P_{r2} N_1 - P_{r1} N_2}{N_1 N_2^2 - N_2 N_1^2}$$

Then $P_{\text{iron}} = A N$ and $P_{\text{f+w}} = B N^2$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | $W_0 = V I_{L0} - I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ | Constant rotational loss (Swinburne) | W |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{VI_L - (I_a^2 R_a + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} + W_0)}{VI_L} \times 100$ | Efficiency at any load | % |
| 3 | $P_{\text{stray}} = 0.01 \times \text{Full load output}$ | Stray load loss (KTU default) | W |
| 4 | Total loss (Hopkinson) $= V I_L = 2W_0 + I_{am}^2 R_{am} + I_{ag}^2 R_{ag} + V(I_{f1}+I_{f2})$ | Combined losses | W |
| 5 | $W_0 = \dfrac{V I_L - I_{am}^2 R_{am} - I_{ag}^2 R_{ag} - V(I_{f1}+I_{f2})}{2}$ | Stray + rotational loss per machine | W |
| 6 | $P_r = J \left(\dfrac{2\pi}{60}\right)^2 N \dfrac{dN}{dt}$ | Retardation rotational power loss | W |
| 7 | $P_r \approx J \left(\dfrac{2\pi}{60}\right)^2 N_{avg} \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{t}$ | Practical retardation formula | W |
| 8 | $P_r = A N + B N^2$ | Two-speed separation model | W |
| 9 | $A = \dfrac{P_{r1} N_2^2 - P_{r2} N_1^2}{N_1 N_2 (N_2 - N_1)}$ | Iron loss coefficient | W/(rpm) |
| 10 | $B = \dfrac{P_{r2} N_1 - P_{r1} N_2}{N_1 N_2 (N_2 - N_1)}$ | Friction+windage coefficient | W/(rpm)┬▓ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Swinburne's Test** is the *factory acceptance test* standard for shunt motors in industrial drive manufacturing (e.g., in cement, sugar, and textile industries in Kerala).
- **Hopkinson's Test** is the workhorse of *traction motor testing* in railway loco sheds (e.g., Southern Railway's testing bays).
- **Retardation Test** is the *research laboratory standard* for assessing energy efficiency of new motor designs ŌĆö directly mandated under **IS 12615:2018** for energy-efficient motors.
- Separation of losses is critical in **IE3/IE4 premium efficiency motor certification** under the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) star-rating system in India.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Swinburne's Efficiency at Predetermined Load

**Given:** $V$ (rated voltage), $I_{L0}$ (no-load line current), $R_a$, $R_{sh}$, $I_L$ (load line current at which efficiency is to be found).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute shunt field current.**
At the moment of the no-load test, voltmeter reading $V$ and the shunt field are connected in parallel. Ohm's law gives:
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute no-load armature current.**
The no-load line current $I_{L0}$ is the sum of armature and shunt currents:
$$I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute constant rotational loss.**
At no-load, the only mechanical power developed is the rotational loss. Input from supply splits into shunt copper loss, armature copper loss, and rotational loss:
$$V I_{L0} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} + I_{a0}^2 R_a + W_0$$
Solving for $W_0$:
$$W_0 = V I_{L0} - I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} - I_{a0}^2 R_a$$
Since $I_{a0}$ is very small (Ōēł2ŌĆō5% of $I_{L,\text{rated}}$), $I_{a0}^2 R_a$ is negligible (Ōēł0.1% of $V I_{L0}$). KTU convention is to **neglect it**:
$$\boxed{W_0 \approx V I_{L0} - I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Armature current at predetermined load $I_L$.**
$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Total losses at load $I_L$.**
$$P_{\text{loss}} = I_a^2 R_a + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} + W_0 + P_{\text{stray}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Efficiency.**
$$\eta = \frac{V I_L - P_{\text{loss}}}{V I_L} \times 100\,\%$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Hopkinson's Test Power Balance

**Setup:** Two identical machines M and G with armature resistances $R_{am} = R_{ag} = R_a$, shunt resistances $R_{shm} = R_{shg} = R_{sh}$.

**Current Relations (Kirchhoff at the bus):**
The motor draws current $I_{am}$ from the supply. The generator supplies current $I_{ag}$ to the bus. The net line current $I_L$ drawn from the supply is:
$$I_L = I_{am} - I_{ag} \;\;(\text{regenerative flow})$$

**Power Balance:**
Power in from supply = Total losses in both machines.
$$V I_L = \underbrace{I_{am}^2 R_{am}}_{\text{M Cu loss}} + \underbrace{I_{ag}^2 R_{ag}}_{\text{G Cu loss}} + \underbrace{I_{f1}^2 R_{shm} + I_{f2}^2 R_{shg}}_{\text{Shunt field Cu}} + \underbrace{2W_0}_{\text{Rotational, both}} + P_{\text{stray}}$$

**Solving for $W_0$ per machine:**
$$W_0 = \frac{V I_L - I_{am}^2 R_a - I_{ag}^2 R_a - V(I_{f1}+I_{f2}) - P_{\text{stray}}}{2}$$

**Motor Output:**
$$P_{\text{out,M}} = V I_{am} - \left[I_{am}^2 R_a + I_{f1}^2 R_{sh} + W_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}P_{\text{stray}}\right]$$
$$\eta_{\text{M}} = \frac{P_{\text{out,M}}}{V I_{am}} \times 100\,\%$$

**Generator Output (to the bus):**
$$P_{\text{out,G}} = V I_{ag}$$
$$\eta_{\text{G}} = \frac{V I_{ag}}{V I_{ag} + I_{ag}^2 R_a + I_{f2}^2 R_{sh} + W_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}P_{\text{stray}}} \times 100\,\%$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Retardation Test Rotational Loss

**Energy stored in rotating mass:**
$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} J \omega^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} J \left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2 = \frac{J \pi^2 N^2}{1800}$$

**Differentiating with respect to time:**
$$\frac{dE}{dt} = \frac{2 J \pi^2 N}{1800} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt} = J \left(\frac{2\pi}{60}\right)^2 N \frac{dN}{dt}$$

**This rate equals the rotational power loss** (since the only sink of kinetic energy is rotational losses):
$$\boxed{P_r = J \left(\frac{2\pi}{60}\right)^2 N \frac{dN}{dt}}$$

**Practical Two-Speed Approximation:**
If the machine decelerates from $N_1$ to $N_2$ in time $t$ seconds:
$$\frac{dN}{dt} \approx \frac{N_2 - N_1}{t} \;\;(\text{negative value})$$
$$P_r \approx J \left(\frac{2\pi}{60}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2} \cdot \frac{N_1 - N_2}{t}$$

The negative sign indicates loss; the magnitude is the rotational loss:
$$P_r = J \left(\frac{2\pi}{60}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{(N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{2t} = \frac{J \pi^2 (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{1800\,t}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical ŌĆö KTU Pattern Problem 1 (Swinburne's Test)

> **Problem:** A **250 V, 30 kW** DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of **0.15 ╬®** and a shunt field resistance of **100 ╬®**. On no-load, it draws a line current of **6 A** at 250 V. Find (i) rotational loss, (ii) efficiency at full load, (iii) efficiency at 1.25 times full load.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute full-load current.**
$$I_{L,\text{full}} = \frac{30{,}000}{250} = 120\;\text{A}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Shunt field current at no-load.**
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5\;\text{A}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö No-load armature current.**
$$I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh} = 6 - 2.5 = 3.5\;\text{A}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rotational loss (neglecting tiny $I_{a0}^2 R_a$).**
$$W_0 = V I_{L0} - I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = 250 \times 6 - (2.5)^2 \times 100$$
$$W_0 = 1500 - 625 = \mathbf{875\;\text{W}}$$

(Check: $I_{a0}^2 R_a = 3.5^2 \times 0.15 = 1.84$ W ŌåÆ truly negligible.)

**Step 5 ŌĆö Stray load loss at full load.**
$$P_{\text{stray}} = 0.01 \times 30{,}000 = 300\;\text{W}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Full-load efficiency.**
$$I_a = I_{L,\text{full}} - I_{sh} = 120 - 2.5 = 117.5\;\text{A}$$
$$I_a^2 R_a = (117.5)^2 \times 0.15 = 2070.94\;\text{W}$$
$$I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = 625\;\text{W}$$
$$\text{Total losses} = 2070.94 + 625 + 875 + 300 = 3870.94\;\text{W}$$
$$\eta_{\text{FL}} = \frac{30{,}000}{30{,}000 + 3870.94} \times 100 = \mathbf{88.57\,\%}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Efficiency at 1.25 ├Ś FL.**
$$I_{L,1.25} = 1.25 \times 120 = 150\;\text{A}$$
$$I_a = 150 - 2.5 = 147.5\;\text{A}$$
$$I_a^2 R_a = (147.5)^2 \times 0.15 = 3263.44\;\text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{stray,1.25}} = 0.01 \times 1.25 \times 30{,}000 = 375\;\text{W}$$
$$\text{Total losses} = 3263.44 + 625 + 875 + 375 = 5138.44\;\text{W}$$
$$\eta_{1.25} = \frac{37{,}500}{37{,}500 + 5138.44} \times 100 = \mathbf{87.94\,\%}$$

> **Observation (KTU Insight):** Efficiency drops slightly at 1.25├Ś FL because the $I^2 R$ loss rises faster than output ŌĆö this is typical of all DC machines and is a **favourite follow-up KTU question**.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical ŌĆö KTU Pattern Problem 2 (Hopkinson's Test)

> **Problem:** Two identical **250 V, 25 kW** shunt machines are subjected to Hopkinson's test. Armature resistance of each = **0.1 ╬®**, shunt field resistance of each = **250 ╬®**. Test readings: line current = **50 A**, motor field current = **1.0 A**, generator field current = **0.95 A**. Find the efficiency of both machines at this load. Assume stray load loss = **1% of full-load output**.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute terminal output power per machine (full-load).**
Full-load current = $\frac{25{,}000}{250} = 100$ A.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Shunt field currents and their copper losses.**
$$I_{f1} = 1.0\;\text{A} \Rightarrow I_{f1}^2 R_{sh} = 1 \times 250 = 250\;\text{W}$$
$$I_{f2} = 0.95\;\text{A} \Rightarrow I_{f2}^2 R_{sh} = 0.9025 \times 250 = 225.6\;\text{W}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Generator and motor armature currents.**
Since motor is fed by the supply minus the generator's regenerative feed:
$$I_L = I_{am} - I_{ag} = 50\;\text{A}$$
Also, by voltage balance and identical EMF characteristics: $I_{am} \approx I_{ag} + 2I_{sh} \approx$ near-equal. For symmetry, KTU assumes $I_{am} = I_{ag} + I_L$ with the shunt fields fed from the bus.
$$I_{am} = I_L + I_{ag} + I_{f1} + I_{f2} = 50 + I_{ag} + 1.0 + 0.95$$
For an exact numerical answer, the KTU-stated simplification is to assume $I_{am} \approx I_{ag} \approx I_L$ for symmetric loading ŌĆö but a more precise formulation is:
$$I_{ag} = \frac{I_L + I_{f1} + I_{f2}}{2} = \frac{50 + 1.0 + 0.95}{2} = 25.975\;\text{A}$$
$$I_{am} = I_{ag} + I_L = 25.975 + 50 = 75.975\;\text{A}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Armature copper losses.**
$$I_{am}^2 R_{am} = (75.975)^2 \times 0.1 = 577.2\;\text{W}$$
$$I_{ag}^2 R_{ag} = (25.975)^2 \times 0.1 = 67.5\;\text{W}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Total losses (excluding rotational).**
$$P_{\text{cu,total}} = 577.2 + 67.5 + 250 + 225.6 = 1120.3\;\text{W}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Input from supply.**
$$P_{\text{in}} = V I_L = 250 \times 50 = 12{,}500\;\text{W}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Total rotational + stray loss.**
$$2W_0 + P_{\text{stray,total}} = 12{,}500 - 1120.3 = 11{,}379.7\;\text{W}$$
At full load, $P_{\text{stray,total}} = 0.01 \times 25{,}000 \times 2 = 500$ W (split equally for the test load which is roughly half rated, KTU often takes $0.01 \times \text{output of generator} = 250$ W):
$$2W_0 \approx 11{,}379.7 - 500 = 10{,}879.7\;\text{W}$$
$$W_0 \approx 5440\;\text{W} \;\; \text{per machine}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Motor efficiency.**
$$P_{\text{in,motor}} = V I_{am} = 250 \times 75.975 = 18{,}993.75\;\text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{loss,motor}} = 577.2 + 250 + 5440 + 250 = 6517.2\;\text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{out,motor}} = 18{,}993.75 - 6517.2 = 12{,}476.55\;\text{W}$$
$$\eta_{\text{M}} = \frac{12{,}476.55}{18{,}993.75} \times 100 = \mathbf{65.68\,\%}$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Generator efficiency.**
$$P_{\text{out,gen}} = V I_{ag} = 250 \times 25.975 = 6493.75\;\text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{loss,gen}} = 67.5 + 225.6 + 5440 + 250 = 5983.1\;\text{W}$$
$$\eta_{\text{G}} = \frac{6493.75}{6493.75 + 5983.1} \times 100 = \mathbf{52.04\,\%}$$

> **Note (KTU Insight):** The efficiency at this *test load* is low because the test is run at light load. KTU often asks: "What would be the efficiency at full load?" The trick is to scale up $I_{am}, I_{ag}$ proportionally and recompute.

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical ŌĆö KTU Pattern Problem 3 (Retardation Test)

> **Problem:** In a retardation test on a DC machine with $J = 50\;\text{kg┬Ęm}^2$, the speed falls from **1500 rpm** to **1400 rpm** in **25 seconds**. Find the rotational loss.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert speed to rad/s is not needed (use the rpm-form formula).**
Using $P_r = \dfrac{J \pi^2 (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{1800\,t}$:
$$N_1^2 - N_2^2 = 1500^2 - 1400^2 = (1500-1400)(1500+1400) = 100 \times 2900 = 290{,}000$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute values.**
$$P_r = \frac{50 \times \pi^2 \times 290{,}000}{1800 \times 25}$$
$$P_r = \frac{50 \times 9.8696 \times 290{,}000}{45{,}000}$$
$$P_r = \frac{143{,}109{,}200}{45{,}000}$$
$$\boxed{P_r \approx 3180.2\;\text{W} \approx 3.18\;\text{kW}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Separation by two-speed method.**
A second run gives $P_{r2} = 1800$ W at 1450 rpm. Solving:
$$A = \frac{3180 \times 1450^2 - 1800 \times 1500^2}{1500 \times 1450^2 - 1450 \times 1500^2}$$
$$A = \frac{3180 \times 2{,}102{,}500 - 1800 \times 2{,}250{,}000}{1500 \times 2{,}102{,}500 - 1450 \times 2{,}250{,}000}$$
$$A = \frac{6.686 \times 10^9 - 4.05 \times 10^9}{3.154 \times 10^9 - 3.263 \times 10^9} = \frac{2.636 \times 10^9}{-1.09 \times 10^8} = -24.18$$

(Taking magnitudes: $A \approx 24.18$ W/rpm and $B \approx 0.0017$ W/rpm┬▓, so $P_{\text{iron}} = 24.18 N$ and $P_{\text{f+w}} = 0.0017 N^2$.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 ŌĆö Swinburne's Test Circuit (Block-Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    BUS[DC SUPPLY BUS V VOLT] --> AMM[AMMETER I_L0]
    BUS --> VW[VOLTMETER V]
    BUS --> RSH[SHUNT FIELD R_SH]
    BUS --> ARM[ARMATURE R_A]
    AMM --> ARM
    RSH --> G1[CONSTANT LOSS PATH]
    ARM --> M1[DC SHUNT MACHINE]
    M1 --> NL[NO LOAD MECHANICAL OUTPUT = 0]
    M1 --> R1[ROTATIONAL LOSS W_0]
    ARM --> ACU[I_a0 SQUARE R_a TINY]
    RSH --> SCU[I_sh SQUARE R_sh]
    R1 --> SUM[EFFICIENCY AT ANY LOAD]
    ACU --> SUM
    SCU --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[PREDICTED OUTPUT = V I_L MINUS ALL LOSSES]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 ŌĆö Hopkinson's Test Architecture (Back-to-Back Regenerative Loop)

```mermaid
graph TB
    SUP[DC SUPPLY BUS V] --> SW[MAIN SWITCH]
    SW --> MM[MOTOR M ARMATURE]
    SW --> FSM[MOTOR FIELD R_SH1]
    SUP --> FB[FEEDBACK LINE]
    FB --> GEN[GENERATOR G ARMATURE]
    GEN --> FSG[GENERATOR FIELD R_SH2]
    MM -. MECHANICAL COUPLING .- GEN
    GEN --> LD[LOAD RHEOSTAT VARIABLE]
    SUP --> AML[SUPPLY AMMETER I_L]
    AML --> SW
    MM --> AMM[MOTOR AMMETER I_am]
    GEN --> AMG[GENERATOR AMMETER I_ag]
    AMM --> EFFM[MOTOR EFFICIENCY]
    AMG --> EFFG[GENERATOR EFFICIENCY]
    SUP --> PB[POWER BALANCE V I_L = TOTAL LOSSES]
    PB --> EFFM
    PB --> EFFG
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 ŌĆö Retardation Test Sequence Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[STEP 1 MOTOR RUNS NO LOAD] --> B[STEP 2 RECORD INITIAL SPEED N1]
    B --> C[STEP 3 OPEN MAIN SWITCH]
    C --> D[STEP 4 KEEP FIELD ENERGISED]
    D --> E[STEP 5 ROTOR COASTS DOWN]
    E --> F[STEP 6 RECORD FINAL SPEED N2 AND TIME t]
    F --> G[STEP 7 COMPUTE P_r FROM J N dN by dt]
    G --> H[STEP 8 SECOND RUN AT DIFFERENT N]
    H --> I[STEP 9 SOLVE TWO EQUATIONS]
    I --> J[STEP 10 SEPARATE IRON AND FRICTION WINDAGE LOSSES]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 ŌĆö Loss Separation Logic Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    TOT[TOTAL ROTATIONAL LOSS P_r] --> IRON[IRON LOSS PROPORTIONAL TO N]
    TOT --> MECH[FRICTION PLUS WINDAGE LOSS PROPORTIONAL TO N RAISED TO K]
    RUN1[RUN 1 RATED FIELD SPEED N1] --> P1[P_r1 MEASURED]
    RUN2[RUN 2 REDUCED FIELD SPEED N1 SAME] --> P2[P_r2 MEASURED]
    P1 --> SUB[SUBTRACT P_r2 FROM P_r1]
    SUB --> FE[IRON LOSS = DIFFERENCE]
    P2 --> FW[FRICTION PLUS WINDAGE = P_r2]
    FW --> MECH
    FE --> IRON
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Adaptation Note:** For a Physical two-circuit Hopkinson schematic (with M, G, switches, and meters), the block-flow above conveys the *functional relationships* with greater clarity and is fully aligned with KTU 2024 answer-script expectations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**A1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Q: What is Swinburne's test? State its main assumption and one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Swinburne's test is an indirect method to predetermine the efficiency of a *DC shunt machine* at any load by running it at **no-load**. The test measures the **no-load input power**, which equals the **constant rotational losses** ($W_0$ = iron + friction + windage), assuming these are constant from no-load to full-load.

**Main Assumption:** Flux is constant at all loads (valid for shunt machines since $I_{sh} \approx$ constant).

**Limitation:** It cannot be applied to **series motors** (flux varies with load) and does not account for **armature reaction** (which reduces flux at high loads).

---

**A2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Q: Why is the retardation test called a "no-load" test? How is rotational loss separated into iron and friction components?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The retardation (run-down) test is called a no-load test because the machine is allowed to **coast freely** (motor is disconnected from supply) and only the rotational losses decelerate the rotor. The rate of decrease of kinetic energy equals the rotational loss:
$P_r = J (2\pi/60)^2 N \, dN/dt$.

**Separation:** Two runs are taken ŌĆö (1) with rated field excitation, and (2) with field unexcited (residual magnetism). Iron loss is negligible in run (2) because flux is very small, so the difference gives the iron loss. Alternatively, two-speed method uses $P_r = AN + BN^2$ and solves for $A$ (iron) and $B$ (friction+windage).

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Swinburne's + Numerical**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat connection diagram how Swinburne's test is conducted on a DC shunt machine. Derive the expression for efficiency at any load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **400 V, 50 kW** DC shunt motor has armature resistance **0.2 ╬®** and shunt field resistance **200 ╬®**. At no-load, the motor takes **8 A** at 400 V. Calculate:
(i) Rotational loss
(ii) Efficiency at full load
(iii) Efficiency at half load
Take stray load loss = 1% of full-load output. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Test Procedure:**
1. The DC shunt machine is connected to a DC supply at its **rated voltage $V$** through a voltmeter and an ammeter.
2. The armature and shunt field are connected in **parallel** across the supply.
3. The machine is run as a **motor at no-load** (no mechanical load on the shaft).
4. The **no-load line current $I_{L0}$** is recorded.
5. The **shunt field current** is calculated as $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$.

**Constant Loss Calculation:**
At no-load, the entire input is dissipated as losses:
$$V I_{L0} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} + I_{a0}^2 R_a + W_0$$
Since $I_{a0}$ is very small, $I_{a0}^2 R_a$ is negligible:
$$W_0 = V I_{L0} - I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$$

**Efficiency at Predetermined Load $I_L$:**
$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$$
$$\eta = \frac{V I_L}{V I_L + I_a^2 R_a + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} + W_0 + P_{\text{stray}}}$$

---

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Full-load current.**
$$I_{L,\text{full}} = \frac{50{,}000}{400} = 125\;\text{A}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Shunt field current and its loss.**
$$I_{sh} = \frac{400}{200} = 2\;\text{A}, \quad I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = 4 \times 200 = 800\;\text{W}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö No-load armature current.**
$$I_{a0} = 8 - 2 = 6\;\text{A}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rotational loss (neglecting tiny $I_{a0}^2 R_a = 36 \times 0.2 = 7.2$ W).**
$$W_0 = 400 \times 8 - 800 = 3200 - 800 = \mathbf{2400\;\text{W}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Stray load loss.**
$$P_{\text{stray}} = 0.01 \times 50{,}000 = 500\;\text{W}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Full-load efficiency.**
$$I_{a,\text{FL}} = 125 - 2 = 123\;\text{A}, \quad I_{a,\text{FL}}^2 R_a = 123^2 \times 0.2 = 3025.8\;\text{W}$$
$$\text{Total loss} = 3025.8 + 800 + 2400 + 500 = 6725.8\;\text{W}$$
$$\eta_{\text{FL}} = \frac{50{,}000}{50{,}000 + 6725.8} \times 100 = \mathbf{88.14\,\%}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Half-load efficiency.**
$$I_{L,0.5} = 62.5\;\text{A}, \quad I_a = 60.5\;\text{A}$$
$$I_a^2 R_a = 60.5^2 \times 0.2 = 732.05\;\text{W}, \quad P_{\text{stray}} = 0.01 \times 25{,}000 = 250\;\text{W}$$
$$\text{Total loss} = 732.05 + 800 + 2400 + 250 = 4182.05\;\text{W}$$
$$\eta_{0.5} = \frac{25{,}000}{25{,}000 + 4182.05} \times 100 = \mathbf{85.67\,\%}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Hopkinson's + Retardation Combined**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the Hopkinson's test for determining the efficiency of two identical DC shunt machines. Derive the efficiency expressions for motor and generator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a retardation test on a DC machine with moment of inertia **75 kg┬Ęm┬▓**, the speed drops from **1200 rpm** to **1180 rpm** in **30 s** with field normally excited. A second run with field weakened gives a drop from **1200 rpm** to **1190 rpm** in **30 s**. Separate the rotational loss into iron loss and friction-plus-windage loss at **1200 rpm**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Test Description:** Two identical DC shunt machines M and G are coupled mechanically on a common shaft. M is connected to the DC supply and runs as a motor. The armature of G is connected to the same supply bus in parallel, but its field is excited such that its generated EMF slightly exceeds the bus voltage, so it feeds power *back* to the bus (regenerative operation).

**Measurements:** $V$ (bus voltage), $I_L$ (line current from supply), $I_{am}, I_{ag}$ (armature currents), $I_{f1}, I_{f2}$ (field currents), $R_a, R_{sh}$ (known).

**Power Balance:**
$$V I_L = I_{am}^2 R_a + I_{ag}^2 R_a + I_{f1}^2 R_{sh} + I_{f2}^2 R_{sh} + 2W_0 + P_{\text{stray}}$$

**Motor Output and Efficiency:**
$$P_{\text{out,M}} = V I_{am} - \left(I_{am}^2 R_a + I_{f1}^2 R_{sh} + W_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}P_{\text{stray}}\right)$$
$$\eta_{\text{M}} = \frac{P_{\text{out,M}}}{V I_{am}} \times 100\,\%$$

**Generator Output and Efficiency:**
$$P_{\text{out,G}} = V I_{ag}$$
$$\eta_{\text{G}} = \frac{V I_{ag}}{V I_{ag} + I_{ag}^2 R_a + I_{f2}^2 R_{sh} + W_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}P_{\text{stray}}} \times 100\,\%$$

**Advantages:** No external load, very little power drawn from supply, true full-load testing possible.

---

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Run 1 (Rated Field):**
$$P_{r1} = \frac{J \pi^2 (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{1800\,t} = \frac{75 \pi^2 (1200^2 - 1180^2)}{1800 \times 30}$$
$$= \frac{75 \times 9.8696 \times (1{,}440{,}000 - 1{,}392{,}400)}{54{,}000}$$
$$= \frac{75 \times 9.8696 \times 47{,}600}{54{,}000} = \frac{35{,}234{,}616}{54{,}000} = 652.5\;\text{W}$$

**Run 2 (Weakened Field):**
$$P_{r2} = \frac{75 \pi^2 (1200^2 - 1190^2)}{1800 \times 30} = \frac{75 \times 9.8696 \times 23{,}900}{54{,}000} = 327.6\;\text{W}$$

**Interpretation:**
In Run 2, the field is weakened, so the flux is reduced, drastically reducing the iron loss. The mechanical (friction + windage) loss at the same speed (1200 rpm) is approximately the same in both runs. Hence:

$$P_{\text{iron}} \approx P_{r1} - P_{r2} = 652.5 - 327.6 = \mathbf{324.9\;\text{W}}$$

$$P_{\text{friction+windage}} \approx P_{r2} = \mathbf{327.6\;\text{W}}$$

**Check:** $P_{r1} = P_{\text{iron}} + P_{\text{f+w}} = 324.9 + 327.6 = 652.5$ W Ō£ō

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the shunt field copper loss** ($I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$) ŌĆö students often compute $W_0 = V I_{L0}$ directly, losing **1 mark** in Swinburne.
> 2. **Sign error in retardation test** ŌĆö $\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ is *negative*; take magnitude or compute $(N_1^2 - N_2^2)$ to keep $P_r$ positive. KTU deducts **1 mark** if sign is mishandled.
> 3. **Confusing $I_L$ with $I_a$** in efficiency formulae ŌĆö always use **armature current** for $I^2R$ losses.
> 4. **In Hopkinson's test, students often forget** that the stray load loss is split between the two machines OR doubled for the system. KTU convention: total $P_{\text{stray}} = 0.01 \times \text{output of one machine} \times 2$ for the system.
> 5. **Wrong moment of inertia units** ŌĆö $J$ must be in **kg┬Ęm┬▓**, $N$ in **rpm**, and time in **seconds**. The constant $\pi^2/1800$ comes from $\tfrac{1}{2}(2\pi/60)^2$ ŌĆö do not re-derive in the exam; just use the standard formula.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Swinburne's Test** = *no-load, single shunt machine*; constant losses = $W_0 \approx V I_{L0} - I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$. Predicts efficiency at **any load**.
- **Hopkinson's Test** = *back-to-back* regenerative test on **two identical** DC shunt machines. Total input = combined losses. Used for **full-load** testing without wastage.
- **Retardation Test** = *coast-down* test; $P_r = J(2\pi/60)^2 N \, dN/dt$ determines rotational losses. Used with the **two-speed** or **two-field** method to separate iron from friction + windage.
- **Iron loss** $\propto N$ (linear), **friction + windage** $\propto N^k$ ($k = 2$ to $3$, KTU uses $k = 2$).
- **Stray load loss** = **1% of full-load output** (KTU default assumption).
- **Flux is assumed constant** from no-load to full-load (valid for shunt machines, *not* series).
- **Armature reaction** reduces flux at high loads ŌĆö Swinburne's test under-estimates losses at loads above ~125% rated.
- The rotational loss in Swinburne / Hopkinson includes **iron + friction + windage + brush contact loss** (KTU usually groups brush loss with rotational loss).
- The retardation test is **inherently inaccurate** at very low speeds because $N_{avg}$ is hard to define; KTU prefers $N_{avg} = (N_1 + N_2)/2$ for small drops.
- **Memorize the formula** $P_r = \dfrac{J \pi^2 (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{1800\,t}$ ŌĆö it is the single most-tested KTU formula on this module.
- **Units mantra:** $J$ in kg┬Ęm┬▓, $N$ in rpm, $t$ in seconds, $P_r$ in Watts. The constant $1800 = 2 \times 900$ comes from $\left(\frac{2\pi}{60}\right)^2 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{2\pi}{60} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$ ŌĆö just remember the numerical form.
- **Practical Engineering Note:** IE3/IE4 premium motors achieve higher efficiency by reducing $W_0$ (better lamination steel, lower-friction bearings) ŌĆö this is the *direct industrial impact* of the loss-separation analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Phase Transformers - Constructional Details - Principle of Operation - EMF Equation - Ideal and Practical Transformer and Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET303 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single Phase Transformers - Constructional Details - Principle of Operation - EMF Equation - Ideal and Practical Transformer and Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Single Phase Transformer?

A **single phase transformer** is a static (stationary) electromagnetic device that transfers electrical energy from one alternating current (AC) circuit to another through the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction**, without any change in frequency. It operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and is the most essential component in power generation, transmission, and distribution systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A transformer is a static piece of apparatus which, by electromagnetic induction, transforms an alternating voltage and current from one value to another at the same frequency.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Water Pressure Reducer" Model

Imagine water flowing through a pipe at very high pressure. You cannot use that water directly to wash a delicate car because the pressure is too high. What do you insert? A **pressure reducer** (a step-down valve) that lowers the pressure while increasing the volume of flow. The total energy stays roughly the same, but the "form" changes.

A **transformer does exactly this with electricity**:
- **High Voltage (pressure) ŌåÆ Low Voltage (usable form)** is called a **Step-Down Transformer** (used in phone chargers, distribution transformers near homes).
- **Low Voltage ŌåÆ High Voltage** is called a **Step-Up Transformer** (used at power generating stations before transmitting electricity over long distances to reduce $I^2R$ losses).

> [!NOTE]
> The key insight: a transformer does NOT generate power; it merely **converts** voltage/current levels while keeping $P = V \times I$ approximately constant.

### 1.3 Why Transformers Matter in Power Engineering

- Power stations generate at **11 kV / 25 kV**.
- Transmission lines step up to **400 kV / 765 kV** to reduce current (and thus copper losses).
- Distribution near homes steps down to **11 kV ŌåÆ 415 V ŌåÆ 230 V** for safe domestic use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A transformer is **bi-directional** ŌĆö it can be used as step-up or step-down depending on which side is energized.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Units

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | $f$ | **Hz** (Hertz) |
| Magnetic Flux | $\Phi$ | **Wb** (Weber) |
| Maximum Flux | $\Phi_m$ | **Wb** |
| Number of Turns | $N_1, N_2$ | Dimensionless |
| Voltage | $V, E$ | **V** (Volt) |
| Transformation Ratio | $K$ or $a$ | Dimensionless |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Details of a Single Phase Transformer

A single phase transformer consists of the following major parts:

#### A. **Magnetic Core**
- Made of **laminated silicon steel (CRGO ŌĆö Cold Rolled Grain Oriented)** sheets.
- Lamination thickness: typically **0.35 mm**, insulated from each other by a thin varnish coating.
- **Purpose**: Provides a low reluctance path for magnetic flux; lamination reduces **eddy current losses**.

**Two types of core construction:**
1. **Core Type**: Windings surround a considerable part of the core. (e.g., distribution transformers)
2. **Shell Type**: Core surrounds a considerable portion of the windings. (e.g., power transformers)

#### B. **Windings**
- **Primary Winding**: Connected to the AC supply, has $N_1$ turns.
- **Secondary Winding**: Connected to the load, has $N_2$ turns.
- Made of **copper** (most common) or **aluminum** (cheaper, larger volume).
- Two types: **Concentric (cylindrical)** and **Sandwich (pancake/Disc)**.

#### C. **Tank, Oil & Conservator**
- **Tank**: Houses the core and windings; filled with **transformer oil** which serves three roles: insulation, cooling, and arc-quenching.
- **Conservator**: An auxiliary oil reservoir mounted above the tank, allowing oil to expand/contract with temperature.
- **Breather (Silica Gel)**: Absorbs moisture from air entering the conservator.

#### D. **Bushings**
- Insulated terminals (porcelain/ceramic) that safely bring the high-voltage windings out of the oil-filled tank.

#### E. **Tap Changer**
- Adjusts the turns ratio to maintain constant output voltage despite input voltage variations.
- **Off-Load Tap Changer (OLTC)** ŌĆö manual, used when transformer is de-energized.
- **On-Load Tap Changer (OLTC)** ŌĆö automatic, can change taps while energized.

#### F. **Cooling Tubes / Radiators**
- Attached to the tank externally; oil circulates through them via **natural convection (ONAN)** or **forced air (ONAF)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** Always draw or describe the labeled diagram of a single-phase transformer and identify any 6 parts with their functions.

### 2.2 Principle of Operation

The transformer works on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the concept of **mutual induction** between two magnetically coupled coils.

**Working Sequence:**
1. An alternating voltage $V_1$ is applied to the primary winding.
2. This produces an alternating primary current $I_1$, which creates an **alternating flux $\Phi$** in the core.
3. The alternating flux links the secondary winding (and also the primary).
4. By **Faraday's Law**, an EMF is induced in both windings:
   - Primary: $E_1$ (this is the back-EMF, opposing $V_1$)
   - Secondary: $E_2$ (this drives current $I_2$ through the load)
5. The frequency of induced EMF is **the same** as the supply frequency $f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A transformer has **no moving parts**. Energy transfer happens purely through the magnetic field ŌĆö there is no electrical connection between primary and secondary.

### 2.3 The EMF Equation ŌĆö The Heart of Transformer Analysis

Consider a single turn of the primary winding. The flux varies sinusoidally:

$$\Phi = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The instantaneous EMF induced in **one turn** is given by Faraday's law:

$$e = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}(\Phi_m \sin(\omega t))$$

$$e = -\omega \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

The **RMS value** of this sinusoidal EMF is:

$$E_{rms/turn} = \frac{\omega \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\pi f \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

For $N$ turns:

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE MASTER FORMULA ŌĆö KTU Examiner Favourite:**
> $$E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$$
> This is the **EMF Equation of a Transformer**. Commit it to memory with all four variables.

### 2.4 Voltage Ratio (Transformation Ratio, $K$)

For an **ideal transformer** (no losses):

$$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = K = a$$

- $K > 1$ ŌćÆ **Step-Down** ($E_1 > E_2$)
- $K < 1$ ŌćÆ **Step-Up** ($E_1 < E_2$)

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$ | Induced EMF in any winding | Volts (V) |
| $\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = K$ | Transformation ratio | Dimensionless |
| $\Phi_m = \frac{E}{4.44 \, f \, N}$ | Maximum flux from EMF | Weber (Wb) |
| $N = \frac{E}{4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m}$ | Number of turns for given $E, \Phi_m$ | Turns |
| $E_1 N_2 = E_2 N_1$ | Cross-multiplied form | ŌĆö |
| $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$ (ideal) | Power conservation | W (Watts) |
| $\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1}{K}$ | Current ratio (inverse of voltage) | ŌĆö |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING RULE:** In the table above, the vertical bar `\vert` in math expressions has been replaced to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.6 Ideal vs. Practical Transformer ŌĆö A Comparative Study

| Property | Ideal Transformer | Practical Transformer |
|---|---|---|
| Winding Resistance | Zero ($R_1 = R_2 = 0$) | Finite ($R_1, R_2 > 0$) |
| Core Losses | Zero (no hysteresis or eddy) | Present ($P_i$ iron loss) |
| Leakage Flux | Zero (100% coupling) | Present ($\Phi_{l1}, \Phi_{l2}$) |
| Magnetizing Current | Zero (no core MMF needed) | Finite (10ŌĆō20% of full load) |
| Efficiency | 100% always | 95ŌĆō99% |
| Voltage Regulation | Zero | Finite (1ŌĆō10%) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often treat a real transformer as ideal in numericals. Always verify whether the question specifies "ideal" or gives winding resistance, core loss, etc. If resistances are given, you MUST account for copper losses $I^2R$.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Transmission**: Step-up at generation, step-down at distribution.
- **Isolation**: 1:1 ratio transformers used for safety in medical equipment.
- **Instrument Transformers**: Current Transformers (CT) and Potential Transformers (PT) for metering.
- **Audio Systems**: Impedance matching between amplifiers and speakers.
- **Rectifier Circuits**: Step-down transformers feed diode bridges for DC supplies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EMF Equation

**Given:**
- A sinusoidal alternating flux: $\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$
- Number of turns in the winding: $N$
- Supply frequency: $f$ Hz
- Angular frequency: $\omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s

**Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** states that the EMF induced in a coil of $N$ turns is:

$$e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the expression for flux.

$$e = -N \frac{d}{dt}\left[\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to time. The constant $\Phi_m$ and $N$ are pulled out.

$$e = -N \Phi_m \, \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that the maximum value of $\cos(\omega t)$ is $1$, giving the peak EMF.

$$E_{max} = N \Phi_m \omega = N \Phi_m (2\pi f)$$

$$E_{max} = 2\pi f N \Phi_m$$

**Step 4:** For a sinusoidal waveform, the RMS value is obtained by dividing the peak value by $\sqrt{2}$.

$$E_{rms} = \frac{E_{max}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\pi f N \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the constant. Compute $\frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}}$.

$$\frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\pi \sqrt{2}}{2} = \pi \sqrt{2} \approx 4.44$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back to obtain the final result.

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N}$$

This is the **EMF equation of a transformer** (and indeed of any AC machine winding).

### 3.2 Derivation of Voltage Ratio

For an ideal transformer with no losses, the applied voltage equals the back-EMF in the primary:

$$V_1 = E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_1$$

Similarly, the induced EMF in the secondary:

$$E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_2$$

**Step 1:** Divide $E_1$ by $E_2$:

$$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_1}{4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_2}$$

**Step 2:** The constants $4.44$, $f$, and $\Phi_m$ cancel out:

$$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = K$$

This is the **transformation ratio** $K$ (also called turns ratio $a$).

### 3.3 Derivation of Current Ratio (Ideal Transformer)

From the principle of **conservation of energy** in an ideal transformer:

$$\text{Input Power} = \text{Output Power}$$

$$V_1 I_1 \cos\phi_1 = V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2$$

For an ideal transformer, $\cos\phi_1 = \cos\phi_2$, so:

$$V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange:

$$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{E_2}{E_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1}{K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Inverse Rule:** In a transformer, voltage and current ratios are **inversely proportional** to the turns ratio. If voltage steps up by 10├Ś, current steps down by 10├Ś.

### 3.4 Numerical Problem 1 (KTU Standard Style)

> **Problem:** A single-phase, 50 Hz transformer has 500 turns on the primary and 1000 turns on the secondary. The net cross-sectional area of the core is $200 \text{ cm}^2$. If the primary is connected to a 2000 V, 50 Hz supply, calculate:
> (a) The maximum flux density in the core.
> (b) The EMF induced in the secondary.
> (c) The primary and secondary full-load currents if the transformer is ideal and rated at 20 kVA.

**Given Data:**
- $f = 50$ Hz
- $N_1 = 500$, $N_2 = 1000$
- $A = 200 \text{ cm}^2 = 200 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.02 \text{ m}^2$
- $V_1 = 2000$ V
- $S = 20$ kVA $= 20{,}000$ VA

#### Part (a): Maximum Flux Density

**Step 1:** Use the EMF equation for the primary:

$$E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_1$$

**Step 2:** For an ideal transformer, $V_1 = E_1$:

$$2000 = 4.44 \times 50 \times \Phi_m \times 500$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\Phi_m$:

$$\Phi_m = \frac{2000}{4.44 \times 50 \times 500}$$

$$\Phi_m = \frac{2000}{111{,}000} = 0.01802 \text{ Wb}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate maximum flux density $B_m$:

$$B_m = \frac{\Phi_m}{A} = \frac{0.01802}{0.02}$$

$$\boxed{B_m = 0.901 \text{ T}}$$

#### Part (b): EMF Induced in Secondary

**Step 1:** Use the turns ratio:

$$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

$$\frac{2000}{E_2} = \frac{500}{1000} = 0.5$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $E_2$:

$$E_2 = \frac{2000}{0.5} = 4000 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_2 = 4000 \text{ V} \text{ (Step-Up Transformer)}}$$

#### Part (c): Full-Load Currents

**Step 1:** For an ideal transformer, $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2 = 20{,}000$ VA.

**Primary current:**

$$I_1 = \frac{S}{V_1} = \frac{20{,}000}{2000} = 10 \text{ A}$$

**Secondary current:**

$$I_2 = \frac{S}{V_2} = \frac{20{,}000}{4000} = 5 \text{ A}$$

**Verification using current ratio:**

$$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1000}{500} = 2$$

$$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{10}{5} = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{I_1 = 10 \text{ A}, \quad I_2 = 5 \text{ A}}$$

### 3.5 Numerical Problem 2 (Practical Transformer with Losses)

> **Problem:** A 5 kVA, 200 V/400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has the following test data:
> - Iron loss = 60 W
> - Full-load copper loss = 100 W
> Calculate the efficiency at (i) full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging and (ii) half load, unity p.f.

**Given Data:**
- Rating $S = 5000$ VA
- $V_1 = 200$ V, $V_2 = 400$ V
- $P_i = 60$ W (constant iron loss)
- $P_{cu,FL} = 100$ W

#### Part (i): Efficiency at Full Load, 0.8 p.f.

**Step 1:** Calculate output power at full load.

$$P_{out} = S \times \cos\phi = 5000 \times 0.8 = 4000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate total losses at full load.

$$P_{loss} = P_i + P_{cu,FL} = 60 + 100 = 160 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate input power.

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss} = 4000 + 160 = 4160 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate efficiency.

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{4000}{4160} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{FL} = 96.15\%}$$

#### Part (ii): Efficiency at Half Load, Unity p.f.

**Step 1:** Output power at half load, unity p.f.

$$P_{out} = 0.5 \times 5000 \times 1.0 = 2500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Copper loss at half load. Since $P_{cu} \propto I^2 \propto (\text{loading fraction})^2$:

$$P_{cu,HL} = (0.5)^2 \times 100 = 0.25 \times 100 = 25 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** Iron loss remains constant at all loads:

$$P_i = 60 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4:** Total losses at half load.

$$P_{loss} = 60 + 25 = 85 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5:** Efficiency.

$$\eta_{HL} = \frac{2500}{2500 + 85} \times 100 = \frac{2500}{2585} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{HL} = 96.71\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Efficiency at half load (96.71%) is **higher** than at full load (96.15%) for this particular transformer because the load is below the "knee point" where iron and copper losses are equal. This is why transformers are designed to operate near full load most of the time.

### 3.6 Condition for Maximum Efficiency

Setting $\frac{d\eta}{dI} = 0$ leads to the famous result:

$$P_i = x^2 P_{cu,FL}$$

Where $x$ is the fraction of full load. Solving for $x$:

$$\boxed{x = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}}}}$$

This gives the **loading fraction at maximum efficiency**. KTU frequently asks students to verify this condition numerically.

---

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Energy Conversion Path of a Single-Phase Transformer

```mermaid

    ACSupply["AC SUPPLY V1 f Hz"] -->|Connects to| PrimTerm["PRIMARY TERMINALS"]
    PrimTerm -->|Feeds current| PrimWind["PRIMARY WINDING N1 turns R1 resistance"]
    PrimWind -->|Produces flux| Core["LAMINATED CORE CRGO Steel"]
    Core -->|Links flux to| SecWind["SECONDARY WINDING N2 turns R2 resistance"]
    Core -->|Leakage flux| Leak1["LEAKAGE FLUX phi l1"]
    Core -->|Leakage flux| Leak2["LEAKAGE FLUX phi l2"]
    SecWind -->|Induces EMF| SecTerm["SECONDARY TERMINALS E2"]
    SecTerm -->|Drives| Load["ELECTRICAL LOAD ZL"]
    PrimWind -->|Hysteresis loss| CoreLoss1["CORE LOSS Pi iron loss"]
    PrimWind -->|I squared R loss| CuLoss1["COPPER LOSS I1 squared R1"]
    SecWind -->|I squared R loss| CuLoss2["COPPER LOSS I2 squared R2"]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a transformer. State the principle of operation of a single-phase transformer.**

**Model Answer:**
A transformer is a static electromagnetic device that transfers AC electrical energy from one circuit to another at the same frequency through the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction**, without any change in frequency.

**Principle of Operation:**
When an alternating voltage $V_1$ is applied across the primary winding, it draws a small magnetizing current which produces an alternating flux $\Phi$ in the laminated iron core. This flux links with the secondary winding and, by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, induces an EMF $E_2$ in the secondary. The magnitude of $E_2$ depends on the turns ratio $N_2/N_1$. The frequency of induced EMF equals the supply frequency $f$.

$$\text{Mathematical Statement: } E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_2$$

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Write the EMF equation of a transformer and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\boxed{E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N}$$

Where:
- $E$ = RMS value of induced EMF in volts (V)
- $4.44$ = Form factor constant (derived from $2\pi/\sqrt{2}$ for sinusoidal flux)
- $f$ = Supply frequency in Hertz (Hz)
- $\Phi_m$ = Maximum (peak) value of mutual flux in Weber (Wb)
- $N$ = Number of turns in the winding

This equation is fundamental in determining the number of turns required for a given voltage rating or, conversely, the flux density in the core for a given design.

---

### 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Use diagrams and step-by-step derivations for full credit.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat labeled diagram, explain the constructional details of a single-phase transformer. State the materials used for core and windings and give reasons.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction Diagram (refer to Mermaid Section 4.3 above for layout)**

A single-phase transformer has the following major parts:

| Part | Function | Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Core** | Provides low-reluctance path for flux; laminated to reduce eddy current losses. | CRGO silicon steel, 0.35 mm laminations, insulated with varnish. |
| **Primary Winding** | Receives AC supply, produces alternating flux. | Copper (high conductivity) or aluminum. |
| **Secondary Winding** | Delivers power to load; flux linkage induces EMF. | Copper or aluminum. |
| **Tank** | Houses core and windings; contains transformer oil for insulation and cooling. | Mild steel. |
| **Conservator** | Allows oil expansion with temperature. | Mild steel cylindrical drum. |
| **Breather** | Silica gel absorbs moisture from air entering conservator. | Porcelain container with silica gel. |
| **Bushings** | Insulated terminals to bring out HV/LV winding connections. | Porcelain/ceramic. |
| **Tap Changer** | Adjusts turns ratio to regulate voltage. | Copper contacts. |
| **Radiators** | Dissipate heat from oil to atmosphere. | Mild steel tubes. |
| **Buchholz Relay** | Detects gas from internal faults. | Cast iron housing. |

**Material Justification:**
- **Silicon steel for core**: Silicon (3-4%) increases electrical resistivity (reducing eddy currents) and reduces hysteresis loss.
- **Lamination**: Each lamination is insulated to break eddy current paths.
- **Copper for windings**: High conductivity, ductile, good thermal properties.
- **Transformer oil**: High dielectric strength, good heat transfer, arc-quenching.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the EMF equation of a single-phase transformer. A 10 kVA, 2000/200 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has 80 turns on the secondary winding. Calculate:
(i) The number of primary and secondary turns.
(ii) The maximum flux in the core.
(iii) The approximate cross-sectional area of the core if the maximum flux density is 1.2 T.

**Model Solution:**

**EMF Derivation:**

Given sinusoidal flux: $\Phi = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$

By Faraday's Law, instantaneous EMF in $N$ turns:

$$e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N \Phi_m \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

Maximum (peak) EMF: $E_{max} = N \Phi_m \omega = 2\pi f N \Phi_m$

RMS value: $E_{rms} = \frac{E_{max}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\pi f N \Phi_m}{\sqrt{2}} = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $S = 10{,}000$ VA, $V_1 = 2000$ V, $V_2 = 200$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $N_2 = 80$ turns, $B_m = 1.2$ T.

**(i) Number of turns:**

$$K = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{2000}{200} = 10$$

$$N_1 = K \times N_2 = 10 \times 80 = 800 \text{ turns}$$

$$\boxed{N_1 = 800, \quad N_2 = 80}$$

**(ii) Maximum flux in the core:**

From $V_1 = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N_1$:

$$2000 = 4.44 \times 50 \times \Phi_m \times 800$$

$$\Phi_m = \frac{2000}{4.44 \times 50 \times 800} = \frac{2000}{177{,}600}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_m = 0.01126 \text{ Wb} = 11.26 \text{ mWb}}$$

**(iii) Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\Phi_m}{B_m} = \frac{0.01126}{1.2}$$

$$\boxed{A = 0.00938 \text{ m}^2 = 93.8 \text{ cm}^2}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between an ideal transformer and a practical transformer. What are the various losses occurring in a practical transformer?

**Model Solution:**

**Ideal vs Practical Transformer:**

| Feature | Ideal Transformer | Practical Transformer |
|---|---|---|
| Winding resistance | Zero | Finite ($R_1, R_2$) |
| Leakage flux | Zero (perfect coupling) | Present ($\Phi_{l1}, \Phi_{l2}$) |
| Core losses | Zero | Hysteresis + Eddy current |
| Magnetizing current | Zero | Finite (2-5% of FL) |
| Voltage regulation | 0% | 1-10% |
| Efficiency | 100% | 95-99% |

**Losses in a Practical Transformer:**

1. **Iron/Core Losses ($P_i$)** ŌĆö Occur in the magnetic core, depend on $V$ and $f$, independent of load.
   - **Hysteresis Loss**: $P_h = \eta B_m^{1.6} f V$
   - **Eddy Current Loss**: $P_e = k_e B_m^2 f^2 t^2 V$
2. **Copper/Ohmic Losses ($P_{cu}$)** ŌĆö Occur in windings, $I^2R$, vary with load squared.
3. **Stray Load Losses** ŌĆö Due to leakage flux in structural parts.
4. **Mechanical Losses** ŌĆö Bearing friction (in rotating machines only ŌĆö not in transformers).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 50 kVA, 4400/220 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has an iron loss of 500 W and a full-load copper loss of 800 W. Determine:
(i) The efficiency at full load, 0.85 p.f. lagging.
(ii) The efficiency at half load, 0.85 p.f. lagging.
(iii) The loading at which maximum efficiency occurs.
(iv) The maximum efficiency at 0.85 p.f. lagging.

**Model Solution:**

Given: $S = 50{,}000$ VA, $P_i = 500$ W, $P_{cu,FL} = 800$ W, $\cos\phi = 0.85$.

**(i) Full load, 0.85 p.f.:**

$$P_{out,FL} = 50{,}000 \times 0.85 = 42{,}500 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{loss,FL} = 500 + 800 = 1300 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{42{,}500}{42{,}500 + 1300} \times 100 = \frac{42{,}500}{43{,}800} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{FL} = 97.03\%}$$

**(ii) Half load, 0.85 p.f.:**

$$P_{out,HL} = 0.5 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.85 = 21{,}250 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{cu,HL} = (0.5)^2 \times 800 = 200 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{loss,HL} = 500 + 200 = 700 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{HL} = \frac{21{,}250}{21{,}250 + 700} \times 100 = \frac{21{,}250}{21{,}950} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{HL} = 96.81\%}$$

**(iii) Loading for maximum efficiency:**

$$\boxed{x = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}}} = \sqrt{\frac{500}{800}} = \sqrt{0.625} = 0.791 \text{ (or 79.1\% of full load)}}$$

**(iv) Maximum efficiency at 0.85 p.f.:**

At max efficiency, $P_{cu} = P_i = 500$ W.

$$P_{out,max} = 0.791 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.85 = 33{,}617.5 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{loss,max} = 2 \times 500 = 1000 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{33{,}617.5}{33{,}617.5 + 1000} \times 100 = \frac{33{,}617.5}{34{,}617.5} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 97.11\%}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in this topic (KTU Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant 4.44**: Writing $E = f \Phi N$ instead of $E = 4.44 f \Phi_m N$ ŌĆö loses 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Unit errors**: Flux in Wb, Area in $\text{m}^2$ (NOT $\text{cm}^2$). Converting $200 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.02 \text{ m}^2$ is a common step students skip.
> 3. **Iron loss treated as variable**: In efficiency problems, $P_i$ is **constant** for all loads. Only $P_{cu}$ changes with load fraction squared.
> 4. **Confusing step-up vs step-down**: $N_1 < N_2$ ŌćÆ step-up; $N_1 > N_2$ ŌćÆ step-down. Many students get this reversed.
> 5. **No diagram**: KTU requires a "neat labeled diagram" for full marks in construction questions. Skipping it = -3 to -4 marks.
> 6. **Assuming ideal transformer when given resistance**: If the question gives $R_1, R_2$, you must include copper loss $I^2R$ in efficiency.
> 7. **Wrong maximum efficiency condition**: Maximum efficiency occurs when $P_i = P_{cu}$, NOT when $P_{out} = P_{loss}$ (these are different statements, though related).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST (Must memorize for KTU exam):**

- [ ] **Definition**: A transformer is a static device that transfers AC power at constant frequency via mutual induction.
- [ ] **EMF Equation**: $E = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, N$ ŌĆö remember the magic constant 4.44.
- [ ] **Transformation Ratio**: $K = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{I_2}{I_1}$.
- [ ] **Voltage-Current Inverse Rule**: $\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$ AND $\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$.
- [ ] **Core Material**: CRGO silicon steel, 0.35 mm laminations.
- [ ] **Winding Material**: Copper (preferred) or aluminum.
- [ ] **Two Core Types**: Core type (winding surrounds core limb) and Shell type (core surrounds winding).
- [ ] **Two Winding Types**: Concentric (cylindrical) and Sandwich (pancake).
- [ ] **Cooling Methods**: ONAN, ONAF, OFAF, OFWF.
- [ ] **Key Parts**: Conservator, breather (silica gel), bushings, tap changer, Buchholz relay, radiators, oil gauge.
- [ ] **Iron Loss**: $P_i = P_h + P_e$ ŌĆö depends on $V$ and $f$, **independent of load**.
- [ ] **Copper Loss**: $P_{cu} = I^2 R$ ŌĆö depends on load, varies as (load fraction)$^2$.
- [ ] **Maximum Efficiency Condition**: $P_i = x^2 P_{cu,FL}$, so $x = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,FL}}$.
- [ ] **Ideal Transformer**: Zero losses, 100% coupling, 100% efficiency, zero voltage regulation.
- [ ] **Practical Transformer**: Has iron + copper losses, leakage flux, finite magnetizing current.
- [ ] **Frequency invariance**: Output frequency = Input frequency = supply frequency.
- [ ] **No moving parts**: Transformer is a STATIC device ŌĆö key distinguishing feature from motors/generators.
- [ ] **Flux density formula**: $B_m = \Phi_m / A$ where $A$ in $\text{m}^2$.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes ŌĆö Single Phase Transformers (Part 1).** This covers construction, principle, EMF equation, and ideal/practical transformer analysis with numericals. Module 3 continuation (if any) would cover equivalent circuits, phasor diagrams, OC/SC tests, and voltage regulation in detail.

# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Transformer Under Operational Study

A **transformer** is a static electromagnetic device that transfers electrical energy between two or more circuits through **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, without a change in frequency. In the KTU 2024 scheme (Module 3), our attention is restricted to a **single-phase, two-winding transformer** operating in **sinusoidal steady state**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box**
> A transformer operates on **mutual induction** between two electrically isolated but magnetically coupled coils wound on a common laminated ferromagnetic core. The winding receiving power is the **primary** (NŌéü turns) and the winding delivering power to the load is the **secondary** (NŌéé turns).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Water-Pressure Gearbox"

Imagine two water wheels connected by a common mechanical shaft. The first wheel is large, turning slowly under low pressure but delivering large volume. The second wheel is small, turning fast under high pressure but with smaller volume. Energy is transferred without any water leaking from one side to the other.

A transformer is the **electrical twin** of this:
- **Primary** (large wheel) ŌåÆ many turns ŌåÆ low current, high voltage.
- **Secondary** (small wheel) ŌåÆ few turns ŌåÆ high current, low voltage (for a step-down).
- **Core flux** ŌåÆ the shared mechanical shaft.
- **No physical contact** between primary and secondary ŌĆö only magnetic coupling.

---

## 1.3 The Three Operating States Studied

| State | Secondary Load | Primary Current | Why It Matters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **No-Load** | Open-circuited | Small **magnetizing current IŌéĆ** | Sets up core flux; defines core losses |
| **Loaded (Unity pf)** | Resistive R | IŌéü = IŌéĆ + IŌééŌĆ▓ | Reference for benchmarking |
| **Loaded (Lagging pf)** | Inductive R-L | IŌéü > IŌééŌĆ▓ | Causes **voltage drop**, real engineering case |
| **Loaded (Leading pf)** | Capacitive R-C | IŌéü < IŌééŌĆ▓ | Causes **voltage rise** (Ferranti effect on small lines) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module-3 syllabus explicitly asks students to draw phasor diagrams for **unity, lagging, and leading power factor loads** and to derive the **equivalent circuit referred to either side**. Voltage regulation is a **directly asked 7ŌĆō14 mark** concept.

---

## 1.4 Key Nomenclature (Strict KTU Convention)

- **EŌéü, EŌéé** : Induced EMFs in primary & secondary (RMS, V)
- **VŌéü, VŌéé** : Terminal voltages across primary & secondary (V)
- **NŌéü, NŌéé** : Number of turns
- **╬”Ōéś** : Maximum (peak) value of mutual core flux (Wb)
- **IŌéĆ** : No-load primary current (A)
- **I_w** : Working (core-loss) component of IŌéĆ ŌĆö in phase with flux
- **I_╬╝** : Magnetizing component of IŌéĆ ŌĆö quadrature to flux
- **K = NŌéé / NŌéü** : Transformation ratio
- **RŌéü, XŌéü** : Primary winding resistance & leakage reactance (╬®)
- **RŌéé, XŌéé** : Secondary winding resistance & leakage reactance (╬®)

# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 No-Load Operation (Open-Circuited Secondary)

When the secondary is **open**, no current flows in it. A small alternating current **IŌéĆ** still flows in the primary because the alternating applied voltage **VŌéü** must establish the alternating flux **╬”** in the core.

The applied voltage VŌéü is balanced by three internal EMFs:

1. The self-induced EMF **EŌéü** opposing VŌéü (Lenz's law).
2. A small resistive drop **IŌéĆ RŌéü** (ohmic heating of primary copper).
3. A small leakage-reactance drop **IŌéĆ XŌéü** (flux that links only the primary).

By **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** (KVL):

$$V_1 = -E_1 + I_0 R_1 + j I_0 X_1$$

Since IŌéĆ is only **2ŌĆō10 %** of full-load current, the IŌéĆRŌéü and IŌéĆXŌéü drops are tiny. Hence:

$$V_1 \approx -E_1$$

The current IŌéĆ is composed of two orthogonal components:
- **Working component I_w = IŌéĆ cos ŽåŌéĆ** ŌĆö supplies hysteresis + eddy-current losses in the core (in phase with VŌéü).
- **Magnetizing component I_╬╝ = IŌéĆ sin ŽåŌéĆ** ŌĆö establishes the flux ╬” (lagging VŌéü by 90┬░).

---

## 2.2 On-Load Operation (Loaded Secondary)

When a load Z_L is connected across the secondary:

1. **Secondary current IŌéé** flows, governed by:  IŌéé = EŌéé / (ZŌéé + Z_L)
2. This secondary current sets up its own **magnetomotive force (MMF) = NŌéé IŌéé**.
3. By **Lenz's law**, this MMF opposes the main core flux.
4. To maintain ╬” nearly constant (because VŌéü Ōēł EŌéü Ōēł 4.44 f NŌéü ╬”Ōéś), the primary draws **additional current IŌééŌĆ▓** such that:

$$N_1 I_2' = N_2 I_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_2' = \frac{N_2}{N_1} I_2 = K \cdot I_2$$

Hence the total primary current becomes:

$$I_1 = I_0 + I_2'$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MMF Balance Equation (the heart of the transformer):**
> $$N_1 I_1 = N_0 I_0 + N_2 I_2$$
> where NŌéĆIŌéĆ is the no-load MMF needed to sustain flux against core losses.

---

## 2.3 EMF Equation ŌĆö The Master Formula

By Faraday's law, the RMS EMF induced in a winding of N turns linked with sinusoidally varying flux ╬”Ōéś at frequency f is:

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m \, B_{\max}$$

**Derivation intuition:** Flux varies as ╬”(t) = ╬”Ōéś sin(Žēt). Induced EMF per turn = ŌłÆd╬”/dt = Žē ╬”Ōéś cos(Žēt). Peak EMF per turn = Žē╬”Ōéś = 2ŽĆf╬”Ōéś. RMS per turn = 2ŽĆf╬”Ōéś / ŌłÜ2 = 4.44 f ╬”Ōéś. Multiply by N turns.

Hence:

$$\boxed{E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \Phi_m \quad ; \quad E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \Phi_m}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{1}{K}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Print This and Pin It

| # | Formula | Meaning | Boundary / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E_1 = 4.44 f N_1 \Phi_m$ | Primary EMF (RMS) | Assumes sinusoidal flux |
| 2 | $E_2 = 4.44 f N_2 \Phi_m$ | Secondary EMF (RMS) | Valid for ideal & practical |
| 3 | $K = N_2 / N_1$ | Transformation ratio | > 1 step-down, < 1 step-up |
| 4 | $V_2 = E_2 - I_2(R_2 \cos\phi_2 + X_2 \sin\phi_2)$ | Loaded secondary V | For **lagging pf** |
| 5 | $I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0$ | Core-loss component | In phase with VŌéü |
| 6 | $I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0$ | Magnetizing component | 90┬░ behind VŌéü |
| 7 | $R_{01} = R_1 + K^2 R_2$ | Total resistance referred to primary | Used in approx. eq. circuit |
| 8 | $X_{01} = X_1 + K^2 X_2$ | Total leakage reactance ref. primary | Used in approx. eq. circuit |
| 9 | $R_{02} = R_2 + R_1/K^2$ | Total resistance referred to secondary | Mirror of #7 |
| 10 | $X_{02} = X_2 + X_1/K^2$ | Total leakage reactance ref. secondary | Mirror of #8 |
| 11 | $\text{VR \%} = \dfrac{V_{2,\text{NL}} - V_{2,\text{FL}}}{V_{2,\text{FL}}} \times 100$ | Voltage regulation | **Always positive** for lagging pf |
| 12 | $\text{VR} \approx \dfrac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi_2 + X_{02}\sin\phi_2)}{V_2} \times 100$ | Approximate VR | Lagging pf; ┬▒ sign for leading |
| 13 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2 + P_i + P_{cu}}$ | Efficiency | Module 4 but useful here |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Trick ŌĆö Sign in VR formula:** For **lagging pf**, both cos ŽåŌéé and sin ŽåŌéé are positive. For **leading pf**, sin ŽåŌéé is **negative**, which can produce a **negative VR** (i.e., VŌéé,FL > VŌéé,NL).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Equivalent circuits and voltage regulation calculations are used by **transformer designers** to predict the **secondary voltage drop** at customer premises under varying load. Power utilities in Kerala (KSEB) use this exact analysis to size distribution transformers for localities where industrial loads cause poor power factor.

# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation ŌĆö Voltage Regulation (Approximate Method)

We start from the KVL on the secondary side, neglecting the small IŌéĆ drops but keeping the load drops.

**Setting up the phasor:** Take flux ╬” as the reference phasor. The induced EMFs EŌéü and EŌéé lag ╬” by 90┬░. For a lagging load with power factor cos ŽåŌéé, the secondary current IŌéé lags VŌéé by ŽåŌéé.

The terminal voltage equation (referred entirely to secondary):

$$V_2 = E_2 - I_2(R_2 + j X_2)$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Decompose IŌéé into rectangular components:**

$$I_2 = I_2(\cos\phi_2 - j\sin\phi_2)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Multiply by (RŌéé + jXŌéé):**

$$I_2(R_2 + jX_2) = I_2 R_2 \cos\phi_2 + I_2 X_2 \sin\phi_2 + j(I_2 X_2 \cos\phi_2 - I_2 R_2 \sin\phi_2)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Drop the quadrature component** (because VŌéé Ōēł EŌéé in magnitude and we are computing small voltage drops):

$$V_2 \approx E_2 - I_2 R_2 \cos\phi_2 - I_2 X_2 \sin\phi_2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute EŌéé = VŌéé,NL:**

$$V_{2,\text{NL}} - V_{2,\text{FL}} = I_2 R_2 \cos\phi_2 + I_2 X_2 \sin\phi_2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Divide by VŌéé,FL and multiply by 100:**

$$\boxed{\text{VR \%} = \frac{I_2(R_2 \cos\phi_2 + X_2 \sin\phi_2)}{V_2} \times 100}$$

For the **referred-to-primary** form, replace RŌéé ŌåÆ R'Ōéé = K┬▓RŌéé, XŌéé ŌåÆ K┬▓XŌéé, IŌéé ŌåÆ IŌéü (load component), VŌéé ŌåÆ VŌéü/K.

---

## 3.2 Derivation ŌĆö Equivalent Circuit Referred to Primary

**Starting point** ŌĆö Exact T-equivalent circuit has RŌéü, XŌéü on the primary side, RŌéé, XŌéé on the secondary side, and the shunt branch (Rc Ōłź jXm) at the junction.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Shift secondary impedances to the primary side.**

A winding of NŌéé turns carrying current IŌéé has the same MMF effect as a winding of NŌéü turns carrying IŌééŌĆ▓. To preserve the voltageŌĆōcurrent relationships:

$$I_2' = K I_2 \quad ; \quad V_2' = K V_2$$

The secondary impedance transforms as:

$$Z_2' = \frac{V_2'}{I_2'} = \frac{K V_2}{K I_2} = \frac{V_2}{I_2} \cdot K = K \cdot Z_2$$

But careful with algebra ŌĆö using K = NŌéé/NŌéü:

$$Z_2' = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 Z_2 = \frac{Z_2}{K^2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Combine series impedances:**

$$R_{01} = R_1 + \frac{R_2}{K^2} \quad ; \quad X_{01} = X_1 + \frac{X_2}{K^2}$$

(equivalent forms: RŌéé' = K┬▓RŌéé and XŌéé' = K┬▓XŌéé when shifted the other way).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Approximate equivalent circuit:**

Move the shunt branch (Rc Ōłź jXm) to the **input terminals**. The error is negligible because IŌéĆ Ōē¬ I_FL. Result:

$$R_{01} + j X_{01} \quad \text{in series with the load } Z_L$$

This is the **approximate equivalent circuit referred to primary** that KTU loves to ask.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Phasor Diagram Cases

| Load Type | cos ŽåŌéé | sin ŽåŌéé | Direction of IŌéé vs VŌéé | Result for VR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unity pf (R)** | +1 | 0 | In phase with VŌéé | Moderate positive VR |
| **Lagging pf (R-L)** | +cos ŽåŌéé | +sin ŽåŌéé | Lags VŌéé by ŽåŌéé | Largest positive VR |
| **Leading pf (R-C)** | +cos ŽåŌéé | ŌłÆsin ŽåŌéé | Leads VŌéé by ŽåŌéé | Small or **negative** VR |
| **Pure Capacitive** | 0 | ŌłÆ1 | Leads VŌéé by 90┬░ | Strongly negative VR |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problems (Full Solutions)

### Numerical Problem 1 ŌĆö Voltage Regulation at Lagging pf

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Modified]**
> A 10 kVA, 2000/200 V, single-phase transformer has the following parameters referred to primary:
> RŌéĆŌéü = 5 ╬®, XŌéĆŌéü = 12 ╬®. The no-load current is 0.5 A at a power factor of 0.2 lagging.
> Determine (a) the full-load voltage regulation at 0.8 pf lagging, and (b) the secondary terminal voltage at full load.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute full-load primary current:**

$$I_{1,\text{FL}} = \frac{\text{kVA} \times 1000}{V_1} = \frac{10\,000}{2000} = 5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the approximate voltage-regulation formula** (referred to primary side):

$$\text{VR} = \frac{I_1(R_{01}\cos\phi_2 + X_{01}\sin\phi_2)}{V_1} \times 100$$

Given cos ŽåŌéé = 0.8 lagging, so sin ŽåŌéé = +0.6.

$$I_1 R_{01} \cos\phi_2 = 5 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 20 \text{ V}$$

$$I_1 X_{01} \sin\phi_2 = 5 \times 12 \times 0.6 = 36 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total voltage drop:**

$$\Delta V = 20 + 36 = 56 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Voltage regulation percentage:**

$$\text{VR \%} = \frac{56}{2000} \times 100 = 2.8 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Secondary full-load voltage:**

Referred drop on secondary = ╬öV ├Ś (VŌéé/VŌéü) = 56 ├Ś (200/2000) = 5.6 V

$$V_{2,\text{FL}} = V_{2,\text{NL}} - 5.6 = 200 - 5.6 = 194.4 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Allocation Insight (7 marks):** Step 1 (1 m) + Step 2ŌĆō3 substitution (2 m) + Step 4 answer (1 m) + Step 5 VŌéé (2 m) + Units (1 m).

---

### Numerical Problem 2 ŌĆö Voltage Regulation at Leading pf

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Modified]**
> For the same transformer as above, find the voltage regulation at 0.8 pf **leading**.

**Solution:**

The only change is **sin ŽåŌéé = ŌłÆ0.6** because the load is leading.

$$I_1 R_{01} \cos\phi_2 = 5 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 20 \text{ V} \quad (\text{unchanged})$$

$$I_1 X_{01} \sin\phi_2 = 5 \times 12 \times (-0.6) = -36 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V = 20 - 36 = -16 \text{ V}$$

$$\text{VR \%} = \frac{-16}{2000} \times 100 = -0.8 \text{ \%}$$

**Interpretation:** The negative sign means VŌéé,FL > VŌéé,NL (secondary voltage **rises** under load). The transformer behaves like a **ferranti-capacitive** system.

$$V_{2,\text{FL}} = 200 - (-1.6) = 201.6 \text{ V}$$

---

### Numerical Problem 3 ŌĆö Finding Equivalent Circuit Parameters from Tests

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Jun 2023]**
> A 5 kVA, 400/200 V transformer gave the following test results:
> **SC test** (LV shorted): 20 V, 10 A, 60 W
> **OC test** (HV open): 200 V, 1 A, 40 W
> Determine the equivalent circuit parameters referred to the HV side.

**Solution:**

The SC test gives the equivalent series impedance (referred to the side that is shorted ŌĆö LV here):

$$Z_{e,\text{LV}} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{20}{10} = 2 \, \Omega$$

$$R_{e,\text{LV}} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.6 \, \Omega$$

$$X_{e,\text{LV}} = \sqrt{Z_e^2 - R_e^2} = \sqrt{4 - 0.36} = \sqrt{3.64} \approx 1.91 \, \Omega$$

**Transformation ratio** K = NŌéé/NŌéü = 200/400 = 0.5 (step-down with LV secondary).

**Refer everything to HV (primary):**

$$R_{01} = \frac{R_{e,\text{LV}}}{K^2} = \frac{0.6}{0.25} = 2.4 \, \Omega$$

$$X_{01} = \frac{X_{e,\text{LV}}}{K^2} = \frac{1.91}{0.25} = 7.64 \, \Omega$$

**OC test** (HV open means HV is energized ŌåÆ already on HV side):

$$I_0 = 1 \text{ A}, \quad \cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_{oc}}{V_{oc} I_0} = \frac{40}{200 \times 1} = 0.2$$

$$R_c = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{P_{oc}} = \frac{200^2}{40} = 1000 \, \Omega$$

$$I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 = 0.2 \text{ A}, \quad I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{1-0.04} = 0.98 \text{ A}$$

$$X_m = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_\mu} = \frac{200}{0.98} \approx 204 \, \Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Board Examiner Note):** Students forget to square K when shifting impedances. Always remember: impedance shifts by **K┬▓**, voltage by **K**, current by **1/K**.

## PART A ŌĆö 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]
**Define voltage regulation of a transformer. State its typical range for a well-designed transformer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Voltage regulation is the **change in secondary terminal voltage** when a specified load is applied at a given power factor, expressed as a percentage of the full-load secondary voltage, with primary voltage held constant at rated value.

$$\text{VR \%} = \frac{V_{2,\text{NL}} - V_{2,\text{FL}}}{V_{2,\text{FL}}} \times 100$$

For well-designed distribution transformers, regulation is typically between **2 % and 5 %** for unity pf and rises to **5ŌĆō8 %** at 0.8 pf lagging.

> *(Marks: 1 m definition + 1 m formula + 1 m range)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]
**Distinguish between the magnetizing current I_╬╝ and the working component I_w of the no-load current IŌéĆ.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The no-load current IŌéĆ has two orthogonal components:

| Component | Function | Phasor Relation | Power |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Magnetizing I_╬╝** | Produces the alternating core flux ╬” | Quadrature (90┬░) with VŌéü; **lags** VŌéü | Reactive (no real power) |
| **Working I_w** | Supplies core losses (hysteresis + eddy) | **In phase** with VŌéü | Real power = VŌéüI_w |

$$I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_\mu^2}, \quad \cos\phi_0 = \frac{I_w}{I_0}$$

> *(Marks: 1 m each component + 1 m phasor/power relation)*
