**(b)** Two identical 20 kVA, 2000 V / 200 V, 50 Hz transformers are tested by Sumpner's method. With secondary loop open, WŌéü = 400 W. With secondary loop closed and full-load current circulated, WŌéé = 600 W. Calculate the efficiency of each transformer at:
- (i) Full load, unity power factor
- (ii) Half load, 0.8 power factor lagging **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Description of Sumpner's Test (7 marks)

**Connection Diagram (described textually):**

1. The **primary windings** of two identical transformers TŌéü and TŌéé are connected in **parallel** across a single-phase AC supply of rated voltage and rated frequency. A voltmeter, ammeter, and wattmeter WŌéü are connected in the primary circuit.

2. The **secondary windings** are connected in **series** with each other in **opposition** (i.e., the polarities are arranged so that their induced EMFs cancel when the loop is closed). An auxiliary low-voltage AC source is connected into this series loop through an ammeter and wattmeter WŌéé.

**Test Procedure:**

**Step 1: Secondary loop open.** Apply **rated voltage** to the parallel primaries.
- The voltmeter reads rated voltage.
- The ammeter reads a small no-load current (sum of two no-load currents).
- Wattmeter WŌéü reads the **total iron losses of both transformers**: $W_1 = 2P_i$.
- (Copper losses are negligible because $I_0$ is tiny.)

**Step 2: Secondary loop closed.** Keep primary supply at rated voltage. Energize the auxiliary source in the secondary loop and adjust it to circulate **full-load current** through the series secondaries.
- The secondary ammeter reads $I_{FL}$.
- Wattmeter WŌéé reads the **total full-load copper losses of both transformers**: $W_2 = 2P_{cu, FL}$.

**Derivation of Loss Expressions:**

Per transformer:
- Iron loss: $P_i = W_1/2$
- Full-load copper loss: $P_{cu, FL} = W_2/2$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Efficiency Calculations (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $W_1 = 400$ W (iron loss of both transformers)
- $W_2 = 600$ W (FL copper loss of both transformers)

**Per Transformer:**
- $P_i = 400/2 = 200$ W
- $P_{cu, FL} = 600/2 = 300$ W

**(i) Efficiency at Full Load, Unity PF:**

$$P_{out} = 20000 \times 1.0 = 20000 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{loss} = P_i + P_{cu, FL} = 200 + 300 = 500 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{FL, upf} = \frac{20000}{20000 + 500} = \frac{20000}{20500} = 0.9756 = 97.56\%$$

**(ii) Efficiency at Half Load, 0.8 PF Lagging:**

$$P_{out, HL} = 0.5 \times 20000 \times 0.8 = 8000 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{cu, HL} = (0.5)^2 \times 300 = 0.25 \times 300 = 75 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{loss, HL} = 200 + 75 = 275 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{HL, 0.8} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 275} = \frac{8000}{8275} = 0.9668 = 96.68\%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to refer OC test parameters to the metering side.** If the OC test is on the LV side and the question asks for equivalent circuit referred to HV, students must multiply $R_0$ and $X_0$ by $K^2$. **(Lose 1-2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Confusing "rating" vs. "test" voltage in SC test.** The SC test is done at 5-10% of rated voltage, but the resulting $P_{sc}$ is the **full-load** copper loss because the test current equals $I_{FL}$. Do NOT scale $P_{sc}$ by the voltage ratio! **(Lose 2-3 marks)**
>
> 3. **Sumpner's test unit confusion.** WŌéü and WŌéé are total losses of **both** transformers. Divide by 2 to get per-transformer loss. **(Lose 1 mark per occurrence)**
>
> 4. **Loss separation sign convention.** The intercept $A$ of the $P_i/f$ vs. $f$ plot gives the hysteresis loss *per Hz*. To get $P_h$ at rated frequency, multiply by $f_r$ ŌĆö do not stop at the intercept value. **(Lose 1-2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Voltage regulation formula ŌĆö unity vs. lagging PF.** The formula changes with power factor. Using $IR/V$ at lagging PF gives wrong (too low) answer. Always use the complete $I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)/V$. **(Lose 2-3 marks)**
>
> 6. **Neglecting the $\sin\phi$ term in regulation.** Many students use $\cos\phi$ only. Always compute both $\cos\phi$ and $\sin\phi$ explicitly. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary ŌĆö Print This for Last-Minute Review**

- ŌśÉ **Polarity Test:** Subtractive polarity ŌćÆ $V_3 = \vert V_1 - V_2 \vert < V_1$ (standard). Additive ŌćÆ $V_3 = V_1 + V_2 > V_1$.
- ŌśÉ **OC Test Side:** Done on **LV side**. Gives $P_i$, $R_0$, $X_0$ (shunt branch).
- ŌśÉ **SC Test Side:** Done on **HV side**. Gives $P_{cu, FL}$, $R_{01}$, $X_{01}$ (series branch).
- ŌśÉ **OC Test Power:** $P_{oc} = V_0 I_0 \cos\phi_0 = P_i$ (iron loss).
- ŌśÉ **SC Test Power:** $P_{sc} = I_{sc}^2 R_{01} = P_{cu, FL}$ (full-load copper loss).
- ŌśÉ **No-load PF:** Always **low and lagging** (typically 0.1 to 0.3), because the magnetizing component $I_\mu \gg I_w$.
- ŌśÉ **Equivalent Impedance:** $Z_{01} = V_{sc}/I_{sc}$; $R_{01} = P_{sc}/I_{sc}^2$; $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$.
- ŌśÉ **Voltage Regulation:** Use the **approximate** formula (most KTU problems): $\%\text{Reg} = [I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)/V] \times 100$.
- ŌśÉ **Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu, FL}}$ where $x$ is the load fraction.
- ŌśÉ **Iron losses are CONSTANT** (independent of load); **Copper losses are VARIABLE** (proportional to load┬▓).
- ŌśÉ **Sumpner's Test:** Two identical transformers, primaries in **parallel**, secondaries in **series-opposition**. WŌéü = 2$P_i$, WŌéé = 2$P_{cu, FL}$. **Regenerative** (energy recirculates).
- ŌśÉ **Loss Separation:** $P_i = P_h + P_e$. Plot $P_i/f$ vs. $f$ ŌåÆ straight line. Intercept $A$ ŌåÆ hysteresis coefficient; slope $B$ ŌåÆ eddy current coefficient.
- ŌśÉ **At rated frequency $f_r$:** $P_h = A \cdot f_r$ and $P_e = B \cdot f_r^2$.
- ŌśÉ **Verification step:** Always check $P_h + P_e = P_i$ at rated $f$.
- ŌśÉ **Turns Ratio:** $K = V_{HV}/V_{LV}$. To refer LV-side parameter to HV: multiply by $K^2$. To refer HV-side to LV: divide by $K^2$.
- ŌśÉ **Copper loss at fraction $x$:** $P_{cu,x} = x^2 \cdot P_{cu, FL}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autotransformer - Saving of Copper and Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET303 - DC Machines & Transformers
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Autotransformer - Saving of Copper and Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Autotransformer:** A transformer in which a **single winding**, common to both the primary and secondary circuits, serves as the entire voltage transformation element. Part of the winding (called the **common winding**) is shared between primary and secondary, while the remainder (called the **series winding**) is connected only in series with the supply. Unlike a two-winding transformer, the autotransformer provides both **conductive (galvanic)** and **inductive (magnetic)** power transfer between its input and output.

The autotransformer is essentially a **two-winding transformer whose windings are connected in series** electrically (in additive or subtractive polarity) so that a part of the winding is common to both primary and secondary.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The autotransformer is treated as a *single-winding, variable-turn-ratio* device. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes the **saving of copper (or conductor material)** derivation and its numerical application ŌĆö this is a frequent 7-mark and 14-mark examination item.

### 1.2 Constructional Features

A single continuous winding on a laminated magnetic core is provided with **taps** at suitable points. The terminals are brought out as follows:

- **Terminals A, B:** Connected across the full winding ŌåÆ these are the **primary terminals** (input).
- **Terminals a, b:** Connected across the tapped portion of the winding ŌåÆ these are the **secondary terminals** (output).

The portion between the common tap and one end is the **common winding** $(N_2)$. The remaining portion is the **series winding** $(N_1 - N_2)$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The "Water Tap" Analogy**
> Imagine a long horizontal pipe carrying water (this represents the **primary winding** with current $I_1$). Now imagine attaching a smaller branch-pipe somewhere along the length of this main pipe (this is the **secondary tapping**). The branch-pipe draws off a portion of the flow (the **secondary current** $I_2$). Crucially, the water that passes through the branch-pipe is *carried* (convected) directly by the main pipe ŌĆö it doesn't all have to "transform" through a magnetic intermediate. The same principle holds in an autotransformer: most of the power flows **directly (conductively)** through the shared portion of the winding, with only a small portion actually undergoing **electromagnetic induction**. This is why the autotransformer is smaller, cheaper, and more efficient than an equivalent two-winding transformer.

**Physical constants / standard metrics (bolded):**
- **Transformation ratio** $K = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ (step-down) or $K = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ (step-up)
- **Fractional copper saving** $= \dfrac{1}{K}$ (where $K$ is the high-side to low-side turns ratio)

### 1.4 Types of Autotransformer

1. **Step-Down Autotransformer** ŌĆö $V_2 < V_1$ (common configuration)
2. **Step-Up Autotransformer** ŌĆö $V_2 > V_1$
3. **Variable Autotransformer (Variac)** ŌĆö A continuously adjustable autotransformer used in laboratories for variable AC supply.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Reasoning

Let the autotransformer be operating as a **step-down** unit with:
- $N_1$ = total primary turns (across input terminals AŌĆōB)
- $N_2$ = secondary turns (across output terminals aŌĆōb)
- $V_1, V_2$ = primary and secondary voltages
- $I_1, I_2$ = primary and secondary currents
- $K = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} > 1$ (step-down ratio)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Voltage relationship (inductive):**
The voltage per turn is constant across the entire winding, so:
$$\frac{V_1}{N_1} = \frac{V_2}{N_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1}{K}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Current relationship by Ampere-turn balance (MMF balance):**
For an ideal autotransformer, the net MMF in the core must be zero (no-load current neglected). The series winding carries $I_1$ and the common winding carries $(I_2 - I_1)$:
$$(N_1 - N_2) \cdot I_1 = N_2 \cdot (I_2 - I_1)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solving for current ratio:**
$$I_2 - I_1 = I_1 \cdot \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_2} = I_1 \cdot (K - 1)$$
$$I_2 = I_1 \cdot K \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1}{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The autotransformer obeys the same volt-ampere balance as a two-winding transformer, but the currents in the two physical sections of the *same* winding are *different*. The series portion sees the full primary current, while the common portion sees only the *difference* current $(I_2 - I_1)$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Identification of winding sections:**

| Winding Section | Number of Turns | Current Carried |
|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Series winding** (between A and a) | $N_1 - N_2$ | $I_1$ |
| **Common winding** (between a and b) | $N_2$ | $I_2 - I_1 = I_1(K-1)$ |

### 2.2 Power Transfer Mechanism (Conductive + Inductive)

The total power delivered to the load is $V_2 I_2$. This power reaches the load via **two simultaneous paths**:

**Path 1 ŌĆö Conductive (Direct) Power Transfer:**
$$P_{\text{conducted}} = V_2 \cdot I_1 = \frac{V_1 I_1}{K} = \frac{S}{K}$$
where $S$ = apparent power rating of the autotransformer.

**Path 2 ŌĆö Inductive (Transformed) Power Transfer:**
$$P_{\text{transformed}} = V_2 \cdot (I_2 - I_1) = V_2 \cdot I_1 (K - 1) = S \cdot \frac{K-1}{K}$$

**Check:** $P_{\text{conducted}} + P_{\text{transformed}} = \dfrac{S}{K} + \dfrac{S(K-1)}{K} = S \;\checkmark$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Engineering Insight:** The fraction $\dfrac{1}{K}$ of the total power is transferred by *direct conduction* (no magnetic action needed). The *transformed* power is only $\dfrac{K-1}{K}$ of the total ŌĆö this is the portion that determines the **core size** of the autotransformer. Hence, an autotransformer has a much smaller core and far less copper than an equivalent-rated two-winding transformer.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains every equation the KTU examiner expects you to reproduce verbatim for a 7-mark or 14-mark question on the autotransformer.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Turns ratio | $K = N_1 / N_2$ | dimensionless; $K > 1$ for step-down |
| 2 | Voltage ratio | $V_2 / V_1 = N_2 / N_1 = 1/K$ | V/V |
| 3 | Current ratio | $I_1 / I_2 = N_2 / N_1 = 1/K$ | A/A |
| 4 | Series-winding current | $I_{\text{series}} = I_1$ | A |
| 5 | Common-winding current | $I_{\text{common}} = I_2 - I_1 = I_1(K-1)$ | A |
| 6 | Rating (apparent power) | $S = V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$ | VA |
| 7 | Conducted power | $P_{\text{cond}} = V_2 I_1 = S/K$ | W |
| 8 | Transformed (induced) power | $P_{\text{ind}} = V_2(I_2 - I_1) = S(K-1)/K$ | W |
| 9 | Weight of Cu in auto-Xmer | $W_a \propto (N_1 - N_2)I_1 + N_2(I_2 - I_1)$ | kg |
| 10 | Weight of Cu in 2-w Xmer | $W_2 \propto N_1 I_1 + N_2 I_2$ | kg |
| 11 | **Saving of copper (fraction)** | $\dfrac{W_2 - W_a}{W_2} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | **Copper saving ratio** | $W_a / W_2 = 1 - 1/K = (K-1)/K$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | EMF per turn | $E / N = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m$ | V/turn |
| 14 | Transformation ratio (form) | $K = V_1 / V_2$ (step-down) | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power distribution networks:** Step-down autotransformers at substations feed 11 kV / 6.6 kV systems with massive copper savings.
- **Induction motor starters:** Used as compensators to apply reduced voltage during starting.
- **Laboratory variacs:** Continuously variable AC supplies (0ŌĆō270 V) for testing.
- **Railway traction:** Step-down autotransformers in 25 kV AC traction systems.
- **Audio matching:** Impedance matching between speakers and amplifiers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Saving of Copper (Standard KTU Proof)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important derivation in Module 4.** KTU examiners have asked this derivation repeatedly (in slightly different forms) for the past decade. Memorize the structure, not just the result.

**Assumptions:** (i) Ideal autotransformer (no losses, no leakage), (ii) Same VA rating as the two-winding transformer it is being compared to, (iii) Same current density and same flux density in both cases (so copper volume ŌłØ Ampere-turns).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the comparison case (two-winding transformer):**

Let an equivalent **two-winding transformer** have:
- Primary: $N_1$ turns, current $I_1$
- Secondary: $N_2$ turns, current $I_2$
- Same VA rating: $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$

The **weight of copper** is proportional to the product of conductor length (ŌłØ number of turns) and conductor cross-section (ŌłØ current at constant current density):

$$W_2 \;\propto\; N_1 I_1 + N_2 I_2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the VA equality:**

From $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$ and $V_1 / V_2 = N_1 / N_2$, we get:
$$N_1 I_1 = N_2 I_2 \quad \text{(since } V/N \text{ is constant per turn)}$$

Therefore:
$$W_2 \;\propto\; N_1 I_1 + N_1 I_1 = 2 \, N_1 I_1 \quad \text{...(Equation A)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define the autotransformer case:**

The autotransformer has:
- **Series winding:** $(N_1 - N_2)$ turns, carrying current $I_1$
- **Common winding:** $N_2$ turns, carrying current $(I_2 - I_1)$

Therefore:
$$W_a \;\propto\; (N_1 - N_2) I_1 + N_2 (I_2 - I_1) \quad \text{...(Equation B)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Simplify the autotransformer expression using $I_2 = K \cdot I_1$:**

Recall $I_2 = I_1 \cdot K = I_1 \cdot \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$, so:
$$I_2 - I_1 = I_1 \left( \frac{N_1}{N_2} - 1 \right) = I_1 \cdot \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_2}$$

Substituting into Equation B:
$$W_a \;\propto\; (N_1 - N_2) I_1 + N_2 \cdot I_1 \cdot \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_2}$$

$$W_a \;\propto\; (N_1 - N_2) I_1 + (N_1 - N_2) I_1 = 2 (N_1 - N_2) I_1 \quad \text{...(Equation C)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the saving of copper:**

$$\text{Saving} = W_2 - W_a \;\propto\; 2 N_1 I_1 - 2(N_1 - N_2) I_1 = 2 N_2 I_1$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Express saving as a fraction of the two-winding weight:**

$$\text{Fractional Saving} = \frac{W_2 - W_a}{W_2} = \frac{2 N_2 I_1}{2 N_1 I_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{W_2 - W_a}{W_2} = \dfrac{1}{K} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\;}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Physical interpretation (the "Why"):**

The autotransformer requires only $\dfrac{1}{K}$ of the copper of an equivalent two-winding transformer. For example, a 10:1 ratio ($K = 10$) gives a **90% saving of copper**; a 2:1 ratio ($K = 2$) gives a **50% saving**. The closer the ratio is to unity, the greater the saving.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Power Transfer Decomposition

Starting from the identity $S = V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Conducted power (through the common winding):**
$$P_{\text{cond}} = V_2 \cdot I_1 = V_2 \cdot \frac{I_2}{K} = \frac{V_2 I_2}{K} = \frac{S}{K}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Induced (transformed) power (through the core):**
$$P_{\text{ind}} = V_2 \cdot (I_2 - I_1) = V_2 \cdot I_1 (K - 1) = \frac{S}{K}(K-1) = S \cdot \frac{K-1}{K}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total power check:**
$$P_{\text{cond}} + P_{\text{ind}} = \frac{S}{K} + S \cdot \frac{K-1}{K} = \frac{S + S(K-1)}{K} = \frac{S \cdot K}{K} = S \;\checkmark$$

### 3.3 Numerical Problem 1 ŌĆö Step-Down Autotransformer (Standard KTU Type)

**Problem Statement:**
A 100 kVA, 2000 V / 200 V, two-winding transformer is to be reconnected as a step-down autotransformer with the **2000 V winding in series** and the **200 V winding acting as the common winding**. Calculate:
1. The rating of the autotransformer.
2. The fraction of power transferred conductively.
3. The fraction of power transferred inductively.
4. The saving of copper as a percentage.

**Solution ŌĆö Step 1: Identify the turns ratio**

$$K = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{2000}{200} = 10$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step 2: Calculate the autotransformer rating**

Primary voltage $V_1 = 2000 \text{ V}$ (across full winding). 
Secondary voltage $V_2 = 2000 - 200 = 1800 \text{ V}$ (across series + common).
Primary current $I_1$ (from 2-winding rating) $= \dfrac{100{,}000}{2000} = 50 \text{ A}$.
Secondary current $I_2 = K \cdot I_1 = 10 \times 50 = 500 \text{ A}$.

$$\text{New rating} = V_2 \cdot I_2 = 1800 \times 500 = 900{,}000 \text{ VA} = \mathbf{900 \text{ kVA}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** A 100 kVA two-winding transformer becomes a **900 kVA** autotransformer ŌĆö a 9├Ś boost in capacity! This is why autotransformers are economically attractive.

**Solution ŌĆö Step 3: Conducted power fraction**

$$P_{\text{cond}} = \frac{S_{\text{auto}}}{K} = \frac{900}{10} = 90 \text{ kVA}$$

$$\text{Conducted fraction} = \frac{90}{900} = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{10} = \mathbf{10\%}$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step 4: Induced power fraction**

$$P_{\text{ind}} = S_{\text{auto}} \cdot \frac{K-1}{K} = 900 \cdot \frac{9}{10} = 810 \text{ kVA}$$

$$\text{Induced fraction} = \frac{810}{900} = \frac{9}{10} = \mathbf{90\%}$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step 5: Saving of copper**

$$\text{Saving} = \frac{W_2 - W_a}{W_2} = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{10} = \mathbf{10\% \; or \; 10:1}$$

### 3.4 Numerical Problem 2 ŌĆö Step-Up Autotransformer

**Problem Statement:**
A single-phase two-winding transformer rated 10 kVA, 500 V / 100 V is connected as an **autotransformer** to step up from 500 V to 600 V. Find: (a) the HV and LV side currents, (b) the rating of the autotransformer, (c) the saving of copper.

**Solution ŌĆö Step 1: Turns ratio of the original transformer**

$$K = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{500}{100} = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1}{5}$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step 2: Autotransformer voltages**

For step-up: $V_1 = 500 \text{ V}$ (LV side), $V_2 = 500 + 100 = 600 \text{ V}$ (HV side).

**Solution ŌĆö Step 3: Currents**

LV side current (full-winding current, which equals original LV current):
$$I_1 = \frac{10{,}000}{500} = 20 \text{ A}$$

HV side current (drawn from the additional series winding):
$$I_2 = \frac{V_1 I_1}{V_2} = \frac{500 \times 20}{600} = \frac{10{,}000}{600} = 16.67 \text{ A}$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step 4: Rating of autotransformer**

$$S_{\text{auto}} = V_2 \cdot I_2 = 600 \times 16.67 = 10{,}000 \text{ VA} = 10 \text{ kVA}$$

Alternatively: $S_{\text{auto}} = S_{\text{2-w}} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right) = 10 \times 1.2 = 12 \text{ kVA}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction by direct calculation:** For step-up autotransformer where the 500 V winding is common and the 100 V winding is the series, the rating becomes $10 \times (1 + 1/5) = 12 \text{ kVA}$. The discrepancy above arose from a labeling choice. Always verify the configuration first.

**Solution ŌĆö Step 5: Saving of copper**

$$\text{Fractional saving} = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{5} = \mathbf{20\%}$$

### 3.5 Numerical Problem 3 ŌĆö Inverse: Finding Original Turns Ratio

**Problem Statement:**
An autotransformer is required to give a 25% saving of copper. Find the transformation ratio $K$ and the ratio of conducted to induced power.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{1}{K} = 0.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = 4$$

The autotransformer has a 4:1 turns ratio. 

$$\frac{P_{\text{cond}}}{P_{\text{ind}}} = \frac{S/K}{S(K-1)/K} = \frac{1}{K-1} = \frac{1}{4-1} = \frac{1}{3}$$

So for every 1 unit of conducted power, there are 3 units of induced power. The total rating is $4 \times$ the rating of the equivalent two-winding transformer.

### 3.6 Numerical Problem 4 ŌĆö Comprehensive KTU-Style Problem

**Problem Statement:**
A 5 kVA, 200 V / 100 V single-phase two-winding transformer is reconnected as a step-down autotransformer with the 200 V winding as the common winding and the 100 V winding added in series (in subtractive polarity so that voltages add). Find: (i) primary and secondary voltages of the autotransformer, (ii) primary and secondary currents, (iii) kVA rating, (iv) kVA transferred conductively and inductively, (v) saving of copper.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify ratings of original 2-winding transformer:**
- HV (200 V) winding: $I_1 = 5000/200 = 25 \text{ A}$
- LV (100 V) winding: $I_2 = 5000/100 = 50 \text{ A}$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Autotransformer voltages (series aiding):**
- $V_1$ (primary) $= 200 + 100 = 300 \text{ V}$ (across full autotransformer)
- $V_2$ (secondary) $= 200 \text{ V}$ (across the common 200 V winding)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Autotransformer currents:**
- Primary current $I_1 = 25 \text{ A}$ (same as HV winding rating)
- Secondary current $I_2 = I_1 \cdot K$ where $K = V_1 / V_2 = 300/200 = 1.5$
- $I_2 = 25 \times 1.5 = 37.5 \text{ A}$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rating of autotransformer:**
$$S_{\text{auto}} = V_1 I_1 = 300 \times 25 = 7500 \text{ VA} = 7.5 \text{ kVA}$$

Equivalently: $S_{\text{auto}} = S_{\text{2-w}} \times K = 5 \times 1.5 = 7.5 \text{ kVA} \;\checkmark$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Power transfer:**
- Conductively: $P_{\text{cond}} = S_{\text{auto}} / K = 7.5/1.5 = 5 \text{ kVA}$
- Inductively: $P_{\text{ind}} = S_{\text{auto}} \times (K-1)/K = 7.5 \times 0.5/1.5 = 2.5 \text{ kVA}$
- Check: $5 + 2.5 = 7.5 \text{ kVA} \;\checkmark$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Saving of copper:**
$$\text{Saving} = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.6667 = \mathbf{66.67\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** A 5 kVA two-winding transformer becomes 7.5 kVA as an autotransformer with 66.67% copper saving. The 2.5 kVA "induced" portion is what would have been the size of the equivalent two-winding transformer.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Quick Comparison Table for Exam

| Quantity | Two-Winding Transformer | Autotransformer (step-down) | Ratio |
|----------|------------------------|----------------------------|-------|
| Windings | 2 electrically isolated | 1 winding with tap | ŌĆö |
| VA rating | $S$ | $K \cdot S$ | $K:1$ |
| Copper weight | $2 N_1 I_1$ | $2(N_1 - N_2) I_1$ | $(K-1)/K$ |
| Core (transformed power) | $S$ | $S(K-1)/K$ | $(K-1)/K$ |
| Efficiency | Lower (more Cu losses) | Higher (less Cu) | ŌĆö |
| Cost | Higher | Lower | ŌĆö |
| Isolation | **Yes (galvanic)** | **No (galvanic)** | ŌĆö |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Autotransformer Architecture & Current Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph coreA["MAGNETIC CORE LAMINATED"]
        direction TB
        n1a["SERIES WINDING TURNS N1 MINUS N2"]:::wind
        n2a["COMMON WINDING TURNS N2"]:::common
    end

    sourceA["PRIMARY SOURCE V1 = 2000 V"]:::src
    loadA["LOAD"]:::load

    termA["TERMINAL A TOP"]:::term
    termB["TERMINAL B BOTTOM"]:::term
    termC["TERMINAL a TAP POINT"]:::term

    sourceA -- "I1 = 50 A" --> termA
    termA -- "FULL WINDING" --> termB
    termB -- "RETURN" --> sourceA

    termA --- n1a
    n1a --- termC
    termC --- n2a
    n2a --- termB

    termA -- "SECONDARY V2 = 1800 V" --> loadA
    loadA -- "I2 = 500 A" --> termB

    I1dir["I1 FLOWS IN SERIES WINDING"]:::note
    Icdir["I2 MINUS I1 FLOWS IN COMMON WINDING"]:::note

    I1dir -.-> n1a
    Icdir -.-> n2a

    classDef wind fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef common fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef src fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,font-style:italic
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Power Transfer Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcB["PRIMARY SOURCE V1 I1"]:::src

    subgraph pathC["AUTOTRANSFORMER WINDING"]
        serB["SERIES WINDING N1 - N2 CARRIES I1"]:::wind
        comB["COMMON WINDING N2 CARRIES I2 - I1"]:::common
    end

    loadB["SECONDARY LOAD V2 I2"]:::load

    path1["CONDUCTIVE PATH Pcond = S / K"]:::condPath
    path2["INDUCTIVE PATH Pind = S K-1 / K"]:::indPath

    srcB -- "POWER IN S" --> serB
    serB --> comB
    comB --> loadB

    comB -. "DIRECT CONDUCTION" .-> path1
    serB -. "THROUGH CORE FLUX" .-> path2
    path1 --> loadB
    path2 --> loadB

    classDef src fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wind fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef common fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef condPath fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef indPath fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Comparison of Power Ratings

```mermaid
graph TB
    tw["TWO WINDING TRANSFORMER S kVA"]:::tw
    aut["AUTOTRANSFORMER SAME PHYSICAL UNIT RATED S times K kVA"]:::auto

    tw -- "RECONNECTED IN SERIES AIDING" --> aut

    cuw["COPPER USED 2N1 times I1 PROPORTIONAL"]:::cu
    cua["COPPER USED 2 times N1 MINUS N2 times I1"]:::cuA
    tw --- cuw
    aut --- cua

    sv["SAVING = 1 DIVIDED BY K"]:::save
    cuw -.-> sv
    cua -.-> sv

    classDef tw fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef auto fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cu fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cuA fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef save fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**
**Define an autotransformer. State any two advantages of an autotransformer over a two-winding transformer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An autotransformer is a transformer having a *single continuous winding* on a laminated core, with appropriate *taps* brought out to provide either step-up or step-down voltage transformation. A portion of the winding (called the **common winding**) is shared between the primary and secondary circuits, and power is transferred by a combination of *conduction* and *induction*.

**Advantages (1.5 marks ŌĆö any two):**
1. **Saving of copper/conductor material** ŌĆö For a transformation ratio $K = N_1/N_2$, the autotransformer uses only $(K-1)/K$ of the copper required by an equivalent two-winding transformer.
2. **Higher efficiency** ŌĆö Lower copper losses due to less material used.
3. **Lower cost and smaller size** ŌĆö Both core and conductor are reduced.
4. **Better voltage regulation** ŌĆö Due to lower leakage reactance and resistance.
5. **Higher kVA rating** ŌĆö A 2-winding transformer of $S$ kVA becomes $KS$ kVA when reconnected as autotransformer.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**
**Derive the expression for the saving of copper in an autotransformer compared to a two-winding transformer of the same rating.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ŌĆö concise version):**

Let $K = N_1/N_2$ be the transformation ratio. For a two-winding transformer:
$$W_2 \propto N_1 I_1 + N_2 I_2 = 2 N_1 I_1$$

For the same rating reconnected as an autotransformer (series winding $= N_1 - N_2$, common winding $= N_2$):
$$W_a \propto (N_1 - N_2) I_1 + N_2 (I_2 - I_1) = 2(N_1 - N_2) I_1$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{W_2 - W_a}{W_2} = \frac{2 N_2 I_1}{2 N_1 I_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1}{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker Note (1 mark):** The derivation must explicitly mention that the **common winding carries $(I_2 - I_1)$** and **series winding carries $I_1$**. Skipping this assumption costs 0.5 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a step-down autotransformer. Define the terms *series winding* and *common winding*. (7 marks)

**(b)** A single-phase two-winding transformer rated at 15 kVA, 3000 V / 300 V is reconnected as a step-down autotransformer with the 3000 V winding acting as the common winding. Calculate: (i) the primary and secondary voltages, (ii) the primary and secondary currents, (iii) the kVA rating, (iv) the power transferred conductively and inductively, and (v) the saving of copper. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

A step-down autotransformer consists of a **single laminated core** carrying one continuous winding with terminals A, B across the full winding and a tap 'a' taken at an intermediate point (see diagram in Section 4).

- **Series winding** = portion between A and a, having $(N_1 - N_2)$ turns.
- **Common winding** = portion between a and B (or b), having $N_2$ turns, and is shared between primary and secondary.

**Working:** When primary voltage $V_1$ is applied across AŌĆōB, a flux $\Phi$ is set up in the core, inducing EMF in the entire winding. The voltage across the tap aŌĆōb (secondary) is:
$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$
The primary current $I_1$ flows through the series winding. The load draws current $I_2$ from the secondary; the difference $(I_2 - I_1)$ flows through the common winding. MMF balance: $(N_1 - N_2) I_1 = N_2 (I_2 - I_1)$.

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

Given: $S_{\text{2-w}} = 15$ kVA, $V_1^{\text{(original)}} = 3000$ V (HV), $V_2^{\text{(original)}} = 300$ V (LV).

Original currents: $I_{\text{HV}} = 15000/3000 = 5$ A; $I_{\text{LV}} = 15000/300 = 50$ A.
$$K = \frac{3000}{300} = 10$$

**Reconnection:** 3000 V winding = common; 300 V winding = series (aiding).

**(i) Voltages:**
- Primary $V_1 = 3000 + 300 = 3300$ V
- Secondary $V_2 = 3000$ V

**(ii) Currents:**
- $I_1 = 5$ A (rated current of common winding = original HV current)
- $I_2 = K \cdot I_1 = 10 \times 5 = 50$ A

**(iii) Rating of autotransformer:**
$$S_{\text{auto}} = V_1 I_1 = 3300 \times 5 = 16{,}500 \text{ VA} = \mathbf{16.5 \text{ kVA}}$$
(Verification: $S_{\text{auto}} = K \cdot S_{\text{2-w}} = 10 \times 15 = 150$ kVA? ŌĆö **NO**, this is incorrect here; the correct multiplier is $(N_1 - N_2)/N_2$ on the series side, not $K$.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction & Clarification:** The autotransformer rating formula $S_{\text{auto}} = K \cdot S_{\text{2-w}}$ applies when the 2-winding transformer's **full kVA is transformed** (i.e., $K = V_{\text{primary, auto}} / V_{\text{secondary, auto}}$). In this problem, the 3000 V common winding carries only $I_1 = 5$ A and the 300 V series winding carries $I_1 = 5$ A. The new rating is therefore $S_{\text{auto}} = 3300 \times 5 = 16.5$ kVA. The saving is then $1/K_{\text{auto}}$ where $K_{\text{auto}} = V_1/V_2 = 3300/3000 = 1.1$.
> $$\text{Saving} = \frac{1}{1.1} = 0.909 = \mathbf{90.9\%}$$
> **KTU students must carefully re-identify $K$ for the reconnected unit ŌĆö it is NOT the original 2-winding ratio.**

**(iv) Power transfer:**
- Conductively: $P_{\text{cond}} = S_{\text{auto}}/K_{\text{auto}} = 16.5/1.1 = 15$ kVA
- Inductively: $P_{\text{ind}} = S_{\text{auto}} \cdot (K_{\text{auto}}-1)/K_{\text{auto}} = 16.5 \times 0.0909 = 1.5$ kVA
- Check: $15 + 1.5 = 16.5$ kVA $\checkmark$

**(v) Saving of copper:**
$$\text{Saving} = \frac{1}{K_{\text{auto}}} = \frac{1}{1.1} = 90.9\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students use $K = 10$ (the *original* turns ratio of the 2-winding transformer) for the saving calculation. The correct $K$ for the **reconnected autotransformer** is $K_{\text{auto}} = 3300/3000 = 1.1$. Using $K = 10$ would give the saving as $1/10 = 10\%$ ŌĆö **wrong**. This single error will cost you **3ŌĆō4 marks** out of 7.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with phasor diagram how a two-winding transformer can be reconnected as a step-up autotransformer. State the expressions for the kVA transferred (i) conductively and (ii) inductively. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 200 kVA, 1100 V / 220 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer is connected as an autotransformer to step down from 1100 V to 880 V. Find: (i) the rating of the autotransformer, (ii) the currents in the windings, (iii) the saving of copper, and (iv) the fraction of power transferred conductively. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

To re-connect as a step-up autotransformer, the two windings (HV and LV) are connected in **series-aiding** (i.e., their EMFs add). The LV winding's 'start' is connected to the HV winding's 'finish'.

- **Phasor diagram:** $V_1$ (input) is across the LV winding; $V_2$ (output) is across the combined series LV + HV windings. Both EMFs are in phase, so $V_2 = V_1 + E_{\text{HV}}$.
- **Conducted kVA:** $S_{\text{cond}} = V_1 \cdot I_2 = S/K$ (where $K = N_1/N_2$ and $S = V_2 I_2$ is the autotransformer rating)
- **Inducted kVA:** $S_{\text{ind}} = V_1 \cdot (I_1 - I_2) = S \cdot (K-1)/K$
- The **core size** is determined by the *induced* kVA, which is less than the total rating ŌĆö hence the smaller core.

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

Given: $S_{\text{2-w}} = 200$ kVA, 1100 V / 220 V.
- $I_{\text{HV}} = 200{,}000/1100 = 181.82$ A
- $I_{\text{LV}} = 200{,}000/220 = 909.09$ A
- Original $K_{\text{2-w}} = 1100/220 = 5$

**Reconnection (step-down 1100 ŌåÆ 880 V):**
For step-down from 1100 V to 880 V, the autotransformer primary is across 1100 V, secondary across 880 V:
$$K_{\text{auto}} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{1100}{880} = 1.25$$

**Configuration:** 1100 V winding = common; an 880-turn portion (= 220 V winding in 5:1 ratio) acts as the additional series winding, but for 1100 V ŌåÆ 880 V *step-down*, the 220 V winding is the *common* and 880 V is the *series-added* ŌĆö let us re-derive carefully.

**Carefully:** Step-down: $V_1 = 1100$ V, $V_2 = 880$ V. The 880 V portion = the series + common. The 220 V winding is the *common*. So the 1100 V portion = (880 V common) + (220 V series). Hence the 220 V winding of the original is the **series winding**, and 880 V of the 1100 V winding is the **common winding**.

- Common-winding current rating = 1100 V winding current = 181.82 A
- Series-winding current rating = 220 V winding current = 909.09 A

**Primary (auto) current** $I_1$ = rated current of full 1100 V winding = **181.82 A**
**Secondary (auto) current** $I_2 = K_{\text{auto}} \cdot I_1 = 1.25 \times 181.82 = 227.27$ A

**Check** ŌĆö series winding current = $I_1 = 181.82$ A, but the series winding is rated 909.09 A. So it's well within limit. The common winding sees $I_2 - I_1 = 227.27 - 181.82 = 45.45$ A, which is well below its 181.82 A rating. $\checkmark$

**(i) Autotransformer rating:**
$$S_{\text{auto}} = V_2 \cdot I_2 = 880 \times 227.27 = 200{,}000 \text{ VA} = \mathbf{200 \text{ kVA}}$$

**(ii) Winding currents:**
- Series winding (220 V portion) carries $I_1 = 181.82$ A
- Common winding (880 V portion) carries $I_2 - I_1 = 45.45$ A

**(iii) Saving of copper:**
$$\text{Saving} = \frac{1}{K_{\text{auto}}} = \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.8 = \mathbf{80\%}$$

**(iv) Conducted power fraction:**
$$P_{\text{cond}} = \frac{S_{\text{auto}}}{K_{\text{auto}}} = \frac{200}{1.25} = 160 \text{ kVA}$$

$$\text{Fraction} = \frac{160}{200} = 0.8 = \mathbf{80\%}$$

(Inducted = 200 ├Ś 0.2 = 40 kVA ŌåÆ 20% of total.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For Question B part (b), students often confuse the polarity of reconnection (series-aiding vs. opposing). If you accidentally connect the 220 V winding in *opposition* instead of aiding, the autotransformer will be a *step-up* from 1100 ŌåÆ 1320 V ŌĆö and all subsequent current, rating, and saving calculations will be wrong. Always explicitly state "**series-aiding connection**" in your answer. The examiner checks this on a typical 14-mark question.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warnings (Common Pitfalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **Five common mistakes that cost KTU students 2ŌĆō4 marks each:**
> 1. **Wrong $K$ used for the saving formula** ŌĆö use the autotransformer's *actual* $V_1/V_2$ ratio, NOT the original 2-winding transformer's ratio.
> 2. **Forgetting the "common winding carries $I_2 - I_1$"** ŌĆö this assumption is worth 1 mark on its own.
> 3. **Sign error in series-aiding vs. opposing** ŌĆö always state the polarity.
> 4. **No unit for the final answer** ŌĆö kVA must be stated explicitly, not just "15000".
> 5. **Skipping the power-transfer decomposition** ŌĆö even if the question doesn't ask, the KTU examiner rewards 2 marks for showing $P_{\text{cond}} + P_{\text{ind}} = S_{\text{auto}}$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 ŌĆö Autotransformer):**
> - Ō£ģ Autotransformer = **single winding with taps**; part of winding is *common* to both primary and secondary.
> - Ō£ģ **Series winding** carries primary current $I_1$; **common winding** carries $(I_2 - I_1)$.
> - Ō£ģ Transformation ratio $K = N_1 / N_2 = V_1 / V_2 = I_2 / I_1$ (for step-down).
> - Ō£ģ **Saving of copper** $= \dfrac{1}{K} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$.
> - Ō£ģ **Rating boost** ŌĆö A 2-winding transformer of $S$ kVA becomes $K \cdot S$ kVA as an autotransformer (for the case where the 2-winding ratio equals the auto ratio).
> - Ō£ģ **Conducted power** $= S/K$ ; **Inducted power** $= S(K-1)/K$. Their sum is $S$.
> - Ō£ģ **No electrical isolation** between primary and secondary ŌĆö major disadvantage.
> - Ō£ģ For a given VA rating, autotransformer has **smaller core, less copper, higher efficiency, lower cost** than a 2-winding transformer.
> - Ō£ģ The **saving is greater when $K$ is close to 1** (e.g., $K = 1.1$ ŌåÆ 91% saving); **saving is small when $K$ is large** (e.g., $K = 100$ ŌåÆ 1% saving).
> - Ō£ģ **Variac** = continuously variable autotransformer used in labs.
> - Ō£ģ **Numerical recipe:** Identify $K_{\text{2-w}}$, then identify $K_{\text{auto}}$ for the *reconnected* unit, then compute: rating = $V_1 I_1$, saving = $1/K_{\text{auto}}$, $P_{\text{cond}} = S_{\text{auto}}/K_{\text{auto}}$, $P_{\text{ind}} = S_{\text{auto}}(K_{\text{auto}}-1)/K_{\text{auto}}$.
> - Ō£ģ **Always verify:** $P_{\text{cond}} + P_{\text{ind}} = S_{\text{auto}}$ in every numerical answer ŌĆö this catches 90% of computational errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3-Phase Transformer - Construction - Different Connections of 3-Phase Transformers (Yy, Dy) and Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET303 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** 3-Phase Transformer - Construction - Different Connections of 3-Phase Transformers (Yy, Dy) and Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **3-Phase Transformer** is a static electromagnetic device that transfers 3-phase alternating electrical energy between two or more circuits through electromagnetic induction, operating on the principle of mutual induction between three sets of primary and secondary windings wound on a common magnetic core. The construction can be either a **single 3-phase unit** (with one core carrying all three phases) or a **bank of three identical single-phase transformers** connected externally to form a 3-phase system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** This topic explicitly covers the **constructional features** of 3-phase transformers and the **standard winding connections** ŌĆö Star-Star (Yy) and Delta-Star (Dy) ŌĆö including their **vector groups**, **voltage/current transformations**, and **numerical analysis** of transformation ratios.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Think of a **3-phase transformer** as **three ordinary single-phase transformers "rolled" into one compact magnetic structure**. Instead of buying three separate transformers, connecting their primaries to a 3-phase supply and their secondaries to a 3-phase load (this is called a *3-phase transformer bank*), engineers can build **a single unit** where the three sets of windings share one magnetic core. 

- A **Star (Y) connection** is like connecting three light bulbs to a common neutral wire ŌĆö the neutral gives a return path, and the voltage across each bulb is the **phase voltage** (V_ph), while the voltage between any two wires is the **line voltage** (V_L = ŌłÜ3 ├Ś V_ph).
- A **Delta (╬ö) connection** is like wiring three bulbs in a closed loop ŌĆö there's no neutral, and the **line voltage equals the phase voltage** (V_L = V_ph), but the **line current is ŌłÜ3 times the phase current** (I_L = ŌłÜ3 ├Ś I_ph).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 3-Phase?** Three-phase systems transmit **57.7% less copper** for the same power compared to single-phase systems. This is why every power station, substation, and large industrial load uses 3-phase transformers.

### 1.3 Construction Overview

A 3-phase transformer consists of:

| Component | Function | Key Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Core** | Provides low-reluctance magnetic path | **CRGO** silicon steel laminations (0.23/0.27 mm) |
| **Primary Windings** | Input 3-phase AC supply | Copper/Aluminum, LV side |
| **Secondary Windings** | Output 3-phase AC load | Copper/Aluminum, LV side |
| **Tank & Oil** | Cooling & insulation | Mineral oil (ONAN/ONAF cooling) |
| **Bushings** | Insulated terminals for HV/LV | Porcelain/Polymeric |
| **Tap Changer** | Voltage ratio adjustment | Off-load (OLTC) or On-load (OLTC) |

The standard **frequency** is **50 Hz** in India, with **3-phase line voltages** standardized at **11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV, 66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of 3-Phase Transformers

#### A. Bank of Three Single-Phase Units
Three independent single-phase transformers are externally connected (in Star or Delta) to form a 3-phase bank. **Advantages:** easy transport for large ratings, any one unit can be replaced if damaged. **Disadvantages:** higher cost, larger footprint, lower efficiency.

#### B. Three-Phase Core-Type Unit
A single core with **three vertical limbs**, each carrying the primary and secondary windings of one phase. The **return flux paths** are through the top and bottom yokes. The **center limb flux** is the algebraic sum of the three phase fluxes, which is **zero in a balanced 3-phase system**. Therefore, the cross-section of the **center limb is reduced by ~13.4%** (factor of 1/ŌłÜ3) compared to the outer limbs in some designs, or kept the same in modern designs for symmetry.

#### C. Three-Phase Shell-Type Unit
Similar to a single-phase shell-type construction but with **five limbs** (three inner + two outer shielding limbs). The outer limbs carry return flux and provide a magnetic shield to reduce external stray fields.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Point:** Core-type 3-phase transformers with **3 limbs** are the most common in distribution networks. The total instantaneous flux in the core is **always zero** under balanced conditions, which is why the return yokes carry only leakage-type flux components.

### 2.2 Standard 3-Phase Transformer Connections

A 3-phase transformer is designated by **two letters** (one for primary, one for secondary):
- **Y** = Star (Wye)
- **D** = Delta (Mesh)
- **Z** = Zig-zag
- **N** = Neutral brought out

The four standard combinations are: **Yy, Yd, Dy, Dd**.

#### A. Star-Star (Yy) Connection
- Both primary and secondary windings are connected in star.
- The **neutral point** can be brought out (denoted as **YN** or **yn**).
- **Line voltage = ŌłÜ3 ├Ś Phase voltage**; **Line current = Phase current**.
- Suitable for **high-voltage transmission** where insulation costs are critical (line insulation is ŌłÜ3 times less stressed than phase insulation).
- **Drawback:** Third-harmonic currents can circulate, causing neutral instability. **Mitigation:** use **tertiary windings**.

#### B. Delta-Star (Dy) Connection
- Primary in **Delta**, secondary in **Star**.
- **Primary side:** V_L1 = V_ph1, I_L1 = ŌłÜ3 ├Ś I_ph1.
- **Secondary side:** V_L2 = ŌłÜ3 ├Ś V_ph2, I_L2 = I_ph2.
- This is the **most common distribution transformer connection** (e.g., 11 kV/433 V distribution transformers in India).
- The Delta primary provides a **closed path for third-harmonic circulating currents**, stabilizing the flux to a near-sinusoidal waveform.
- **Phase displacement:** Secondary line voltage **leads** primary line voltage by **30┬░** ŌåÆ Vector group **Dy1**.
- If winding senses are reversed, the displacement becomes **ŌłÆ30┬░ (lagging)** ŌåÆ Vector group **Dy11**.

#### C. Other Connections (Brief)
- **Star-Delta (Yd):** Used for **step-down applications** near generating stations. Vector group **Yd1** or **Yd11**.
- **Delta-Delta (Dd):** Used when a tertiary delta winding is required. Vector group **Dd0** or **Dd6**.

### 2.3 Vector Group Notation (KTU High-Yield)

The **vector group** describes the **phase displacement** between primary and secondary line voltages using a **clock-face analogy**:
- The **primary line voltage** is taken as the **minute hand at 12 o'clock** (0┬░).
- The **secondary line voltage** is read on the **clock face** to give the displacement.

| Vector Group | Connection | Phase Displacement | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Yy0** | Star-Star | 0┬░ | Autotransformers, special cases |
| **Yy6** | Star-Star | 180┬░ | Specific industrial loads |
| **Dy1** | Delta-Star | +30┬░ (leads) | **Distribution transformers (11 kV/433 V)** |
| **Dy11** | Delta-Star | ŌłÆ30┬░ (lags) | Distribution (alternative) |
| **Yd1** | Star-Delta | +30┬░ (leads) | Step-down at generating stations |
| **Yd11** | Star-Delta | ŌłÆ30┬░ (lags) | Step-down (alternative) |
| **Dd0** | Delta-Delta | 0┬░ | Tertiary winding applications |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** A **Dy11** vector group is the **mandatory standard** for 11 kV/433 V distribution transformers in India as per IS 1180. All parallel-connected transformers MUST have the same vector group; otherwise, severe circulating currents will flow even at no-load.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Star (Y) Connection | Delta (╬ö) Connection |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Line-to-Phase Voltage** | $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ | $V_L = V_{ph}$ |
| 2 | **Line-to-Phase Current** | $I_L = I_{ph}$ | $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$ |
| 3 | **Total 3-╬” Power** | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ |
| 4 | **Neutral Current (Balanced)** | $I_N = 0$ | No neutral |
| 5 | **Neutral Current (Unbalanced)** | $I_N = \sqrt{I_A^2 + I_B^2 + I_C^2 - I_A I_B - I_B I_C - I_C I_A}$ | N/A |
| 6 | **Transformation Ratio (per phase)** | $K = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}}$ | $K = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}}$ |
| 7 | **Line Voltage Ratio (Yy)** | $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}} = K$ | ŌĆö |
| 8 | **Line Voltage Ratio (Dy)** | $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph2}} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{3}}$ | ŌĆö |
| 9 | **Line Current Ratio (Yy)** | $\dfrac{I_{L1}}{I_{L2}} = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | ŌĆö |
| 10 | **Line Current Ratio (Dy)** | $\dfrac{I_{L1}}{I_{L2}} = \dfrac{1}{K} \cdot \sqrt{3}$ | ŌĆö |
| 11 | **kVA per Phase** | $S_{ph} = \dfrac{S_{total}}{3}$ | $S_{ph} = \dfrac{S_{total}}{3}$ |
| 12 | **Phase Voltage (Star)** | $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $V_{ph} = V_L$ |
| 13 | **Phase Current (Delta)** | $I_{ph} = \dfrac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $I_{ph} = \dfrac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Numerical Problems)

### 3.1 Fundamental Derivation: Voltage & Current Relations

#### Derivation 1: Phase-to-Line Voltage in a Star Connection

Consider three windings connected at a common neutral point 'N'. The phase voltages (RMS) are $V_{AN}, V_{BN}, V_{CN}$, displaced by 120┬░ from each other.

The line voltage $V_{AB} = V_{AN} - V_{BN}$.

Using phasor representation: $V_{AN} = V_{ph} \angle 0┬░$, $V_{BN} = V_{ph} \angle -120┬░$, $V_{CN} = V_{ph} \angle -240┬░$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{AB} &= V_{AN} - V_{BN} \\
&= V_{ph}\angle 0┬░ - V_{ph}\angle -120┬░ \\
&= V_{ph}\left[(\cos 0┬░ + j\sin 0┬░) - (\cos(-120┬░) + j\sin(-120┬░))\right] \\
&= V_{ph}\left[(1 + j0) - \left(-\dfrac{1}{2} - j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right] \\
&= V_{ph}\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{2} + j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right] \\
&= V_{ph}\left[\dfrac{3}{2} + j\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right] \\
|V_{AB}| &= V_{ph}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} = V_{ph}\sqrt{\dfrac{9}{4} + \dfrac{3}{4}} = V_{ph}\sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the **fundamental Star relation** is:

$$
V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph} \quad \text{and} \quad I_L = I_{ph}
$$

#### Derivation 2: Line-to-Phase Current in a Delta Connection

In a delta, the **line current equals the phasor difference of two phase currents**. For phase currents $I_{AB}$ and $I_{AC}$ meeting at node A:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_A &= I_{AB} - I_{AC} \\
|I_A| &= \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$ and $V_L = V_{ph}$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 (Yy Connection)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** A 3-phase, 200 kVA, 6600 V / 400 V, 50 Hz, Yy0 transformer has 1200 turns on the primary. Calculate: (a) Number of turns on the secondary, (b) Primary and secondary phase currents, (c) Primary and secondary line currents.

**Given Data:**
- Rating: $S = 200 \text{ kVA}$, $V_{L1} = 6600 \text{ V}$, $V_{L2} = 400 \text{ V}$
- $N_1 = 1200$ turns, Connection: **Yy0** (Star-Star)
- $f = 50$ Hz

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate Primary and Secondary Phase Voltages (Star Connection)**

For a **Star** connection, phase voltage = line voltage / ŌłÜ3.

$$
V_{ph1} = \dfrac{V_{L1}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{ph2} = \dfrac{V_{L2}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.9 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate Number of Secondary Turns**

Transformation ratio: $K = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}}$

$$
N_2 = N_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph2}}{V_{ph1}} = 1200 \cdot \dfrac{230.9}{3810.5} = 1200 \cdot 0.0606 = 72.7 \text{ turns}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_2 \approx 73 \text{ turns}}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate Primary Line Current**

$$
I_{L1} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L1}} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600} = \dfrac{200000}{11431.5} = 17.50 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Calculate Secondary Line Current**

$$
I_{L2} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2}} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 400} = \dfrac{200000}{692.8} = 288.7 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Phase Currents (Star: $I_{ph} = I_L$)**

$$
I_{ph1} = I_{L1} = 17.50 \text{ A}, \quad I_{ph2} = I_{L2} = 288.7 \text{ A}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 (Dy Connection ŌĆö High-Yield KTU Type)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]** A 3-phase transformer has 1000 turns on the primary and 50 turns on the secondary winding. The primary is connected to an 11 kV, 50 Hz supply in Delta, and the secondary is connected in Star. Calculate: (a) Secondary line voltage, (b) Line currents on both sides at full-load, when the transformer supplies a balanced 3-phase load of 500 kVA at 0.8 p.f. lagging.

**Given Data:**
- $N_1 = 1000$ turns, $N_2 = 50$ turns
- $V_{L1} = 11000 \text{ V}$ (Delta primary)
- Connection: **Dy1** (Delta primary, Star secondary)
- Load: $S = 500 \text{ kVA}$, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging

**Step 1 ŌĆö Transformation Ratio**

$$
K = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{1000}{50} = 20
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Primary and Secondary Phase Voltages**

For a **Delta** primary: $V_{ph1} = V_{L1} = 11000 \text{ V}$

For a **Star** secondary, the phase voltage is the secondary phase voltage; the line voltage will be ŌłÜ3 times this.

$$
V_{ph2} = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{K} = \dfrac{11000}{20} = 550 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Secondary Line Voltage**

$$
V_{L2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph2} = \sqrt{3} \times 550 = 952.6 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{L2} \approx 952.6 \text{ V}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Primary Line Current**

Total 3-╬” apparent power $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L1} \cdot I_{L1}$

$$
I_{L1} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L1}} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000} = \dfrac{500000}{19052.6} = 26.24 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Secondary Line Current**

$$
I_{L2} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2}} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 952.6} = \dfrac{500000}{1649.9} = 303.1 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Verification using Line Current Ratio (Dy formula)**

For Dy connection: $I_{L1} = I_{L2} \cdot \dfrac{V_{L2}}{V_{L1}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}/\sqrt{3}}$ ŌĆö Let's verify via per-phase:

For Star secondary: $I_{ph2} = I_{L2} = 303.1$ A
For Delta primary: $I_{ph1} = \dfrac{I_{L1}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{26.24}{\sqrt{3}} = 15.15$ A
Check: $\dfrac{I_{ph1}}{I_{ph2}} = \dfrac{15.15}{303.1} = 0.05 = \dfrac{1}{K} = \dfrac{1}{20}$ Ō£ō **Verified.**

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 3 (Comparison of All Connections)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Style]** A 3-phase transformer has 500 turns on the primary and 100 turns on the secondary. The primary line voltage is 6600 V. Find the secondary line voltage for: (i) Yy, (ii) Yd, (iii) Dy, (iv) Dd connections. Also calculate the line currents if the load draws 200 A per phase in delta.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Transformation Ratio**

$$
K = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{500}{100} = 5
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Primary Phase Voltage**

We consider both cases. For star primary: $V_{ph1} = \dfrac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5$ V
For delta primary: $V_{ph1} = 6600$ V

**Step 3 ŌĆö Secondary Phase Voltage**

$V_{ph2} = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{K}$

**(i) Yy Connection:**
- Primary phase voltage: $V_{ph1} = 6600/\sqrt{3} = 3810.5$ V
- Secondary phase voltage: $V_{ph2} = 3810.5/5 = 762.1$ V
- Secondary line voltage: $V_{L2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot 762.1 = \mathbf{1320 \text{ V}}$

**(ii) Yd Connection:**
- Primary phase voltage: $V_{ph1} = 6600/\sqrt{3} = 3810.5$ V
- Secondary phase voltage: $V_{ph2} = 3810.5/5 = 762.1$ V
- Secondary line voltage: $V_{L2} = V_{ph2} = \mathbf{762.1 \text{ V}}$

**(iii) Dy Connection:**
- Primary phase voltage: $V_{ph1} = 6600$ V
- Secondary phase voltage: $V_{ph2} = 6600/5 = 1320$ V
- Secondary line voltage: $V_{L2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot 1320 = \mathbf{2286.3 \text{ V}}$

**(iv) Dd Connection:**
- Primary phase voltage: $V_{ph1} = 6600$ V
- Secondary phase voltage: $V_{ph2} = 6600/5 = 1320$ V
- Secondary line voltage: $V_{L2} = V_{ph2} = \mathbf{1320 \text{ V}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** For the same turns ratio and primary line voltage, the **Dy connection gives the highest secondary line voltage** because the star secondary benefits from the ŌłÜ3 multiplier.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem 4 (Vector Group and Phase Displacement)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Specific Style]** A 3-phase transformer has a turns ratio of 2:1 (primary to secondary) and is connected in Dy. If the primary phase voltages are $V_{AN} = 100\angle 0┬░$ V, $V_{BN} = 100\angle -120┬░$ V, $V_{CN} = 100\angle -240┬░$ V, find the secondary line voltages and identify the vector group.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Primary Line Voltages (Delta)**

In delta, the line voltage equals the phase voltage. Thus:
$V_{AB} = 100\angle 0┬░$ V, $V_{BC} = 100\angle -120┬░$ V, $V_{CA} = 100\angle -240┬░$ V

**Step 2 ŌĆö Secondary Phase Voltage (Star)**

The secondary phase voltages are:
$V_{an} = \dfrac{V_{AN}}{2} = 50\angle 0┬░$ V (corresponding to primary phase A)
$V_{bn} = \dfrac{V_{BN}}{2} = 50\angle -120┬░$ V
$V_{cn} = \dfrac{V_{CN}}{2} = 50\angle -240┬░$ V

**Step 3 ŌĆö Secondary Line Voltages (Star)**

Using the Star relation with a 30┬░ phase shift (Dy1 vector group):

The secondary line voltage $V_{ab}$ leads the primary line voltage $V_{AB}$ by 30┬░:

$$
V_{ab} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{an} \angle (+30┬░)
$$

$$
V_{ab} = \sqrt{3} \cdot 50 \angle 30┬░ = 86.6\angle 30┬░ \text{ V}
$$

Similarly, $V_{bc} = 86.6\angle -90┬░$ V and $V_{ca} = 86.6\angle -210┬░$ V

**Vector Group: Dy1** (Secondary line voltages lead primary line voltages by 30┬░ ŌåÆ "1 o'clock" position on the phasor clock).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a 3-Phase Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[3-Phase HV Input Supply] --> B[HV Bushings H1 H2 H3]
    B --> C[Primary Windings HV Side]
    C --> D{Magnetic Core CRGO Laminations}
    D --> E[Secondary Windings LV Side]
    E --> F[LV Bushings X1 X2 X3]
    F --> G[3-Phase LV Output Load]
    D --> H[Core Limb Top and Bottom Yokes]
    C --> I[Off-load Tap Changer OLTC]
    I --> J[Voltage Ratio Adjustment]
    J --> C
    E --> K[Neutral Point N brought out for Y connection]
    K --> G
    G --> L[Vector Group Identification Dy1 Yy0 etc]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Connection Comparison Yy vs Dy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PrimarySide
        A1[Yy Connection Star Primary] --> A2[V_L1 = sqrt 3 x V_ph1]
        A2 --> A3[I_L1 = I_ph1]
        B1[Dy Connection Delta Primary] --> B2[V_L1 = V_ph1]
        B2 --> B3[I_L1 = sqrt 3 x I_ph1]
    end
    subgraph SecondarySide
        A3 --> A4[Yy Star Secondary V_L2 = sqrt 3 x V_ph2]
        B3 --> B4[Dy Star Secondary V_L2 = sqrt 3 x V_ph2]
    end
    A4 --> A5[Vector Group Yy0 or Yy6]
    B4 --> B5[Vector Group Dy1 or Dy11]
```

### 4.3 Vector Group Clock Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Yy0 Connection Phase shift 0 degrees] --> P2[Clock Position 12 to 12]
    P3[Yy6 Connection Phase shift 180 degrees] --> P4[Clock Position 12 to 6]
    P5[Dy1 Connection Phase shift 30 degrees lead] --> P6[Clock Position 12 to 1]
    P7[Dy11 Connection Phase shift 30 degrees lag] --> P8[Clock Position 12 to 11]
    P9[Yd1 Connection Phase shift 30 degrees lead] --> P10[Clock Position 12 to 1]
    P11[Yd11 Connection Phase shift 30 degrees lag] --> P12[Clock Position 12 to 11]
    P13[Dd0 Connection Phase shift 0 degrees] --> P14[Clock Position 12 to 12]
```

### 4.4 Constructional Architecture: Core-Type 3-Phase Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[Input 3-Phase Supply] --> T2[HV Bushing Terminal H1]
    T1 --> T3[HV Bushing Terminal H2]
    T1 --> T4[HV Bushing Terminal H3]
    T2 --> T5[Primary Winding on Limb A]
    T3 --> T6[Primary Winding on Limb B]
    T4 --> T7[Primary Winding on Limb C]
    T5 --> T8[Limb A Core CRGO]
    T6 --> T9[Limb B Core CRGO]
    T7 --> T10[Limb C Core CRGO]
    T8 --> T11[Top Yoke Return Path]
    T9 --> T11
    T10 --> T11
    T11 --> T12[Bottom Yoke Return Path]
    T12 --> T8
    T12 --> T9
    T12 --> T10
    T8 --> T13[Secondary Winding on Limb A]
    T9 --> T14[Secondary Winding on Limb B]
    T10 --> T15[Secondary Winding on Limb C]
    T13 --> T16[LV Bushing Terminal X1]
    T14 --> T17[LV Bushing Terminal X2]
    T15 --> T18[LV Bushing Terminal X3]
    T16 --> T19[Output 3-Phase Load]
    T17 --> T19
    T18 --> T19
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** In a balanced 3-phase system, the sum of the three limb fluxes $\Phi_A + \Phi_B + \Phi_C = 0$ at every instant. This means **no flux flows through the center of the yokes in pure core-type designs**, though the yokes must still be sized to handle unbalanced conditions and zero-sequence components.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Set A]**
> **Q1.** List any **three advantages** of a 3-phase transformer over a bank of three single-phase transformers.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Lower cost:** A single 3-phase unit uses less material (one core, one tank, one oil filling) than three separate units, reducing manufacturing and installation cost.
2. **Higher efficiency:** Reduced core material ŌåÆ lower core (iron) losses, single tank ŌåÆ lower cooling surface requirement ŌåÆ better thermal efficiency.
3. **Smaller footprint:** A 3-phase unit occupies less floor space, making it ideal for compact substations and indoor installations.
4. (Bonus) **Simpler interconnections** and reduced transportation weight for ratings up to ~10 MVA.

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
> **Q2.** Why is the **Delta-Star (Dy) connection** preferred for distribution transformers (e.g., 11 kV/433 V) instead of Star-Star (Yy)?

**Model Answer:**
1. **The Delta primary provides a closed circulating path for third-harmonic currents**, preventing them from flowing in the supply lines and stabilizing the magnetic flux to a near-sinusoidal waveform. This avoids the third-harmonic voltage amplification problem in Yy transformers.
2. **The Star secondary provides a neutral** for supplying single-phase loads (e.g., 230 V lighting) in addition to 3-phase loads (e.g., 415 V motors).
3. **The 30┬░ phase shift** allows parallel operation of transformers with different vector groups (e.g., Dy1 and Dy11) only after careful consideration.
4. **Better short-circuit withstand capability** due to the Delta primary distributing fault currents across two windings per phase.

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Module 4, 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a **neat connection diagram**, explain the **Star-Star (Yy) connection** of a 3-phase transformer. Derive the relationships between line and phase values of voltages and currents for this connection. State **two limitations** of this connection.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 3-phase, 500 kVA, 11000 V / 433 V, 50 Hz transformer is connected **Dy1**. The primary has 1500 turns per phase. Calculate:
- (i) Turns on the secondary winding per phase
- (ii) Primary and secondary line currents at full load
- (iii) Primary and secondary phase currents at full load

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Connection Diagram Description:**

In a **Yy connection**, both the primary and secondary windings are connected in star. One end of each of the three primary windings is joined at the **primary neutral N1**, and similarly, the secondary windings are joined at **secondary neutral N2**. The free ends of the primary windings are connected to line terminals **R, Y, B**, and the free ends of the secondary windings are connected to line terminals **r, y, b**. The primary is supplied with 3-phase voltages $V_{RN}, V_{YN}, V_{BN}$ and the secondary delivers $V_{rn}, V_{yn}, V_{bn}$.

**Voltage and Current Relationships:**

For both primary and secondary, in star connection:

$$
V_{L1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph1}, \quad I_{L1} = I_{ph1}
$$

$$
V_{L2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph2}, \quad I_{L2} = I_{ph2}
$$

The transformation ratio per phase is $K = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$.

**Line-to-line ratio (Yy):**

$$
\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph1}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph2}} = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}} = K
$$

**Limitations of Yy Connection:**
1. **Third-harmonic and triplen currents** cannot circulate in the absence of a neutral, causing severe neutral displacement and voltage distortion. Mitigation requires a tertiary delta winding.
2. **If the neutral is brought out, a large fault current** can flow during a single line-to-ground fault on the secondary, which may exceed the rupturing capacity of the protective devices on the primary side.
3. **Difficulty in handling large unbalanced loads** due to the neutral point shifting under unbalanced conditions.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $S = 500$ kVA, $V_{L1} = 11000$ V (Delta primary), $V_{L2} = 433$ V (Star secondary), $N_1 = 1500$ turns, **Dy1** connection.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Primary and Secondary Phase Voltages:**

For Delta primary: $V_{ph1} = V_{L1} = 11000$ V

For Star secondary: $V_{ph2} = \dfrac{V_{L2}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{433}{\sqrt{3}} = 250$ V

**Step 2 ŌĆö Turns on Secondary Winding:**

$$
K = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}} = \dfrac{11000}{250} = 44
$$

$$
N_2 = \dfrac{N_1}{K} = \dfrac{1500}{44} = 34.1 \approx 34 \text{ turns}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Line Currents:**

$$
I_{L1} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L1}} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000} = \dfrac{500000}{19052.6} = 26.24 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{L2} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2}} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 433} = \dfrac{500000}{749.91} = 666.7 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Phase Currents:**

For Delta primary: $I_{ph1} = \dfrac{I_{L1}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{26.24}{\sqrt{3}} = 15.15$ A

For Star secondary: $I_{ph2} = I_{L2} = 666.7$ A

| Parameter | Primary | Secondary |
|---|---|---|
| Phase Voltage | 11000 V | 250 V |
| Line Voltage | 11000 V | 433 V |
| Phase Current | 15.15 A | 666.7 A |
| Line Current | 26.24 A | 666.7 A |
| Turns | 1500 | 34 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat phasor diagram, explain the **vector group Dy11** of a 3-phase transformer. Discuss how it differs from **Dy1** and state **two applications** of each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 3-phase transformer is rated at **1000 kVA, 6600 V / 415 V, 50 Hz, Dy11**. The LV side (secondary) has 30 turns per phase. Find:
- (i) HV side turns per phase
- (ii) HV side line current
- (iii) LV side phase current
- (iv) Transformation ratio

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Vector Group Dy11:**

In a **Dy11** connection, the **delta-connected primary** has line voltages that lag the corresponding star-connected **secondary** line voltages by **30┬░** when traced in the standard sequence A-B-C. Equivalently, the secondary line voltage **lags the primary line voltage by 30┬░**. On a clock-face analogy, if the primary line voltage is at the **12 o'clock** position, the secondary is at the **11 o'clock** position (hence "Dy11").

**Difference Between Dy1 and Dy11:**

| Feature | Dy1 | Dy11 |
|---|---|---|
| Phase displacement | Secondary **leads** primary by 30┬░ | Secondary **lags** primary by 30┬░ |
| Clock position | 1 o'clock | 11 o'clock |
| Polarity convention | Subtractive | Additive (winding sense reversed) |
| Direction of phase sequence | If primary is A-B-C, secondary is a-b-c | If primary is A-B-C, secondary is a-c-b |

**Applications of Dy1:** Commonly used in **solar inverter step-up transformers** and certain **European distribution networks**.

**Applications of Dy11:** **Standard vector group for Indian 11 kV/433 V distribution transformers** as per IS 1180. Also used in **mining and heavy industrial drives** where the lagging power factor is naturally improved by the ŌłÆ30┬░ shift.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $S = 1000$ kVA, $V_{L1} = 6600$ V (Delta primary), $V_{L2} = 415$ V (Star secondary), $N_2 = 30$ turns per phase.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find $N_1$ (HV turns per phase):**

For Star secondary: $V_{ph2} = \dfrac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6$ V

For Delta primary: $V_{ph1} = 6600$ V

$$
K = \dfrac{V_{ph1}}{V_{ph2}} = \dfrac{6600}{239.6} = 27.55
$$

$$
N_1 = K \cdot N_2 = 27.55 \times 30 = 826.5 \approx 827 \text{ turns}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö HV Side Line Current:**

$$
I_{L1} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L1}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600} = \dfrac{10^6}{11431.5} = 87.48 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö LV Side Phase Current:**

For Star secondary: $I_{ph2} = I_{L2} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L2}} = \dfrac{10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = \dfrac{10^6}{718.8} = 1391.2$ A

**Step 4 ŌĆö Transformation Ratio:**

Per phase: $K = N_1/N_2 = 27.55$ (or stated as **27.55:1**)

Line-to-line ratio for Dy: $\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{6600}{415} = 15.9$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Loss of Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing line and phase quantities** ŌĆö A 3-mark penalty is common. Always write $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$ explicitly for Star and $V_{ph} = V_L$ for Delta.
> 2. **Forgetting the ŌłÜ3 in Dy/Dd calculations** ŌĆö When the primary is Delta and secondary is Star, students often use $V_{L1}/V_{L2}$ directly as the turns ratio, which is **WRONG**. The correct turns ratio is $K = V_{ph1}/V_{ph2} = (V_{L1})/(V_{L2}/\sqrt{3}) = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L1}/V_{L2}$.
> 3. **Not specifying the vector group** ŌĆö In numerical problems involving Dy, always mention the displacement (Dy1 = +30┬░, Dy11 = ŌłÆ30┬░).
> 4. **Units inconsistency** ŌĆö Use **V** for voltage, **A** for current, and **kVA/MVA** for rating, **never** mix them.
> 5. **Forgetting to calculate phase currents** in Delta-connected sides ŌĆö Both line and phase currents must be reported for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **3-Phase Transformer Definition:** Static electromagnetic device with three sets of windings on a common core, transferring 3-phase AC power. Either a single 3-phase unit or a bank of three single-phase units.
- **Construction Types:** Core-type (3 limbs) is most common. Shell-type (5 limbs) is used for very high ratings. Bank of 3 single-phase units is used for very high voltages or transport-constrained installations.
- **Magnetic Core:** CRGO silicon steel laminations (0.23ŌĆō0.27 mm). **Total flux in core = 0** under balanced conditions.
- **Four Standard Connections:** Yy, Yd, Dy, Dd. Plus Zig-zag (Zy, Zd) for special rectifier applications.
- **Star Connection Formulas:** $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$, $I_L = I_{ph}$. **Neutral** can be brought out.
- **Delta Connection Formulas:** $V_L = V_{ph}$, $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$. **No neutral**; provides closed path for third-harmonic currents.
- **Yy Application:** High-voltage transmission where neutral is not critical. Needs tertiary delta winding to suppress harmonics.
- **Dy Application:** **Most common for distribution transformers (11 kV / 433 V in India).** Vector group: **Dy11** (standard as per IS 1180).
- **Vector Groups:** Displacement is **0┬░ or 180┬░** for Yy/Dd, and **┬▒30┬░** for Dy/Yd. Clock positions: 0, 1, 6, 11.
- **Transformation Ratio (Per Phase):** $K = N_1/N_2 = V_{ph1}/V_{ph2}$. **Always use phase values, not line values.**
- **Line-to-Line Voltage Ratios:**
   - Yy: $V_{L1}/V_{L2} = K$
   - Yd: $V_{L1}/V_{L2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot K$
   - Dy: $V_{L1}/V_{L2} = K/\sqrt{3}$
   - Dd: $V_{L1}/V_{L2} = K$
- **Line Current Ratios:**
   - Yy: $I_{L1}/I_{L2} = 1/K$
   - Dy: $I_{L1}/I_{L2} = \sqrt{3}/K$
   - Yd: $I_{L1}/I_{L2} = 1/(\sqrt{3} \cdot K)$
   - Dd: $I_{L1}/I_{L2} = 1/K$
- **3-Phase Power:** $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ (constant for all connection types at given load).
- **KVA per Phase:** $S_{ph} = S_{total}/3$.
- **Parallel Operation Rule:** All transformers in parallel must have the **same vector group, voltage ratio, percentage impedance, and phase sequence**, otherwise circulating currents will flow.
- **Standard Indian Distribution Transformer:** 11 kV / 433 V, 50 Hz, **Dy11**, ONAN cooling, Class A insulation, IS 1180 compliant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference Between Power Transformer and Distribution Transformer - All-Day Efficiency and Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET303 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Difference Between Power Transformer and Distribution Transformer - All-Day Efficiency and Numerical Problems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Power Transformer vs Distribution Transformer ŌĆō Formal Definitions ### Power Transformer (High-Power / Network Transformer) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > A *power transformer* is a large-rated transformer used in **transmission networks** (typically rated **above 200 kVA and above 33 kV**), installed at generating stations and primary substations. It operates at **near-full load for the entire 24 hours** with very high efficiency (98%ŌĆō99.5%) and is designed for **maximum efficiency at full load**. ### Distribution Transformer (Pole-Mounted / Consumer-End Transformer) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > A *distribution transformer* is a small-to-medium rated transformer used in **distribution networks** (typically rated **up to 200 kVA and below 11 kV / 33 kV**), installed near or at consumer premises. It is **rarely fully loaded** and operates on a **varying load cycle throughout the day**, so it is designed for **maximum all-day efficiency**, not maximum full-load efficiency. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆō Plain English Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy (KSEB Context):** > > **Power Transformer = "Highway Tollbooth"** ­¤Üø > Imagine a huge tollbooth on a national highway. It processes thousands of vehicles *continuously, day and night*. It is huge, efficient, always running at near-capacity, and rarely idle. Its job is to transfer bulk energy from one form to another without worrying about *when* the traffic peaks ŌĆö it is *always* busy. > > **Distribution Transformer = "Neighbourhood Ration Shop"** ­¤Åś’ĖÅ > Imagine a small ration shop in your colony. Customers arrive in **morning rush (6ŌĆō9 AM)**, **evening peak (6ŌĆō10 PM)**, and are almost **zero at 2 AM at night**. The shop is open 24 hours but is busy only in short bursts. The shopkeeper does not care about "instant efficiency" ŌĆö he cares about **how much total grain he sold in the whole day** versus **how much rent, electricity, and spoilage he paid for 24 hours**. > > This is exactly the difference between **conventional (instantaneous) efficiency** and **all-day efficiency**. --- ## 1.3 Tabular Summary of Definitions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU 2024 High-Yield Metrics to Remember:** > - **Power Transformer Efficiency:** **98% ŌĆō 99.5%** > - **Distribution Transformer Efficiency:** **60% ŌĆō 90%** (at any instant) > - **All-Day Efficiency** is the key metric for distribution transformers. > - **Iron Loss (P_i)** = constant 24 hours. > - **Copper Loss (P_cu)** = depends on load and its duration. --- ## 1.4 Why Do We Need "All-Day Efficiency"? Conventional efficiency $$\eta_{conv} = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Input Power}} = \frac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi + P_i + P_{cu}}$$ This is a **snapshot** ŌĆö evaluated at one instant. For a distribution transformer, the load changes every hour, so a single snapshot is meaningless. We need a metric that captures the *full day's behaviour*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **All-Day Efficiency is to a Distribution Transformer what Fuel Economy (kmpl) is to a Car.** > A car's *instantaneous* mileage (instant efficiency) varies every second, but what matters to the owner is **how many km the car travelled in total per litre of fuel over a day** (all-day efficiency). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Comparison ŌĆō Power vs Distribution Transformer | Parameter | Power Transformer | Distribution Transformer | |---|---|---| | **Location** | Generating station, Primary EHV substation | Distribution substation, near consumers | | **Voltage Rating** | High (**33 kV / 110 kV / 220 kV / 400 kV**) | Low (**11 kV / 6.6 kV / 415 V**) | | **Power Rating** | Large (**> 2.5 MVA typically**) | Small to Medium (**Ōēż 200 kVA ŌĆō 2.5 MVA**) | | **Loading Pattern** | Nearly constant (close to full load 24 h) | Highly variable, peak only few hours | | **Operating Cycle** | **Continuous** | **Intermittent / Cyclic** | | **Efficiency Concern** | **Conventional Efficiency** (max at full load) | **All-Day Efficiency** (max at average load) | | **Typical ╬Ę (conventional)** | **98% ŌĆō 99.5%** | **60% ŌĆō 90%** | | **Flux Density (B_m)** | **High (1.5 ŌĆō 1.7 T)** to reduce size & cost | **Low (1.0 ŌĆō 1.4 T)** to reduce iron losses (since ON 24 h) | | **Iron Loss (P_i)** | Not critical (operates at full load) | **Very critical** (iron core is energized 24 h) | | **Copper Loss (P_cu)** | Critical at full load | Less critical (low loading factor) | | **Design Aim** | Minimize **total losses at full load** | Minimize **iron loss** (since P_i ├Ś 24 h dominates) | | **Tap Changer** | Off-load / On-load tap changer | Usually off-load tap changer | | **Cooling** | OFWF / ONAN / ONAF (forced) | ONAN (natural cooling) | | **Cost per kVA** | Lower at higher ratings | Higher (customized for low load) | | **Example KSEB** | 100 MVA, 220/110 kV at Madakkathara | 250 kVA, 11 kV / 415 V pole-mounted | --- ## 2.2 Why Distribution Transformers Need Different Design Logic > [!IMPORTANT] > The fundamental engineering reason: > - In a **power transformer**, the energy supplied per day Ōēł (Full Load) ├Ś 24 h. Copper losses dominate. > - In a **distribution transformer**, the energy supplied per day Ōēł (Average Load) ├Ś 24 h, but **iron losses occur for the full 24 hours**, while copper losses occur only when load is connected. So the *energy wasted in iron* over 24 hours = **P_i ├Ś 24 kWh**, which can be huge compared to copper loss energy. Hence iron loss must be minimized by **reducing flux density B_m**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆō Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU Board-exam mandatory.
